Betting here is thin — Claude called my flop c-bet and will rarely fold hands that now beat me; I don’t want to build a pot when I’m almost always behind and can be check-raised by trips/two-pair. I check for pot control, to induce bluffs, and to keep the river decision manageable. Plan: fold to substantial turn aggression (check-raise/large bet), call small bets only to see the river because of the heart draw, and re-evaluate on the river.

My draw completely missed - I have just 8-high against a strong aggressive opponent who raised preflop and on the flop. With no showdown value and a critically short stack (5,300 chips), checking is correct. This allows me to either fold if she bets (losing minimum) or reach showdown if she checks back. Betting here would be a desperate bluff against an opponent who has shown consistent strength and has a dominant stack, risking additional chips in a hopeless situation. Preserving my chip stack for better opportunities is essential in a 150-hand grind.

I have K♣T♥ on A-6-2-A-Q. I c-bet the flop and Claude called, then both checked the turn after the board paired A — his calling range from the SB (limp/call) is wide but is now heavily weighted to Ax, medium pairs and some 6x/2x combos. On the river I have no pair and very little showdown value; betting here would be a thin bluff because many of the hands that called the flop (and checked the turn) will not fold to a river bet (Ax, two-pair/trips, and many medium pairs). There’s also the risk of a check-raise from a value hand. With only K-high and a small pot (1,200) relative to my stack, the lowest-variance +EV line is to check down and preserve stack rather than fire a low-equity bluff. I will check and fold to meaningful river aggression; if Claude mucks I win the pot, if he shows a weak hand I save chips for better spots.

I am UTG with Jack-Four offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. In a 4-handed game, this hand does not meet the requirements for an opening range, especially given the aggressive play seen from Minni and Elon in previous hands. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I'm on the button with Ad7h, a decent holding in late position. The action is favorable - Flash has folded, and I only need to call 100 to compete for a 250-chip pot (including my call). This represents 2.5:1 odds. A7o is a standard button play against the small blind and big blind, particularly when I can see a flop with reasonable position. My stack of 5300 is moderate, so I should be playing solid hands from late position. Calling here allows me to see the flop and play post-flop poker where my button position gives me a significant advantage. The hand has good potential with the ace and decent kicker value.

Call 100. 6♣9♣ is a speculative, suited connector with decent playability but it's a marginal hand to be building a pot with from the small blind. Calling the extra 50 into a 250 pot is cheap (~6:1 pot odds, break-even equity ~14%), so I’ll see a flop rather than raising and getting out-of-position vs both opponents or inviting a 3‑bet from Elon. With deep stacks there are implied odds to hit straights/flushes, but postflop I will play cautiously: fold to heavy aggression when missed, proceed aggressively with made hands or strong draws, and try to keep the pot controlled otherwise.

KQo is a premium hand in the big blind facing two limpers (Minni SB and Claude button). Limpers typically have wide, weak ranges. Raising isolates against their weak holdings, builds the pot for value, and leverages KQo's strong playability and domination potential post-flop. Checking risks a multiway pot where we're often dominated by better kings or suited connectors. Standard isolation raise size around 5bb (500 total) given the pot at 300 and multiway limpers, larger than min-raise to fold out junk while getting calls from worse.

Calling 400 with Ad7h from the button. I'm getting 2.75:1 pot odds with position, which is reasonable for a playable hand. Elon's aggression is likely wide-ranging from the BB, and my button position gives me a significant postflop advantage. I can evaluate the flop and play based on texture. Calling preserves my stack flexibility and lets me use my superior position to win the pot or extract value.

Call to a total of 500. 9♣6♣ is a speculative, suited connector with good implied odds and playability — and with both Elon raising and Claude calling (multiway) the price to see a flop is cheap relative to the stacks. The pot is 1,100 and calling the extra 400 requires roughly 26.7% equity (400/(1,100+400)), which is reasonable for a hand that can make straights and flushes. I’m out of position so I won’t 3‑bet with this holding; a raise would commit too much with a marginal hand and invite OOP postflop spots. Plan postflop: fold to heavy continuation-bet aggression on dry boards, proceed aggressively if I hit a strong made hand or a strong draw, and use implied odds to extract value when I flop well.

Check. 9♣6♣ completely missed this flop (7♣-2♠-4♥) — I have no pair and only a backdoor club/runner-runner straight, which is too weak to lead or build a pot out of position in a three-way hand. I wasn’t the preflop raiser so I lack continuation-bet credibility; checking lets me control the pot and see a turn card for cheap. Plan: fold to meaningful aggression from either opponent, only continue if I pick up a club, a pair, or get offered very good pot odds on a tiny bet.

Preflop isolation raise with KQo got called by two limpers (Claude BTN, Minni SB), creating a 1500 pot. Flop 7c2s4h is very dry (rainbow, disconnected low cards, no obvious draws beyond gutshots). My hand completely misses but has ace-high equity and blocks broadway. As preflop raiser OOP, checked to by first limper (Minni), c-betting maintains initiative, exploits wide limper ranges that miss this board (Ax suited/weak pairs/low suited connectors), and has high fold equity. Checking risks free cards to draws/improvers and multiway pot OOP where we can be dominated. Half-pot sizing (750) balances value from worse (worse Ax, missed draws) vs protection/foldout, leaving room for turn play. Effective stacks support aggression; history shows opponents fold to postflop pressure.

Calling 750 with Ad7h on 7c 2s 4h. I have top pair with an excellent kicker and am getting 4:1 pot odds on a dry flop. My position and hand strength are both strong here, and calling allows me to maintain flexibility while gathering information. The hand is likely ahead of Elon's wide-ranging aggression, and Minni's check suggests weakness. I retain positional advantage on future streets while getting paid favorable odds.

Board is 7♣-2♠-4♥ and I hold 9♣6♣ — no pair and only runner‑runner possibilities (a two‑card club run for a flush ≈4.2% and similar tiny chance to complete a straight). Elon’s flop bet 750 into a 3,000 pot means I need ~20% equity to justify a call (750/(3000+750)=20%). With Claude calling Elon, this is a multiway pot and their ranges are likely strong enough that my equity is well below the required 20%. I’m out of position and previously planned to fold to meaningful aggression on this dry board. Preserve the stack and fold rather than chasing a very unlikely runner‑runner draw.

Turn pairs the deuce with a heart, bringing a flush draw to the board (now two hearts: 4h, 2h). My KQo remains ace-high with no pair, draw, or improvement, but retains some blockers to broadway and backdoor potential. Preflop isolation and flop c-bet got one caller from button limper (Claude), building a 3000 pot heads-up OOP. First to act on turn, checking is standard: no value to bet (no made hand), limited bluff equity OOP against a flop-calling range (likely 7x, 4x, overpairs 88+, Ax high, sets) that connects decently with the paired low board. Betting risks getting called by better and folding out worse Ax; checking controls pot, invites bluffs/value bets I can hero-call selectively or check behind for showdown. Effective stacks (mine 7900, Claude 4050) allow flexibility post-turn; history shows Claude passive postflop in spots (calls but doesn't always bet). Pot-committed aggression unnecessary here; live to river.

I have top pair with an ace kicker (A7) on a paired board (7c 2s 4h 2h). Elon checked on the turn after raising the flop, suggesting either a medium-strength hand or a trap. Given my strong hand and position, I should bet for value. A 1500 chip bet is roughly 50% of the pot (3000), which applies reasonable pressure while extracting value from Elon's likely range of medium pairs or weaker ace holdings. This sizing preserves stack flexibility for the river while capitalizing on my strong hand and the initiative Elon's check gave me.

Facing a 1500 bet into a 4500 pot on a turn that pairs the deuce and adds a heart flush draw possibility. KQo is king-high with no pair, draw, or improvement, blocking some broadways but minimal showdown value against Claude's range (flop caller from BTN limp: likely 77/44/22, 88+, Ax, sets, or strong draws like flush/straight draws that hit). Preflop limp-call raise-call c-bet suggests strength that connects with this board; his turn lead after my check represents value or protected semi-bluff, but calling range crushes king-high. Pot odds ~3.25:1 require ~23% equity, but realistic equity <20% vs polarized range (beats pure bluffs/air rarely in his spot). Raising as bluff OOP commits too much (min-raise to 3000 leaves awkward stacks, his 2550 commits him, low fold equity vs sticky history); preserves 7900 stack for better spots. Standard disciplined fold exploits positionally aware play long-term.

AQ is a premium hand in the top tier of starting hands. Playing from UTG, I should open with strong hands to build the pot and control action. A standard 3x raise to 300 (3BB) is appropriate here. This will likely narrow the field and allow me to play a strong hand from position advantage, while also building value. The hand has excellent potential both preflop and postflop against a wide range of opponent holdings.

4♠8♠ is too weak to continue here. Claude opened to 300 from UTG in a 4-max game, so his open range is relatively strong; calling 300 into a 450 pot requires ~40% equity to break even, which 4-8s does not have versus an UTG open. If I call the blinds (Elon SB, Flash BB) still act and Elon has shown aggression in previous hands, increasing the chance of a squeeze or an ugly multiway pot. 3-betting as a bluff with 4♠8♠ is also a poor play — it’s too weak to get proper equity if called and will be vulnerable to 4-bets. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot on the button with a stronger or more playable holding.

76o is a weak offsuit gapped hand from the small blind, out of position against an UTG raise from Claude, who has shown strength in early position raises in prior hands (e.g., Hand 16). Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (250 to call into 700 post-call pot), insufficient for this speculative hand multi-way potentially with BB. Folding preserves stack early in the session (Hand 20/150) to wait for better spots.

Claude's 3x raise from UTG in a 4-handed game (effectively the Cutoff position) indicates a relatively strong range. Ten-Nine offsuit (T9o) is a marginal hand that struggles to realize its equity when out of position, especially against a standard 3x open. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, the lack of suitedness and the positional disadvantage make this a fold in a disciplined strategy.

I’m UTG with 8♠6♥ — a weak, non-connected offsuit hand with poor playability out of position. In a 4-handed game UTG/opening range should be much stronger; calling 100 to see a multiway flop leaves me OOP vs the button and the blinds and is prone to being dominated or having reverse implied odds. Min-raising to 200 to try and steal is suboptimal with this holding because I don’t want to face 3-bets or play big pots OOP with such a hand. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

J3o is a complete trash hand with no playability, straight potential, or suited value. On the button, it has some positional advantage, but folding preflop against blinds is standard to avoid spewing chips with a hand that rarely makes strong hands postflop. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 are not compelling for this equity. Opponents' tendencies don't change this; preserve stack for better spots over 150 hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s 3s. Minni and Elon have folded, leaving me and Claude (Big Blind) in the hand. While 23s is suited, it is one of the weakest possible hands in terms of high-card value and straight potential. Playing this hand out of position (OOP) against Claude is generally a losing proposition long-term. Folding is the safest play here to avoid committing more chips with a marginal hand.

Pocket kings (KK) is a premium hand from UTG in a 4-handed game. Standard strategy is to raise to isolate and build the pot while thinning the field. Opponents' histories show Minni is aggressive but folds to strong lines sometimes, Claude calls loosely but can fold postflop, Flash folds frequently. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate sizing given the 100 BB and pot of 150, committing 300 total. Larger raise risks bloating pot OOP with AA possible but low frequency; this size allows postflop flexibility with deep stacks.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces (Ac As), the strongest possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Elon has raised to 300 from UTG. Given Elon's high activity level and the potential for the blinds (Claude and Minni) to call or re-raise, I must 3-bet for value and to build a pot immediately. A standard 3-bet size here is approximately 3x the initial raise. I will raise to 900 to maximize value while keeping my range looking strong but balanced.

Fold T8o out of position facing a 3-bet. The hand lacks sufficient equity against Flash's strong 3-bet range, and being out of position with a marginal hand is unfavorable. With a 7200 chip stack, I should preserve capital for premium situations where I have stronger holdings.

I’m in the big blind with 9♣7♣ facing an UTG open to 300 and a button 3-bet to 900. The pot is 1,350 and I must call 800 (need ~37% equity). 9♣7♣ is a good speculative hand heads-up, but against two continuing ranges (UTG opener plus a button 3-bet) my multiway equity is well below that threshold, and I’ll be out of position postflop. A squeeze to 1,500 is technically an option, but 9♣7♣ lacks the blockers and showdown value to make a profitable squeeze here — I’ll frequently get called or 4-bet by players who have been aggressive and deep-stacked. Preserving a healthy stack and waiting for a better spot is higher EV than calling or bluff-raising in this spot, so I fold.

Pocket kings remain extremely strong facing a button 3-bet after my UTG open. Button ranges for 3-betting UTG opens are polarized: premium hands like AA, or bluffs/lights like Axs, suited connectors, given Flash's folding tendencies in history (folds most preflop spots). Folding KK is -EV disaster; calling OOP risks playing a deepstack postflop where we are crushed by AA and vulnerable to domination by AK/sets. 4-betting isolates, builds pot for value from worse (QQ, AK, bluffs), denies equity to calling range, and applies max pressure on a frequent folder like Flash. Sizing to 2250 (2.5x the 3-bet) is standard for deepstack 4-bet vs button: large enough for fold equity, but allows a 5-bet shove from AA (~3.3x pot post-4bet). Shoving 7550 overkill induces hero calls from worse and commits us OOP if called lighter. Deep stacks and history favor aggressive line.

I have Pocket Aces (Ac As), the best possible pre-flop hand. Elon has 4-bet to 2250 after my initial 3-bet. Given Elon's high frequency of aggression and the current pot size, a 5-bet shove is the most profitable play. Since Elon's total stack is 5600, I will raise to 5600 to put him all-in and maximize the value of my hand.

Pocket Kings remain one of the best hands in poker, even facing a button 5-bet after my UTG open and 4-bet. Flash's 5-bet range is extremely strong and polarized: primarily AA, possibly KK/QQ/AK, with minimal bluffs given his history of frequent preflop folds (folded in nearly every hand shown except one open where he c-bet and won uncontested). However, folding KK is massively -EV as it forfeits all equity against a calling range where KK has ~18% vs AA, ~50% vs QQ, ~65% vs AK, and dominates bluffs/lights. Calling 3350 OOP leaves only 2250 behind into an 11,350 pot, effectively pot-committed on any flop with poor playability and reverse implied odds if behind. All-in raise to 7850 (shoving my remaining 5600) is optimal: builds maximum value pot (~15,850 if called), denies equity to Flash's worse hands (QQ, AK, any air), applies fold equity against bluffs/lighter 5-bets (Flash's nitty image suggests some but small), and simplifies decision tree. Post-call all-in preflop is a pure coinflip or better against non-AA. History supports aggression as Flash hasn't shown multi-street bluffs. Long-term EV maximized by jamming premiums here.

I hold Pocket Aces (Ac As), the strongest possible starting hand. Elon has gone all-in for a total of 7850. My previous raise was to 5600, so I only need to call an additional 2250 to see the showdown. With a current pot of 13600 and such a premium hand, calling is the only logical and most profitable play. Folding is out of the question as I have a significant equity advantage against any hand Elon might hold.

7-8 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability post-flop. While I have positional advantage on the button and it's only 100 to call, the hand lacks the strength to justify entering the pot against two opponents, especially when I'm short-stacked relative to them (7200 vs 15200 and 17450). My previous hands show disciplined play, and I should maintain that approach. Folding preserves chips for premium opportunities ahead.

Heads-up from the small blind with 6♦4♣. This is a weak, but playable hand in a heads-up SB-vs-BB spot — I want to open a wide stealing range to avoid being too passive out of position. The pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to just complete; instead I’ll raise to take the initiative and deny easy BB odds. I’ll raise to the standard 2BB (200 total). If Flash 3-bets heavily I’ll fold this holding; if called I’ll proceed cautiously out of position, c-betting favorable, dry flops and folding to significant resistance on coordinated boards where I have little equity.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Six of Clubs. Minni, in the Small Blind, has raised to 200, which is only a 100-chip call for me. Although my hand is relatively weak, getting 3-to-1 odds to see a flop against a single opponent makes this a standard call. Raising (3-betting) is an option, but Minni has shown aggression in previous hands (like Hand #4), so I'll keep the pot controlled and play post-flop with position since she is in the Small Blind.

I opened preflop but this flop (K♥ Q♥ 9♥) is very unfavorable for my 6♦4♣ — no pair, no hearts, no backdoor outs, and the board heavily favors the BB’s calling range (many hearts, Kx/Qx). Betting here would be a thin bluff with almost no blockers and low fold equity; facing a bet/raise I have essentially zero equity and should not bloat the pot out of position. I’ll check to control the pot, realize showdown equity if he checks back, and fold to reasonable aggression. If Flash checks I can consider a delayed play on a safe turn; if he bets I fold.

On a monochrome board of Kh-Qh-9h, I hold Ace-high with no hearts. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked. This board is extremely dangerous as it hits a lot of a Small Blind's raising range (broadways and suited connectors). While I could try to bluff, Minni's check doesn't necessarily mean she's weak; she could be pot-controlling a King or Queen, or checking a made flush. Given I have no draw to a flush and only one overcard, taking a free card to see if I can improve to top pair or if the board texture changes is the most prudent play.

I paired my 6 on the turn (6♠) but this is still a very marginal hand on a K♥ Q♥ 9♥ board that heavily favors the BB’s calling range (lots of Kx/Qx and heart flushes). I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with bottom pair and no heart blocker — a bet here would be a thin bluff with poor blockers and will often be called or raised by hands that already beat me. Pot control is the priority: check and see what Flash does. If he fires a meaningful bet I’ll fold to aggression (only a very tiny probe might be considered for a call), and if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the river for a showdown or a cautious action depending on the card.

I have bottom pair (sixes) with an Ace kicker on a very coordinated board (Kh-Qh-9h-6s). Minni raised pre-flop but has checked both the flop and the turn. While my pair of sixes might currently be the best hand if Minni has missed with cards like AJ or AT, the board is extremely dangerous. Any heart or any broadway card makes a better hand for her. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and get to see the river for free. There is little value in betting here, as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I don't want to get check-raised off my modest showdown value.

I only have bottom pair (6s paired on the turn) with no heart blocker on a very heart-heavy K♥ Q♥ 9♥ board. Both streets were checked and the river 7♦ doesn't improve me beyond bottom pair; the BB's range includes many Kx/Qx/heart flushes and even straights (J-T). Betting here has very poor fold equity and will be called by most hands that beat me, so the lowest-variance, highest-EV line is pot control: check and take it to showdown. If Flash now fires a meaningful bet I will fold to aggression.

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a highly coordinated board (Kh-Qh-9h-6s-7d). Minni raised pre-flop but has checked all three streets. This suggests she either has a very weak hand she's giving up on, or a marginal hand she's trying to take to a cheap showdown. By checking back, I realize my showdown value. My pair of sixes is likely ahead of her missed high-card hands (like Ace-high or Jack-high), and there's no reason to bet as I won't get called by worse hands and probably won't fold out better ones.

Three-handed on the button with 9♥4♥. Deep stacks (~150bb) and position are my biggest edges — this hand has little intrinsic value but has backdoor flush/straight potential and plays well in position. Opening on the button is high-EV in 3-handed play: a small raise will pick up the blinds a lot and keeps the pot manageable when the hand is marginal. I’ll raise to the minimum (200 total) to seize initiative and apply fold equity. Plan: fold to a large, committed 3-bet; call a small–medium 3-bet very selectively; if called, play post‑flop aggressively on favorable boards (hearts/connected) and control the pot on dry boards. The raise is cheap relative to my stack and maximizes long-term EV from this seat.

I am in the Small Blind holding 5h 2s, which is an exceptionally weak starting hand. Minni has raised from the Button, and even though the price to call is small (150 to a pot of 350), playing such a weak hand out of position is statistically unprofitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Minni raised to 200 from the button after Flash folded from the small blind. I'm in the big blind with 6h-Qd, a weak hand. Analysis: (1) Q6o is a marginal hand with poor equity against most of Minni's raising range. (2) I'm out of position post-flop, which amplifies the weakness. (3) My stack is 7100, relatively short, requiring careful preservation for better opportunities. (4) While pot odds are 3.5:1, my hand equity is insufficient to justify the call given positional disadvantage and stack considerations. (5) Minni has been aggressive throughout the session and likely has a stronger range. This is a clear fold - I should preserve my chips and wait for a stronger hand in a better position.

I am on the button with K3s in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds and capture the pot or play in position. I will raise to 300, which is consistent with the standard opening sizes observed in previous hands.

Q♣ 3♠ is a weak holding with poor kicker strength. I'm in the small blind (worst position at the table) against an aggressive button raiser. Flash has shown a willingness to apply pressure from late position. Playing Q-3 out of position requires a much stronger raiser than what I have here. My stack of 7050 is healthy enough to fold marginal hands and wait for premium opportunities. The cost-to-benefit analysis doesn't justify calling 250 chips to see a flop in position disadvantage with such a weak hand. Folding is the fundamentally sound play.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with Q♦J♦ and would need to put 200 more (total 300) to call. With effective stacks ~150+bb this is a deep, postflop-heavy situation. QJ suited has good equity and playability versus a typical button opening range and the pot odds are reasonable (200 into a 450 pot → ~30.8% break-even). 3-betting out of position with a hand that wants to see flops is suboptimal — I’d be giving up initiative OOP or bloating the pot without a strong realized equity advantage. Folding is too weak for this holding. I’ll call to realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and plan to continue on favorable flops (diamond/connected/broadway textures) or fold to strong, consistent aggression on dry boards.

Preflop I called 300 with Q♦J♦ to realize equity vs a button open with deep stacks. The flop K♥-7♠-3♣ misses me — I have no pair, only 6 clean outs (3 Qs + 3 Js) and a backdoor diamond possibility, so roughly 24–25% to improve to a pair by river. This is a dry board and the button will c-bet a wide range, so out of position I should control the pot rather than firing or raising. Checking preserves flexibility: I can call small continuation bets to realize equity but fold to large, polarizing aggression. With the pot at 650 the break-even bet size given my ~24.7% equity is about 210–215 chips (X/(650+X)≈0.247), so plan is to check now and call c-bets up to roughly 200–225 if they’re small; fold to bigger c-bets unless I improve on the turn or get a clear read to continue. I will not bluff-raise OOP with this holding.

I have flopped top and bottom pair (Kings and Threes) on a K-7-3 rainbow board. This is an extremely strong hand in a three-handed game, as it beats all single-pair hands and is unlikely to be second-best given the pre-flop action (only 77 or KK would realistically have me beat, and KK likely would have re-raised pre-flop). Minni has checked to me. I want to build the pot and get value from hands like K-x, middle pairs, or straight draws. A standard continuation bet of about half the pot is appropriate here to keep her range wide while extracting value.

I have Q♦J♦ on K♥-7♠-3♣. Flash (BTN) c-bet 325 into a 975 pot so I must call 325. My clean outs are only 3 queens + 3 jacks = 6 outs (no flush/backdoor because there are zero diamonds on the board), which gives ~24.1% to pair by the river. Pot odds = 325 / (975 + 325) = 25.0%, so the call is essentially break-even or slightly negative vs. pure equity. More importantly I'm out of position with deep stacks and will face further turn aggression frequently; raising OOP with no equity or fold equity is thin and likely to create tough, losing spots. I considered calling to realize equity, but the bet is larger than my comfort threshold and the future playability is poor. Therefore I fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots.

Q5o from the button is a weak, non-premium holding with poor playability postflop. While I have position, the hand lacks the strength needed to profitably call against two opponents. With Minni (SB) and Flash (BB) still in the hand, I face 3:1 pot odds on my 100 chip call, but Queen-five offsuit is too weak to justify the investment. Folding weak hands from late position is critical for long-term profitability, especially in a 150-hand grind where hand selection matters significantly. I'll preserve my stack and wait for better opportunities.

Hand is 5♣2♠ — essentially the worst offsuit hand. I already posted the 50¢ small blind, but playing this out of position vs the big blind is a losing proposition long-term. Calling 50 into a 150 pot gives decent raw pot odds, but post‑flop playability is terrible (dominated often, no backdoor redraws), and a raise to 200 to try to steal is too ambitious with this hand against a deep, likely defending big blind. I’ll fold and wait for a better, playable spot — preserve stack and avoid multi‑street marginal OOP decisions.

I'm on the button with pocket jacks in a 3-handed pot — a premium hand that wants initiative and fewer opponents. Standard open here is to raise to ~3x the big blind to isolate the blinds, build a pot and avoid getting multiway where jacks lose equity to overcards. Both opponents are deep, but I don't want to slow-play. I raise to 300 (3x BB); if I face a 3-bet I'll re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent tendencies, but for now take the initiative and apply pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 7d. Minni (Button) has raised to 3x the big blind. This is a weak, offsuit hand that does not play well out of position against a raise. Given the stacks and the action, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

Folding A5o from the big blind facing a button raise. While pot odds are reasonable, the hand is too weak to play out of position effectively. A5o lacks playability post-flop and I'd be forced to act first with information disadvantage. Better to preserve chips and wait for premium situations.

I am on the button with a strong pocket pair of nines. This is a clear raising hand in this position to build the pot and take control. A standard opening size of 300 (3x big blind) is appropriate here.

4d 6d is a weak off-suit hand that lacks both high card strength and playability from the small blind. Against Flash's button raise, I don't have sufficient equity to justify calling 250 chips out of position. This is a straightforward fold to preserve chips for stronger spots.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with 4♠8♦ — a weak, offsuit hand with poor playability and few backdoor prospects. Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call (I’d need ~31% equity to break even). Versus a button opening range my hand’s true equity is well below that and I don’t want to invest out of position or turn this into a marginal showdown. A 3-bet bluff here would be too loose and unnecessarily risky. Fold and wait for a better spot.

Pocket queens is an excellent starting hand (3rd best possible), and I'm in the best position at the table (Button). I have position on both opponents post-flop. The pot currently stands at 150 with only 100 to call. 

Raising is clearly the correct play here. I'm sizing to 450 (representing a standard 3-4x raise) to build the pot while maintaining value and keeping both opponents engaged. This raise:
1. Protects my premium hand
2. Builds a pot I want to play in with such a strong holding
3. Extracts value from weaker hands
4. Is not so large that it folds out all action (I want calls from hands like AK, TT-JJ, etc.)
5. Gives me position advantage post-flop if called

Both opponents have significant stacks and have shown willingness to play, so I expect at least one call.

Claude opened to 450 (4.5bb) from the button. I’m in the small blind with K♦8♠ — an off‑suit, medium‑strength hand that is frequently dominated by the button’s opening range (KQ, KJ, AK, etc.) and plays poorly out of position against two opponents. Calling 400 to see a flop will put me OOP vs both opponents and likely into multi‑way pots where my hand has poor postflop playability. A min‑3bet to 800 is a weak isolation attempt with a marginal hand and invites action from the big blind or a larger 3‑bet from Flash. With a healthy stack and plenty of hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is preferable. Fold and conserve stack for stronger positionable spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack of Hearts and Six of Diamonds. Claude, the Button, has opened to 4.5x the big blind (450). My hand (J6o) is quite weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the relatively large raise size from a shorter stack (Claude has about 65 big blinds left), I see no reason to defend this marginal hand. Folding is the most profitable long-term play here to preserve my stack.

On the button with 2d3d — a small suited connector with good postflop playability and position. The pot is 150 and the to-call is only 100 (big blind), so I can either limp or open. I choose to open to 300 (3x BB): this achieves three goals — it applies pressure to the blinds to steal the pot, isolates one opponent more often, and gives me initiative and positional leverage on the flop where 2-3 suited realizes equity well. The 300 sizing denies cheap multiway odds without bloating the pot; if I get one caller I'll c-bet and play aggressively in position, if both call I'll proceed cautiously looking for straights/flushes or backdoors. I will fold to a large 3-bet (an overbet/3-bet well above ~900) since the hand has limited high-card value and reverse implied odds.

I am in the Small Blind with K7o. The Button (Minni) has opened to 3x. While K7o is a marginal hand to play against a button raise, it's generally too weak to 3-bet for value and out of position, and calling invites the Big Blind to squeeze or see a flop with better pot odds. However, looking at the stack sizes, I have a healthy stack and can afford to occasionally defend or fold. Given the high rake/efficiency considerations in 50/100 games and my position, folding K7o against a 3x open is a standard and safe play to avoid being out of position with a dominated king.

Strong hand (Ace with 8 kicker) in a heads-up situation against an aggressive opponent. Minni is on the button and raised to 300 with a wide stealing range. I'm getting favorable pot odds (450 to 200, or 2.25:1) to defend my big blind. Rather than passively call, I should apply pressure and take the initiative with a 3-bet raise. This serves two purposes: (1) win the pot immediately against a light button raise, or (2) build a pot in position post-flop with a strong starting hand. A raise to 700 represents strength while remaining within reasonable stack management with my 6950 chip stack.

I opened to 300 on the button with 2d3d for initiative and position. Claude’s reraise to 700 is a small-to-moderate 3-bet — it’s below my own threshold for folding to a “large” 3-bet (~900+). With a suited 2-3 I have good postflop playability in position (backdoor flush/straight possibilities and the ability to fold when I miss). Calling 400 requires ~28% equity (400/(1050+400)) which is reasonable versus a BB’s mixed 3-bet range. I don’t want to 4-bet bluff (would bloat the pot and commit with a speculative hand and few blockers), nor is folding necessary given price and position. Plan: call to see the flop, play aggressively when I hit draws or pair/strong equity, and fold to heavy aggression if the board and action make continuation unrealistic.

I flopped a pair of eights with an ace kicker (8d-Ac on 7s-8h-4d). This is a strong hand on this flop. After a preflop 3-bet duel, I'm well-positioned. Minni's call of my 700 3-bet suggests she has a moderate hand—likely a medium pair, broadway cards, or a hand with potential rather than a premium hand. My pair of 8s is ahead of most of her likely holdings (unpaired cards, lower pairs). I should continue my aggressive preflop strategy by betting the flop to: (1) build the pot when I'm ahead, (2) win it immediately against a weak hand, or (3) define her hand strength. A bet of 800 (roughly 55% of the 1450 pot) is sized appropriately given my remaining stack of 5650. This bet maintains aggression while being sustainable if she decides to raise.

Preflop I called with 2♦3♦ for position and backdoor playability, planning to fold to heavy aggression. On this flop (7♠ 8♥ 4♦) I only have weak backdoor outs: I need runner-runner diamonds for a flush or two perfect cards to make a straight — there are essentially no one-card outs. Claude’s line (3-bet preflop then betting the flop 800 into a 2250 pot) represents a fairly value-heavy range and the bet sizes mean calling 800 requires roughly 800/(2250+800) ≈ 26% equity to break even. My realistic equity with 2♦3♦ on this board versus a 3-bettor’s flop-betting range is well below that (low teens or less), so a call is +EV negative and a raise would be too thin — he will fold infrequently and can have hands that crush me. Preserve the stack for better spots and fold.

I am on the button with Jd 2d. This is a speculative suited hand that performs better in multi-way pots or with position. However, J2s is quite weak for a standard opening or a call. Claude is the small blind with a relatively short stack (77.5bb) and Minni is in the big blind. While suited connectors or gappers are playable, J2s is too weak to voluntarily enter the pot from the button when facing action. Since there is no action yet except for the blinds, I have the option to raise or fold. Given the weakness of the hand, I will fold to maintain a tight and disciplined range.

I'm in the small blind with Qs7h heads-up against Minni in the big blind (Flash folded from button). With only 50 more to call and decent pot odds, this is a clear call. While Qs7o is not premium, it has decent showdown value in heads-up play and can flop pairs or straights. The cost to see the flop is minimal relative to my stack, and I'll have reasonable position postflop to play my hand effectively. Folding would be unnecessarily tight given the low price and heads-up situation.

I’m in the big blind with Qc9d facing a limp from Claude (SB). He’s shown a fairly wide limping range and I’m out of position post‑flop, so a tiny preflop raise to 200 would have little fold equity and just create marginal OOP spots. Q9o is a playable hand that wants to see a cheap flop to realize equity. I’ll check and take a free flop — plan is to evaluate texture: barreling or calling only on flops where I have pair/strong draw or good backdoor equity; on dry/bad boards I’ll check‑control and fold to large aggression to conserve stack.

I'm heads-up against Minni on a flop of 6h 3c 3h with Qs7h. This is a weak holding with no made hand and no strong draw. Minni checked from the big blind, which is a weakness indicator on this paired board. 

In heads-up play, checking allows me to:
1. Keep the pot small with a marginal hand that has limited showdown value
2. See another card cheaply and improve (I have two more cards to come)
3. Control the pot size when out of position with a weak holding
4. Let Minni make decisions rather than playing a weak hand out of position aggressively

Betting with Qs7h on this board is too ambitious given how weak my hand is. If called, I'm nearly drawing dead. Checking is the prudent choice to minimize losses while maintaining flexibility to fold if faced with significant aggression or to get to showdown if Minni also checks through.

I checked preflop to see a cheap flop with Qc9d and that exact plan applies now: the flop (6h 3c 3h) is very unfavorable for my hand. I have no pair, no meaningful draw (only one club on board, two hearts on board but I hold none), and Claude limped preflop so his range contains many small pairs and set combos that interact well with this board. Betting OOP here has poor fold equity and will often be called or raised by hands that dominate me. Best line is pot control — check and fold to significant aggression, only continue if I pair/improve or face a very small probing bet I can reasonably call as a bluff-catcher.

On the turn with Qs7h against Minni on a board of 6h 3c 3h 9s, I have a very weak holding - just queen high with no made hand and no legitimate draw. The 9s doesn't complete any straight (I'd need an 8 for the inside draw), and I don't have a flush draw.

This is a classic "showdown with marginal equity" situation. My hand has maybe 5 outs (three remaining Queens for top pair, two remaining 7s for a second pair), giving me around 20% equity. Given the low price to see the river and Minni's checked flop (weakness indicator), the best play is to check down and either reach showdown with queen high or fold if faced with a bet on the river.

Betting here would be overplaying a marginal hand when out of position. Checking allows me to:
1. See the river card cheaply
2. Maintain my stack discipline with a weak hand
3. Evaluate Minni's river action before committing additional chips
4. Potentially win if Minni also checks and my queen high holds up

This is a straightforward check to minimize losses while keeping flexibility.

I checked the flop and turn to control the pot with Q9o and I’ve now improved to a pair of nines on the turn (6h 3c 3h 9s). Claude limped preflop and has been passive (checked flop and turn), so his range contains many worse one-pair hands (6x, 4x, 5x), weak overcards, and some sets (33) that beat me. A small value bet is the best play: it extracts from worse pairs and curious overcards, charges any heart-runner or runner-runner draws, and keeps the pot manageable if I get check‑raised (which is usually a strong hand I can fold to). I’ll make a half‑pot sized bet to get thin value while preserving fold equity. If he calls I’ll reevaluate on the river; if he check‑raises strongly I’ll fold.

On the turn with Qs7h on a board of 6h 3c 3h 9s, I have queen high with approximately 6 outs (three remaining Queens and three remaining 7s), giving me roughly 24% equity. 

Minni's action pattern is important: she checked the flop, which suggested weakness, then raised 100 on the turn. This could be a delayed c-bet, a turn barrel, or even a semi-bluff. The bet size of 100 is relatively modest compared to the pot and my stack size.

Pot odds analysis: I need to call 100 to win a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds and requiring 25% equity to break even. My actual equity at ~24% is marginally tight, but there are several factors that make the call reasonable:

1. Heads-up play typically involves wider ranges and more semi-bluffs
2. Minni's flop check is a genuine weakness indicator
3. The bet sizing is conservative relative to the growing pot
4. If I improve to a pair on the river, I likely have the best hand
5. My stack (7700) is large enough that this 100 chip call doesn't significantly impact my position

This is a close call equity-wise, but the marginal nature combined with position flexibility and Minni's apparent weakness on the flop justifies calling to see the river card.

On the river with Qs7h, I have a pair of 3s with Queen-9-8 kickers - a very weak made hand. The complete board is 6h 3c 3h 9s 8c.

Action pattern analysis: Minni checked both the flop and pre-flop (weakness indicators), raised to 100 on the turn (moderate aggression), and now it's my action with "To Call: 0" indicating she checked the river as well.

This is a classic situation where my hand has marginal showdown value but is too weak to bet for value. Betting would be overplaying a hand that:
- Loses to any Queen with better kicker paired with the 3s
- Loses to any pair higher than 3s
- Only beats hands that completely missed (unlikely given her flop check then turn raise pattern)

My hand (pair of 3s, Q-9-8) is only a moderate showdown hand in heads-up. The prudent play is to check and go to showdown, letting Minni make the decision rather than risking additional chips on a weak hand. If she checks, I win the pot (400). If she bets, I can evaluate fold equity based on the bet size and her tendencies, but checking first minimizes losses while maintaining flexibility.

This is disciplined play with a marginal hand on the final street.

I have Qc9d on 6h-3c-3h-9s-8c: second pair (9s) with a decent queen kicker. Claude limped preflop and has shown a very passive line (check-check on flop, check-call on a small turn bet), which weights his range to a lot of small/medium pairs (66, 55, 44, 22), some 9x, occasional sets (33) and some overcards. My turn bet got one call from that range, so there are many worse pairs that will pay me off on the river. A small-to-medium value bet is the best play — I want to extract from worse pairs and curious overcards while keeping the pot manageable vs the hands that beat me (33 or a superior 9). Betting half the pot (≈200 into 400) accomplishes that: it gets called by many worse hands, keeps fold equity, and avoids bloating the pot where I’m behind. If Claude raises strongly, I treat that as a show of strength (likely trips or a very strong 9) and will fold. Therefore I’ll bet ~half‑pot for thin value.

Calling with Qs7h on river (board: 6h 3c 3h 9s 8c) against Minni's 200 bet. I have a pair of 3s with Q-9-8 kicker - a weak hand but with real showdown value. Pot odds are 4:1 (calling 200 to win 800), requiring only 20% equity. Against Minni's mixed range (flop check suggests weakness, turn raise suggests moderate holding), I likely have 25-35% equity. The combination of favorable pot odds and marginal showdown value makes calling standard poker rather than overcommitting with such a weak hand. If I improve or if Minni is bluffing, I win; if I'm behind, at least I got the right odds to call.

Pocket nines on the button in a 3-handed game is a solid hand to raise with. I have the best position and can win the blinds immediately or play post-flop with positional advantage. A raise to 300 chips is a standard aggression that shows strength without over-committing. This sizing allows me to apply pressure while keeping my risk-reward favorable.

I’m in the small blind with A♠K♥ (premium). Claude opened to 300 from the button; calling 250 leaves me out of position and allows the big blind to enter cheaply. I want to either take the pot down preflop or isolate Claude heads-up, so I 3-bet for value and fold equity. A raise to 1,200 (total) is large enough to pressure a lot of button opens and still leaves room to call a 4-bet or continue postflop if called. If Claude 4-bets big or shoves I’ll re-evaluate (AK is often a call vs shoves but will depend on sizing and villain tendencies); if called, I’ll proceed aggressively on ace/high flops.

I am in the Big Blind holding 4h 5h, which is a decent speculative hand. However, Claude has opened for 3x from the Button and Minni has 3-bet to 1200 from the Small Blind. The price to call is 1100 more into a pot of 1600. While suited connectors have good playability, 45s is at the bottom of that range. Given the significant 3-bet size and the fact that I'll be out of position against two active players, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I'm holding pocket nines (99) on the button in a 3-handed game. After I raised to 300, Minni 3-bet to 1200 from the small blind, and Flash folded from the big blind.

Key factors:
1. Hand Strength: Pocket nines is a premium pair in the top tier of starting hands
2. Position: I have excellent position (button vs Minni's small blind)
3. Pot odds: Current pot is 1600, and I need to call 900 for a 1.78:1 ratio, which is favorable
4. Stack consideration: I have 7100 chips remaining; calling 900 keeps me flexible post-flop
5. Minni's range: Her 3-bet could be premium pairs (AA-TT), AK/AQ, or an aggressive move from position

Analysis: Folding a premium pair to positional pressure would be too passive. A 4-bet could escalate the situation unnecessarily when I have position post-flop. Calling allows me to see a flop in position where I can leverage my 99 effectively. With position on Minni post-flop, I can make more informed decisions based on the board texture. This is a standard call with a strong hand that has good equity against Minni's 3-bet range.

Decision: Call the 900 to see a flop in position.

I have KsAh and the flop is 2♠ K♣ K♥ — I’ve flopped trips (three kings) with the Ace kicker, one of the strongest possible hands on this board. Claude called my preflop 3‑bet from the button, so his range here includes a lot of Kx, medium pocket pairs, some A‑x and occasionally 22 or K2 (the hands that currently beat me are 22, K2 or pocket KK/quads but those are relatively rare). Checking lets Kx hands see cheap cards and avoid building the pot; betting extracts value from worse kings and pairs and keeps Claude’s 6,200 stack engaged for later streets. I’ll bet for value and protection (even though there are no real draws) — size to get calls from Kx and mid pairs but leave room for later action. I choose to bet 2,000 into the 2,500 pot. If called I’ll continue to value‑bet on safe turn cards; if I face a large raise or shove I’ll re‑evaluate because that line would often indicate a full house/quads.

I need to fold here despite holding pocket nines. While 99 is normally a premium hand, the flop is extremely unfavorable for my hand. The board shows 2s Kc Kh - a paired board with kings that dramatically diminishes my hand strength.

Key analysis:
1. Board texture: The paired kings are the worst possible flop for pocket nines. Most hands in Minni's 3-bet range beat me: AA, KK, QQ, JJ, TT, AK, and AQ all dominate my nines.

2. Minni's action pattern: She 3-bet pre-flop (suggesting premium hands or strong holdings) and now bets 2000 on a paired board. This is a strong indicator of genuine hand strength, not a bluff.

3. Equity consideration: While pot odds are 2.25:1 (requiring ~30% equity to call), my actual equity against Minni's likely range (heavy on premium pairs and AK) is probably only 20-25%. I'm hoping to hit a set with my pair, which is a long shot.

4. Stack management: I'm only 32 hands into a 150-hand tournament. There's no need to take marginal spots or flip-like situations. Preserving my 6200 chip stack for better opportunities is the prudent play.

This is a fold, not from weakness but from clear-eyed hand evaluation. My 99 is likely dominated, and the board texture makes this a spot to regroup and pick better spots.

Button with J♦6♥ is marginal but playable — I want to take the initiative and use position rather than limp. A standard ~3x open to 300 applies pressure to the blinds, gives fold equity, and lets me play postflop in position. Claude (BB) has been active preflop but only ~61bb, so I’ll fold to a large, committed 3-bet; if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and play cautiously when out of position or heavily contested. Raising is better than a passive call here to avoid being dominated and to try to steal the blinds.

I'm in the small blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. Minni, on the button, has opened with a 3x raise to 300. Queen-Seven offsuit is generally too weak to defend against a standard button open, especially from the small blind where I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand. While Minni has shown some aggression in previous hands, this hand is well below my range for calling or 3-betting in this spot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

8-To offsuit is a weak hand with only ~32-35% equity against Minni's button opening range. Out of position in the big blind facing a raise, the marginal pot odds (450:200) do not justify calling with such a poor holding. With 6100 chips, I should be selective and avoid marginal situations. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities with premium or stronger suited hands.

I am on the button with 4c 5d, which is a very weak hand. Claude is in the Small Blind and Minni is in the Big Blind. While I could potentially steal here, 45 offsuit is too weak to justify a raise, and calling is not a good strategy from the button with trash. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Raising to 200 with Ad8s in heads-up SB position. This is a solid hand that warrants taking the initiative. A 2x BB raise is standard from the SB - it applies pressure, takes the pot down sometimes, and positions me well for post-flop play. My stack allows for reasonable aggression here. Ad8s plays well both in 3-bet/4-bet scenarios and in heads-up play, making it suitable for raising from the button position.

I’m in the big blind with Ac Kc facing a small‑blind raise to 200 (pot 300, to call 100). AKs is a premium hand that I want to take the initiative with — good equity against a wide SB open and strong fold equity with a 3‑bet. I’ll 3‑bet to apply pressure and isolate Claude, sizing to 900: large enough to fold out weaker aces and many pairs, but not so huge that I overcommit with postflop play. If he calls I’ll continue aggressively on favorable flops; if he 4‑bets I’ll re‑evaluate based on sizing.

Calling 700 more with Ad8s. Pot odds of 2.57:1 require only ~28% equity, and Ad8s has approximately 40-45% equity against Minni's 3-bet range. Out of position but with reasonable stack depth and a hand that plays well in 2-handed scenarios. This allows me to see a flop and make position-based decisions post-flop rather than committing too much with a 4-bet or folding my marginal hand.

Checking on the flop with Ad8s against Minni's 3-bet. The board (2s-5s-Ks) is somewhat dry and favorable to my hand despite being out of position. 

Key considerations:
1. I have a strong flush draw (9 outs to the nuts with any spade) plus the ace as an overcard and potential wheel outs with a 4
2. Minni likely has a strong 3-bet range (likely QQ+, AK, AQ, possibly AJs), but they're first to act on this flop
3. By checking, I accomplish several things: (a) I get to see how Minni continues their aggression, (b) I keep pot sizes manageable with good equity, (c) I can make informed decisions on their bet, (d) I may catch on turn/river with solid outs
4. Stack depths are reasonable (I have 5200, pot is 1800) - I'm not committed yet but have skin in the game
5. Out of position, it's better to let Minni lead and maintain flexibility rather than bloat the pot with a draw

If Minni bets, I'll have decisions based on bet size and position, but checking first gives me maximum information with a strong drawing hand.

I flopped top pair with the best kicker (A K on 2s-5s-Ks) after 3-betting preflop, so this is a clear value/pressure spot. Claude called my 3-bet, so his range likely contains Kx, medium pocket pairs and broadway hands — many worse hands that will pay me off. The board is relatively dry (no obvious straights, only two spades so flush draws require two spades in hand), so a sizeable c-bet will get value from worse Ks/pairs and deny equity to any backdoor combos. Pot is 1800; I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (1200) to extract value while still allowing call from most Kx and medium pairs. If he calls I’ll continue based on the turn card; if he check-raises big I’ll re-evaluate for sets/two-pair and respond based on sizing (likely call a reasonable raise, fold to an overcommitment if he jams).

Calling 1200 with Ad8s on a 2s-5s-Ks flop. I have 40-45% equity against Minni's 3-bet range (QQ+, AK, AQ, etc.), significantly exceeding the 28.6% needed to call based on pot odds of 2.5:1. My 9-spade flush draw plus ace overcard provides strong equity and turn/river outs. Stack depth (4000 remaining after call vs 4200 pot) allows for profitable turn/river decisions with good implied odds. Out of position, calling is preferable to raising semi-bluff; I gather information while maintaining flexibility. This is a mathematically sound call with strong drawing potential.

Checking on the turn with Ad8s. I have premium drawing equity (9 spade outs, 3 ace outs, 4 wheel outs) and am nearly pot-committed with 4000 chips remaining against a 4200 pot. Out of position, checking allows me to gather information on Minni's continuation before committing my remaining stack. If Minni bets, I have favorable pot odds to call with my 40-45% equity and multiple ways to win. If Minni checks behind, I see the river for free with 16 outs. This preserves flexibility while keeping my marginal equity situation manageable.

I have top pair (K) with the best kicker (A). I cbetted the flop and Claude called, so his range contains Kx, medium pairs and some pocket pairs/draws. The turn (3c) is a blank for most of his draws and doesn’t change my hand strength. Pot is 4200 and Claude is short (~4000), so betting for value and to deny equity is correct. I’ll make a ~2/3‑pot turn bet (2800) to extract value from worse Ks and medium pairs, and to put him to a decision. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll reevaluate the river; if he shoves all‑in I’m getting the right price to call against his likely range and will call the jam.

Calling 2800 with Ad8s on the 2s-5s-Ks-3c turn. My estimated 40-45% equity against Minni's 3-bet range (QQ+, AK, AQ) significantly exceeds the 28.6% required by 2.5:1 pot odds. I have multiple strong outs: 9 spade outs for a flush, 3 ace outs for top pair, and 4 wheel outs for a straight draw (~16 total outs). Though nearly pot-committed after this call (1200 remaining), the mathematical advantage is clear - this is a profitable spot to call. The turn blank (3c) didn't improve Minni's likely range, and my drawing equity justifies committing my stack.

Checking on the river with Ace-high (Ad8s on 2s-5s-Ks-3c-Jh). The Jh was a complete blank that didn't improve my draws - no flush made, no straight made. With 1200 chips remaining and a marginal holding, checking allows me to: (1) win the pot if Minni checks behind, (2) gather critical information if Minni bets, and (3) avoid committing my final stack on a vulnerable hand. Against Minni's aggressive 3-bet range (QQ+, AK, AQ), Ace-high is likely behind if I face a bet, but checking preserves maximum flexibility. If Minni bets, my pot odds (~8:1) will determine whether calling is warranted based on my estimated 11-15% equity against their strongest holdings.

I have top pair (K) with the best kicker (A) on a fairly dry board (2♠-5♠-K♠-3♣-J♥). Claude check‑called my flop and turn bets and is now capped at 1,200 behind — his line is consistent with Kx or a medium pocket pair (55/22/33/TT‑QQ) and he’s unlikely to fold those to a small bet. The pot is 9,800, so a small river bet of 1,200 effectively prices him in and extracts maximum value from worse Kx and medium pairs while not overbetting given his short stack. Shoving gains nothing because he can only call 1,200; checking risks missing thin value. Bet 1,200 to get called by the widest number of worse hands.

River decision with Ad8s (Ace-high only). Minni bet 1200 into ~9800 pot. My stack is exactly 1200 remaining, so I'm analyzing whether to call and go all-in. Pot odds are 12200:1200 = 10.17:1, requiring ~9.8% equity to break even. Against Minni's aggressive 3-bet range (QQ+, AK, AQ, AJs), I'm typically behind to made hands, but the question is bluff frequency. Minni's river bet sizing (12% pot) isn't massive value, and given stack dynamics, there's meaningful probability of bluffs or marginal holdings. Even conservative bluff frequency estimates (10-15%) exceed my equity requirement. I'm nearly pot-committed regardless; folding leaves me with 1200 chips in hand 34 of 150. Mathematically, the pot odds justify calling with my estimated 10-15% equity (primarily from bluff scenarios).

Heads-up on the button (I already posted SB), pot 150 and I need 50 to complete. I hold 6♦5♠ — a marginal but playable, connected hand that plays well in position. In heads-up play the button should open a very wide range to seize initiative and pick up the blind. Raising to 200 (2x BB) is a standard, low-risk way to apply pressure: I win the pot outright a lot, deny cheap equity to the BB, and play postflop with position if called. If Flash 3-bets big I will fold this hand and preserve a deep stack; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable, dry flops and control pot size on wet/connected boards. The sizing is small relative to our stacks and keeps postflop options open.

In Hand #35, I am in the Big Blind with 9h 2c. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 200. This is a 2x raise. 92o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although the price to call is only 100 more, I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand with a very weak range. Based on previous hands, Minni has been quite active and aggressive. Defending such a weak hand out of position is generally a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit (As Jc) on the button in a heads-up situation against the big blind (Minni). This is a strong premium hand for heads-up play. Since I am in the Small Blind/Button position and it is folded to me, I should raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. A standard 3x raise is 300 total chips.

Heads-up in the big blind with pocket 3s (3h3d) facing a button open to 300. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to see the flop. With deep stacks (22.1k vs 17.5k) a small pocket pair is best played by flatting to set-mine rather than inflating the pot out of position with a small 3-bet. Calling keeps the pot controllable, preserves strong implied odds if I hit a set, and lets me proceed cautiously postflop if I miss. Plan: call preflop to see the flop; if I flop a set I'll extract value, otherwise give up to heavy aggression or try for cheap showdowns.

I flat-called preflop to set-mine with 3h3d. Flop 8h-4h-Js misses me — I have only two clean outs to make a set (≈4.3% on the turn, ≈8.7% by the river) and a remote backdoor heart route. The board has two hearts and a Jack/overcards, so I have very little showdown equity and can't profitably call most c-bets. Pot is 600; to justify a call I need equity = bet/(600+bet) — with ~8.7% equity my breakeven bet size is only ≈57 chips, which is unrealistic. Plan: keep the pot small and give up when faced with meaningful aggression. Check here to control the pot; fold to any sizable bet, only consider calling an extremely tiny probe.

I have flopped top pair with top kicker (TPTK) on a board of Jack-High (Js 8h 4h). My opponent, Minni, has checked to me. While there is a heart flush draw and some potential straight draws, my hand is currently very strong. I should bet to gain value from weaker Jacks, flush draws, and straight draws. A bet of roughly two-thirds the pot (400 chips) is a standard sizing to build the pot and protect my equity.

I checked the flop with 3h3d to keep the pot small while set-mining. The flop 8h-4h-Js missed me. Flash c-bet 400 into the 600 pot (1000 total) — calling 400 requires ~40% equity (400/(600+400)). I have only two outs to hit a set (2/47 ≈ 4.3% on the turn; ≈8.4% by the river) and a remote backdoor heart possibility (~(10/47)*(9/46) ≈ 4.2%). My realistic equity is far below the 40% needed, so calling is a large -EV. A bluff raise OOP is too risky with deep stacks and a wide calling/3-betting range from the opponent. Fold to preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with 8h8s — a strong pocket pair and I have positional advantage postflop. With deep stacks and a wide big-blind defending range, I want to take the initiative and avoid giving Flash a cheap flop. A standard 3x open to 300 (vs the 100 BB) builds value, folds out weaker holdings, and defines action while leaving plenty of chips to play postflop. Calling 50 is too passive; folding is not an option. Plan is to c-bet most flops, control the pot size when necessary, and re-evaluate if Flash 3-bets aggressively.

I am in the Big Blind with Td6s, a relatively weak holding. The opponent, Minni, has raised to 300 from the Button (Small Blind). While the price to call is only 200 more into a 400 pot, T6o plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Minni has been active and aggressive. Given this is early in the match and I want to wait for better spots to commit chips, folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with T3o. This is a very weak hand. In a heads-up situation against Minni, who has been quite active and aggressive in recent hands, completing the blind or raising with such a weak hand out of position post-flop (if she calls) is generally unprofitable. While I could attempt a steal, the potential reward of 100 chips doesn't justify the risk given the low playability of T3o. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 5h3h and deep stacks (~220bb). In heads-up play I should be opening a wide range to use my positional advantage post-flop and to generate fold equity. 5‑3 suited is playable — it has flush/straight potential and plays well in position vs a wide BB defending range. A standard 2x BB open (raise to 200 total) is the right sizing: it gets value/fold equity without bloating the pot and keeps lots of post-flop maneuvering room. If Flash folds I take the blinds; if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops, float/call with draws, and pot-control with marginal holdings. I will fold to a large 3‑bet but can call small 3‑bets occasionally given implied odds.

In Hand #39, I am in the Big Blind with 8s 6d. Minni, on the Button (Small Blind), has raised to 200 (a min-raise). My stack is healthy at 17,850 chips, while Minni has 21,850. The pot is 300 and it costs me 100 more to call. Given the very favorable pot odds (3:1) and the fact that we are heads-up, 86o is a reasonable hand to defend with, especially against a small raise from a loose-aggressive player like Minni, who has been raising frequently in previous hands. However, out of position, it can be difficult to realize equity. Looking at the history, Minni has been quite active. Calling here allows me to see a flop and evaluate. A fold is too tight given the price, and a raise is unnecessary with a speculative hand like this. I will call.

On a board of Ac 2d Jc, I have 8-high with no draws. This board strongly favors Minni's range as the pre-flop raiser, as it contains two high cards (Ace and Jack). As the Big Blind acting first post-flop, the standard and most prudent play is to check and see how the opponent reacts. I have no reason to lead out (donk bet) into the pre-flop aggressor on a board that likely connects with their range.

I opened wide preflop and Flashes check on A♣ 2♦ J♣ — this is a textbook spot to continuation-bet in heads-up. That board heavily favors my perceived range (I can easily have A or J combos) while a large portion of Flash’s calling range (Kx, Qx, small pairs, many missed broadways) has missed. My actual hand (5♥3♥) has no pair, no backdoor flush (board is clubs/diamonds) and only a 4-out wheel draw to a 4, so this is a pure bluff/pressure spot. A half-pot c-bet (≈50%) maximizes fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. Bet 200 into 400 to take it down now; if I get called I'll give up on most turns unless I hit the 4 or pick up clear additional equity, and I’ll fold to a significant check-raise.

On a board of Ac 2d Jc, I have 8-high with absolutely no equity—no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. This texture heavily favors Minni's pre-flop raising range, which likely contains many Aces, Jacks, and high clubs. Minni's bet of 200 into 400 is a standard continuation bet. Continuing with air out of position is a losing strategy. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Holding T9o on the Button (SB) in a heads-up situation against Minni. T9o is a solid hand to play in position. I will raise to take control of the pot and potentially end the hand pre-flop, or build a pot where I have positional advantage.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300 — I have 8♠9♦ in the big blind. Against a wide opening range this connected offsuit hand has reasonable equity and good implied-odds to hit straights. Calling 200 into a 400 pot requires ~33% equity, which 98o can achieve vs a button's wide range. I'm out of position postflop, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet; calling preserves my ability to see a flop and realize equity. Plan: call to see the flop, continue with pairs/strong draws/two-pair or better, and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards where I miss. Total to commit (including my BB) = 300.

Heads‑up, out of position on A♥‑5♣‑9♣ and I hold 9♦8♠ — a pair of nines with a weak kicker. Villain opened wide preflop so their continuing range has lots of A‑x (which beat me) but also many Kx/Qx, small pairs and club draws that I beat or can charge. Betting a small probe (~1/3 pot = 200) extracts value from worse hands and makes club draws pay to see turns, while keeping the pot controllable if I face aggression. Plan: bet 200; if Flash raises big (polarized) I’ll give credit to strong Ax/set and fold or re‑evaluate strongly; if called I’ll re‑assess the turn and continue for value on safe cards.

I have middle pair (nines) with a Ten kicker on an Ace-high board. Minni's lead (donk bet) of 200 into a 600 pot is a small sizing that could represent a variety of hands, including a weak Ace, a draw, or a smaller pair. Folding middle pair to such a small bet is too tight. Raising would likely only get called by better hands (like an Ace) and force out the bluffs I want to keep in. I will call to maintain pot control and see how the turn develops, using my position to evaluate further action.

I’m out of position with only a single pair (9♦) and a weak kicker on A♥‑5♣‑9♣‑Q♥. My flop probe got called, so the caller’s range contains lots of A‑x, Qx, small pairs and club draws — the turn Q heavily improves many hands that now beat me (Qx and A‑Q), and also brings some heart/second‑pair combos. Pot control is the priority: I don’t want to inflate the pot with a vulnerable one‑pair hand. Checking lets me see whether Flash barrels as a bluff (which I can call a small/medium bet and re‑evaluate) or shows strength (in which case I can fold to large raises). Plan: check. If Flash bets a small–medium size (roughly up to ~1/2 pot) I’ll call to realize equity and target showdown; if he bets big or raises, I’ll fold to avoid getting stacked by two‑pair/sets/Qx. If Flash checks back, I’ll re‑assess the river and look to extract a small value bet on safe runouts.

I have middle pair (nines) on a board with an Ace and a Queen. Minni donk-bet the flop small and has now checked the turn. This check often indicates a surrender or a marginal hand looking to get to showdown cheaply. While I might have the best hand, my hand is not strong enough to value bet for three streets, especially with two overcards present. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, control the size of the pot, and potentially see a cheap showdown or catch a bluff on the river.

Check. I have a lone pair of nines with a weak 8-kicker on A♠‑5♣‑9♣‑Q♥‑K♦. Villain’s line (open preflop, call of my flop probe, passive turn) contains lots of A‑x, Qx and Kx combos that the turn and river often improve — many of which now beat my hand. Betting for thin value will get called too frequently by better hands and only fold out worse rarely; checking keeps the pot under control and lets me reach showdown against bluffs and weaker pairs. Plan: check to showdown and call small river bets from obvious bluffs/weaker hands, but fold to a large river raise.

I have a pair of nines on a board with three overcards (Ace, Queen, and King). Minni led small on the flop but has checked both the turn and the river. This sequence typically suggests a marginal hand or a missed draw. While my nines have some showdown value, they are unlikely to get called by a worse hand if I bet, and I'm certainly not folding out an Ace, Queen, or King at this point. Checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown and realize my equity without risking more chips against a board that has become quite unfavorable.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 4♠6♥ — a marginal, offsuit, low connected hand. The pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 to see a flop (total commitment 100). In heads-up play I’d usually open a wide stealing range, but with this particular holding I prefer pot control and to keep positional advantage postflop rather than bloating the pot OOP with a weak hand. Calling is cheap (I’m getting roughly 25% pot odds on the call) and lets me see a flop where I can continue only with a real pair, strong draw, or favorable texture; otherwise I can fold to significant aggression. I’ll call 50 to 100 total and reevaluate on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit. Minni, on the button, has simply called the small blind. A9o is a strong hand heads-up and is well ahead of a typical limping range. I will raise to 400 to build the pot and take the initiative, despite being out of position for the remainder of the hand.

I limped the small blind with 4♠6♥ intending to see a cheap flop, but Flash’s raise to 400 makes this an expensive proposition. The pot is 500 and calling 300 more means I need ~300/(500+300)=37.5% equity to break even — far too high for 4‑6 offsuit against a raiser’s likely range. A min‑3bet to 700 would be an overaggressive bluff with little fold equity and creates ugly postflop decisions; calling leaves me in tough spots on most flops. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left in the session, preserving chips and waiting for a better, more playable spot is the correct play. I’ll fold.

I am on the button with 6s 7s, a strong suited connector. In a heads-up or short-handed situation, this is a premium hand to open-raise with to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot early. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

I’m in the big blind with 2♥5♦ facing a standard heads-up open to 300 (pot 400, I must call 200). 2-5 offsuit is one of the worst hands preflop: almost no playability OOP (only six pair outs and a single backdoor diamond), frequently dominated by the opener’s range. Calling 200 requires about 25% equity to be profitable; versus a normal button opening range my equity is well under that and I’ll be put in many difficult spots postflop. A light 3-bet to 500 would be a pure bluff with thin fold equity and poor postflop prospects deep-stacked. Prefer to preserve stack and wait for a playable spot — fold here.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 7h6d — a marginal but playable, connected hand with good postflop playability in position. Against a single big blind I want the initiative and to widen my opening range; raising also folds out weaker blinds and gets value from worse hands. With deep stacks (220 BB) I can navigate postflop and realize implied odds, so I’ll open to the standard min-raise (2x BB) to 200. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call smaller 3-bets or continue in position and c-bet favorable flops.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. Minni, who is on the Button/Small Blind, has made a small 2x raise to 200. The pot odds are very favorable at 3:1 (100 to call into a 300 pot). While A5o is not a powerhouse, it is ahead of much of a standard small blind opening range and warrants a defense. I will call to see the flop.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace, though my kicker (5) is weak. On this board (Ad-Qh-3h), there are some draws available (hearts) and many hands in Minni's button-raising range that I beat (K-high, lower pairs, or suited connectors). In a heads-up pot as the Big Blind, the standard play with top pair/weak kicker is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows her to bluff or continuation-bet with a wider range of hands. I plan to check-call most standard bet sizes on this street to keep the pot manageable while remaining ahead of her bluffs and semi-bluffs.

Heads-up in position after I opened and was called. Flop Ad Qh 3h: I hold 7h6d — no pair, only a backdoor heart possibility. As preflop raiser my range is weighted toward A/Q/broadways so this Ace‑high board is a very good c-bet spot to pick up the pot and fold out many of Flash’s flat-call hands (small pairs, Kx, Jx, weak broadways). I’ll apply pressure with a half‑pot continuation bet to preserve fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal hand. If I get raised I’ll fold; if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and look to barrel or check depending on the card.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace, although my kicker is weak (5). Minni's half-pot continuation bet of 200 into 400 is standard for a pre-flop aggressor on an Ace-high board. While I could be out-kicked by hands like AJ, AT, or A9, I am ahead of her bluffs, draws (like hearts), and any Queen. Raising here would likely only get action from hands that beat me, so calling is the most profitable line to keep her range wide and control the size of the pot.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (Aces, 5 kicker). On the turn, the 8d is relatively neutral. As the out-of-position player, the standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows Minni to continue bluffing with her air or semi-bluffs (like heart draws) while keeping the pot manageable in case she has me out-kicked with an Ace-Ten or better. I intend to check-call most standard bet sizes.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the flop; I have 7h6d and the turn (8d) is a blank for my hand — my backdoor heart run is gone and I have almost no showdown equity. Flash checked the turn, showing weakness. Heads‑up in position my raising range contains many Ax/Qx/broadways, so a second barrel here is credible and can fold out Kx, weak broadways and many single‑pair hands that called the flop. I’ll bet a half‑pot-sized bluff (400 into 800) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot controllable if called. Plan: if Flash folds we take down the pot; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the river (check‑down or fold to big bets). If he check‑raises the turn I’ll fold this weak holding.

I have top pair with an Ace, although my kicker is weak (5). The turn card (8d) doesn't change much, and I am still likely ahead of many hands in Minni's button-opening range, including heart draws, Queens, and lower pairs. Minni's bet of 400 into an 800 pot offers me 3:1 odds to call. Raising here would likely only be called by hands that have me beaten (better Aces or two pairs), so calling is the optimal way to control the pot size while keeping her range wide enough to include bluffs. I will call and see the river.

On the river board of Ad-Qh-3h-8d-Js, I have top pair with an Ace but a very weak 5 kicker. The heart draw has missed, and the board has become somewhat connected with the Jack. I have check-called two streets already. There is very little value in betting myself, as most hands that would call me (better Aces, two pairs, or sets) have me beaten, while hands I beat (Queens, missed draws) are likely to fold or check back. By checking, I give Minni the opportunity to bluff with missed draws like heart combinations or T9, and I can evaluate her bet size if she chooses to fire a third barrel.

I opened in position and barreled flop (200) and turn (400), representing a strong Ace/Queen-heavy range. The river (Js) gives me zero showdown value with 7h6d — my backdoor heart is gone and I have no pair. Flash checked the river after calling both previous barrels, showing some weakness or a desire to avoid a big river confrontation. With nothing to gain by checking (I’ll almost always lose at showdown) I should try a value-polarized bluff. 

I’ll bet a credible half-pot (800). Size rationale: 800 is large enough to fold out Kx, Jx, missed heart draws and many small pairs yet small enough to limit my loss to a manageable amount if Flash calls with Ax/Qx. The line (200 → 400 → 800) is consistent with representing top pair/two pair.
