If Flash folds I scoop the 1600 pot; if he calls I expect to lose at showdown but the bluff has enough fold equity to be +EV overall. If he check-raises the river I will fold (my hand has no showdown value).

I have top pair with an Ace, though my kicker (5) is weak. The board has developed with a Queen and a Jack, and the heart draw has missed. Minni has bet half-pot on every street (flop, turn, and river). Based on previous hands, specifically Hand #34, Minni has shown a willingness to triple-barrel with marginal hands like middle pair. While it is possible she has me out-kicked with a hand like Ace-Ten or better, or has improved to two pair with the Jack or Eight, the pot odds of 3:1 (800 to call into a 2400 pot) make this a mandatory call in a heads-up situation. Folding top pair here would be too exploitable against an aggressive opponent who frequently fires multiple barrels. I will call to see the showdown.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 4d3h, a very weak hand. In a heads-up situation, while I have position, this hand is at the bottom of the range. Looking at previous hands, Minni has been quite active and has shown a willingness to play back. Calling or raising with 4-3 offsuit is generally a losing play over the long term, even with position. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my position.

Heads-up on the button with Js9d. Pot is 150 and I need to put in 50 to call; stacks are very deep (~200bb). J9o is a strong, playable heads-up hand (decent high-card, straight potential and position advantage). I want to seize initiative and avoid giving the big blind cheap odds to see flops, so I’ll open-raise. A 3x BB sizing (to 300 total) is standard and fits table tendencies — large enough to apply pressure but small relative to our deep stacks. Plan: if Flash calls I’ll use position to c‑bet favorable flops, control the pot on dry boards, and fold to a large, committed 3‑bet; if Flash folds I pick up the blinds. Raising maximizes EV here versus just completing the small blind.

I have been dealt pocket kings (Kd Kh), the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My opponent, Minni, has raised from the button/small blind to 300. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that we are head-to-head, I want to build a large pot and potentially get all the money in. I will put in a 3-bet (re-raise). A standard size here would be around 3x to 4x the open raise, especially being out of position. I'll raise to 1,000 to extract value and narrow the range.

I opened to 300 from the button and Flash 3-bet to 1,000. Stacks are very deep (~200bb), so I want to preserve post‑flop maneuverability and use my positional advantage. J♠9♦ is a strong, playable heads‑up hand vs a wide 3‑bet range — calling 700 more (1000 total) is cheap relative to the stacks and gives me ~35% pot odds to see a flop and realize equity. A 4‑bet bluff here would need to be sized larger to be credible and would commit too much with a marginal hand; folding is too nitty given the price and position. Plan: call, see the flop in position, c‑bet favorable textures or check/realize equity on dry boards, and fold to heavy, committed aggression if the action indicates strong made hands. Call 1000.

I am the 3-bet aggressor pre-flop with pocket Kings. The flop comes Ace-high (As 4d 5h), which is a classic scare card for my hand. Since I am first to act post-flop, I need to decide between a continuation bet and a check. While I represent the Ace in my 3-betting range, Minni's range also contains many Aces that would have called a 3-bet (like AJs, ATs, or AQo). By checking, I exercise pot control with a hand that has strong showdown value but is likely beaten if a lot of money goes into the middle. This also allows me to induce bluffs from smaller pairs or floats, and I can evaluate her bet size to decide on a call.

Heads‑up with position and a checked A‑5‑4 rainbow flop. My J♠9♦ has little immediate showdown value but some backdoor equity; more importantly, Flash’s check looks like weakness or pot control rather than a polar strong line. A small c‑bet will fold out many of his missed broadways and medium pairs while keeping the pot manageable if I face resistance. Size to ~1/3 pot (≈700 into 2000) — applies pressure without committing deep stacks and preserves options: collect the pot if he folds, call/float if he calls and re-evaluate on the turn, but fold to a large check‑raise because my hand has poor raw equity. Bet 700.

I have pocket Kings, but the flop contains an Ace, which is a significant threat to my hand given that Minni called a 3-bet pre-flop. After I checked to exercise pot control, Minni bet 700 into a 2,000 pot. This bet size (about 35% of the pot) is small enough that I cannot fold yet, as she could be betting with a middle pair (like JJ-88) or a draw. I have the necessary pot odds to call and see if she continues her aggression on the turn. I will call the 700 to evaluate the turn.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Five of Clubs (5c). My pocket Kings (Kd Kh) remain in a difficult spot due to the Ace on the flop. However, the 5c is relatively a blank card, as it is unlikely Minni has many 5s in her 3-bet calling range (perhaps only A5s or 56s). I checked the flop and called a small bet to control the pot size and induce bluffs. Checking again on the turn is the most consistent play. It allows Minni to continue betting with worse hands or bluffs, while also preventing me from bloating the pot against an Ace. If she checks back, I get a free look at the river; if she bets, I will evaluate the sizing and likely call to reach showdown.

Turn: the 5♣ pairs the board (5♥4♦A♠5♣). My J♠9♦ missed and has essentially no showdown value vs the range that called my flop c-bet. Flash was the pre‑flop 3‑bettor and called my flop bet — his continuing range is heavy in A‑x, pocket pairs and the occasional 5x (and those hands now either have or heavily beat me). Betting here has poor fold equity (Ax will largely call for pot control, trips/full houses will slow‑play or check‑raise) and can commit a big chunk of our deep stack with essentially zero equity. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity, and keep the option to bluff or fold on the river depending on the runout and his action.

I am holding pocket Kings on a board of As-5h-4d-5c-Td. The presence of the Ace on the flop is the primary concern. In the pre-flop action, I 3-bet and Minni called. On the flop, I checked to control the pot, and Minni bet 700, which I called. The turn (5c) and river (Td) didn't significantly change the board texture. Minni's check on the turn is a key piece of information; it suggests she either has a marginal Ace she's pot-controlling, a middle pair (like QQ, JJ, or 99), or she was bluffing the flop and has given up. By checking the river, I allow myself to get to a relatively cheap showdown. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands (like QQ or JJ) on an Ace-high board, and I will almost certainly be called by any Ace that beats me. Therefore, checking is the most prudent move to maximize value from her potential bluffs or to lose the minimum to a marginal Ace.

Flash was the preflop 3-bettor, then called my small flop c-bet and proceeded to check‐check on the turn and river. That line is consistent with A‑x and medium pocket pairs (or pot control with strong hands). My J♠9♦ has no pair and basically zero showdown value on 5♣4♦A♠5♥10♦; most hands that continued will call a river bluff rather than fold, so my fold equity is very low. Betting here risks chips for little chance to win the pot and opens me to a check‑raise I can’t profitably continue against. With the pot small relative to effective stacks and no reasonable value to extract, I’ll preserve my stack and check to showdown (or fold to a bet).

I am on the button with Ace-Four offsuit. In heads-up play, this is a strong starting hand that should almost always be played aggressively to take control of the pot. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to either win the blinds immediately or build a pot where I have positional advantage.

Heads-up in the BB vs SB open to 300 and I hold K♠T♠ — a strong, well-blocking, suited hand that performs very well in HU. Flash’s open from the SB will be wide; calling would give him position and let him see flops cheaply. I want to seize initiative and get value from worse Kings, broadway combos and suited connectors while also folding out some of his weakest speculative holdings. I size a 3-bet to 1,200 (≈4x the open) — big enough to gain fold equity and thin his range, but small enough to leave plenty of stack for postflop maneuvering. If he 4-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a very polarized shove; call or continue vs smaller 4-bets depending on sizing).

I have Ace-Four offsuit (A4o) on the button (SB) in a heads-up situation. I opened for 3x, and Minni has 3-bet me to 1,200. Minni has shown a high level of aggression in previous hands, including 3-betting light and 4-betting as a bluff (Hand #4, Hand #34). While A4o is at the lower end of a calling range against a 3-bet, the presence of an Ace serves as a blocker to her value range (AA, AK, AQ). Given that I have the positional advantage and have seen her play aggressively with marginal hands, I will call the 3-bet to see a flop and evaluate, rather than folding away the equity of an Ace-high hand or bloating the pot further with a 4-bet.

Heads-up after my 3-bet to 1,200 and Flash’s call; flop is Qc 7s Js and the pot is 2,400. I hold K♠T♠: I have the nut spade draw (K-high flush draw), plus broadway straight outs (A or 9) and a high overcard. Counting unique outs I have roughly 15 clean outs to make a winning hand by the river (~54% to improve by river). As the preflop aggressor I want to keep the initiative and can credibly represent Qx/Jx or strong broadway hands — plus this hand has real equity when called. I’ll make a standard semi-bluff / c-bet sized to build the pot when I hit and to fold out weaker holdings (Ax without a pair, some Kx, missed broadways). A half‑pot bet keeps pressure without committing too much of my deep stack; if Flash raises big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to an obviously polarized shove but continue vs a small raise depending on sizing). Bet 1,200 into 2,400 pot to apply pressure and build when favored.

On a flop of Qc-7s-Js, my Ace-high has very little equity against Minni's 3-betting range, which includes hands like AQ, AJ, JJ, and QQ. Minni's half-pot continuation bet indicates strength on a board that favors the pre-flop aggressor. Given that I have no draws and only one overcard (the Ace) which might not even be clean if she holds AQ, folding is the most prudent move to preserve my stack.

I’m on the button (small blind) with 8♦2♣ — a very weak, unsuited hand. In heads-up play the button should be opening extremely wide to seize initiative and use positional advantage postflop, rather than passively giving the BB a free or cheap flop. The pot is 150 and it’s 50 to call; min-raise is 200. I’ll raise to 200 total (putting in +150). This is a standard, small heads-up open to steal the blind and keep the pot controllable. If Flash folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll play position postflop and fold to large, committed 3-bets with this garbage hand.

I am in the Big Blind holding 5s 4h. Minni, on the button, has made a minimum raise to 200. While the pot odds are 3:1 (requiring 100 more to see a 300 pot), 54-offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially when playing out of position. Given that we are early in the 150-hand match and I want to avoid high-variance or marginal situations out of position with bottom-tier hands, I will choose to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 4c 5h, which is a very weak starting hand. While I would have position post-flop, this hand is too weak to justify a raise against an opponent who has shown aggression. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button with T8o is a clear open-raise spot. I have position, decent post-flop playability, and a deep 200bb stack — so I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the BB. Raising to 300 (3x the BB) gives good fold equity against marginal hands, builds a pot when I have position, and keeps a comfortable stack behind for postflop maneuvering. Plan: if Flash calls I’ll c-bet on dry, A/K-high flops and play cautiously on coordinated/flushy boards; fold to a large 3-bet unless it’s a small-sized squeeze where a call or light 4-bet is warranted by reads.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Six offsuit is a strong hand that is well ahead of a random button-raising range. Since Minni is quite active, I will call the raise to see a flop and keep the pot manageable while playing out of position. Calling allows me to realize the value of my Ace-high if the board comes dry or hit a pair while not committing too many chips to a potential 4-bet.

I am first to act on a dry King-high flop. My Ace-high has some showdown value but is vulnerable. Since Minni was the pre-flop raiser and this board texture generally favors the raiser's range, checking is the most standard and prudent play. I will check to see how Minni reacts and potentially catch a bluff or realize my equity cheaply.

I was the preflop raiser and the flop Kc 2h 4s is a very dry, rainbow texture that favors my raising range. I hold T♠8♦ — missed the flop but I have position and little to lose by bluffing. Against one opponent heads-up this is a high-frequency continuation-bet spot to pick up the pot and to fold out many overcards and weak pairs. I’ll size small to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot controllable: roughly 1/3 pot (600 pot → bet 200). If Flash calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and often check back unless I pick up meaningful equity or see a good double-barrel spot; if I face a significant check-raise I’ll usually fold given my lack of showdown value.

On this very dry King-high board (Kc 2h 4s), Minni has made a small continuation bet of 1/3 pot. My Ace-high (Ad 6s) still has significant showdown value against her air/bluff range, and I have three outs to top pair if she has a smaller pair (like 33 or 55). Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 800, which is 4:1), I have the necessary equity to continue. I will call here to see the turn and keep the pot manageable, as raising would likely only get called by better hands.

The Turn card is the 6 of Diamonds, giving me second pair (Sixes) with an Ace kicker. The board is relatively dry (Kc 2h 4s 6d), though there are some back-door straight possibilities. Minni checked behind on the turn after I checked to her. This check-back from the pre-flop aggressor often signals a marginal made hand (like a weak Ace or a small pair) or a complete air ball that she's giving up on. Since I've improved to a pair and checked to her, I will continue checking to keep the pot small and potentially induce a bluff on the river or reach a cheap showdown. My hand is now significantly stronger than just Ace-high.

I opened to 300 and c‑bet 200 on the very dry Kc 2h 4s flop; Flash called. Turn is 6d and he checks to me. Holding Ts 8d I have no pair and no meaningful draw — the 6d doesn't improve my equity. Flash’s flop-call then turn-check looks like pot control with Kx, a small pair, or a marginal holding that will often call a turn bet; betting here would bloat the pot with very little chance of getting folds from hands that currently beat me and would force tough decisions if he check‑raises. With deep stacks and position, preserving stack and controlling the pot is the higher EV line. I’ll check back, see the river, and then choose a river plan (value/raise if I improve to a pair, small bluff on safe rivers, fold to serious aggression).

On the river, the board has paired the sixes, giving me trips with an Ace kicker. After Minni checked back the turn, it's unlikely she has a powerhouse, but she could have a King or a middle pocket pair. I will lead with a value bet of 600 into the 1000 pot. This size is large enough to get value from a King or a stubborn pocket pair, while still being a credible size for a missed draw or a thin value bet, potentially inducing a call from her marginal range.

Pot is 1600 and Flash bet 600 on the river (I must call 600). I hold T8o with no pair, no kicker, and no relevant blockers. Flash’s line — calling my flop c‑bet then checking the turn and firing a small 600 into 1600 on the river — looks like a thin-value or blocking bet (Kx, a small pair or 6x), not a frequent pure bluff. Calling requires ~27% equity (600 to win 2200) which I don’t have versus a value-heavy range; raising as a bluff has poor fold equity because I lack blockers and Flash has shown willingness to call down. Best EV play is to preserve my stack and fold to this river aggression.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with Ace-Nine offsuit (A9h), a strong hand for heads-up or short-handed play. I have the positional advantage for the rest of the hand. Minni has been active and sometimes aggressive, but A9o is well ahead of an average opening or defending range. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Heads-up on the big blind with T♥T♦ — a strong, value hand well ahead of the SB’s wide opening range. With both stacks ~200bb, I want value and fold equity rather than just calling and playing a bigger, cheap pot out of position. A small min-raise (to 500) is too weak; I’ll 3-bet to build the pot and charge speculatives while keeping maneuverability vs a potential 4-bet. Raise to 1,200 total (about 4x the open): if called I’ll continuation bet on many favorable flops and re-evaluate on turn; if faced with a large 4-bet I’ll reassess based on sizing and read (TT can call reasonable 4-bets but fold to extremely polar shove sizes).

I have Ace-Nine offsuit (As 9h) on the Button/Small Blind. I opened to 300, and Minni 3-bet to 1200. While A9o isn't a premium hand, it is quite strong in a heads-up situation. Minni has shown high aggression previously (e.g., Hand #46 where she 3-bet my open). Folding here would be too exploitable given my positional advantage. A 4-bet is possible but might isolate me against better hands like AQ+ or 99+. Calling allows me to play the pot in position and see how the flop develops, keeping her potential bluffs in her range.

I 3‑bet preflop and we’re heads‑up in a relatively dry 3‑bet pot (pot 2400) on J♣ 6♠ 4♥. I hold T♥T♦ — a vulnerable but still very playable pair: I’m behind any J and behind sets/two‑pair, but ahead of lots of the caller’s range (small pairs, A‑x/K‑x, some broadways and bluffs). As the preflop aggressor I should continue with a c‑bet to get value from worse hands, deny equity to any backdoor combinations, and apply pressure to unmade hands. I’ll size to about half the pot to balance extracting value from small pairs/Ax while not overcommitting if I get resistance; that keeps SPR reasonable for post‑turn decisions. If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (proceed cautiously on J/4/6 or coordinated cards); if faced with a large raise I’ll assume a strong/polarized range (J, set, two‑pair or big bluff) and fold to extreme pressure or call smaller sizing depending on reads. Bet 1200.

I am on the Button with Ace-Nine offsuit (As 9h). I raised pre-flop and Minni 3-bet to 1200, which I called to play the pot in position. The flop comes Jack-Six-Four rainbow (Jc 6s 4h). Minni leads out with a continuation bet of 1200 into the 2400 pot. This board texture is fairly dry but heavily favors a 3-betting range that includes JJ, AJ, and overpairs. My Ace-high has very little equity here and no real draws to rely on. While Minni has shown aggression in previous hands, calling down with just Ace-high on a Jack-high board without a clear plan to bluff is a recipe for losing a large pot. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 8♠9♦ — a playable, slightly speculative hand that benefits from position post‑flop. With only one opponent and deep stacks, I want to take the initiative rather than just complete to the BB. Standard heads‑up open is 2x the big blind, which (a) defines ranges, (b) prevents giving a free flop, and (c) keeps the pot a comfortable size for postflop play. If Flash calls, I’ll C‑bet favorable textures and proceed with position; if he 3‑bets big I’ll fold this offsuit connector, but I can call a smaller, reasonable 3‑bet to see a flop. Raise to the minimum open (200) to implement this plan.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Seven of Diamonds, a strong suited hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has opened with a minimum raise to 200. I need to call only 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. This is a mandatory defend with a hand of this strength. While I could 3-bet for value or as a bluff, calling allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable post-flop, where my hand has decent playability.

I am out of position as the Big Blind on a dry board of 9h-5d-3c. I hold King-high with overcards to the board and a backdoor flush draw. Standard play in this situation is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) to see their continuation bet sizing and assess whether my King-high has enough equity to continue or if I can potentially take the pot away later.

I have 9♦8♠ on 9♥-5♦-3♣: top pair with a decent kicker on a very dry board. Heads‑up I was the preflop aggressor, so a continuation bet is standard to get value from worse pairs (5x/3x, small pocket pairs) and to deny equity to backdoor draws or overcard floats. Flash checked, showing weakness or a hand he wants to see a price with — a ~60% pot c‑bet balances value and pot control. I will bet 250 into the 400 pot to extract value and charge draws; if he check‑raises large I’ll re‑evaluate and likely fold to heavy aggression, but I’ll call a small raise and continue cautiously on the turn.

I am holding King-high with a backdoor flush draw on a dry 9-5-3 rainbow board. Minni has placed a continuation bet of 250 into a 400 pot (62.5% pot). While I have overcards, playing out of position against an aggressive opponent with only King-high and no real draw is difficult. I don't have enough equity to comfortably call this sizing, and I prefer to wait for a better spot rather than floating with a marginal hand.

I am on the Button/Small Blind with AKo, a premium starting hand. This is a clear raising hand to build value and gain initiative. Given the stack depths and heads-up play, I want to apply pressure and control the pot. I'll raise to 3x the big blind, which is 300 total.

Facing a standard 3bb open to 300 from the button, I’ll defend with 4♠3♠ by calling (total 300). Heads-up and ~210bb deep, small suited connectors have good playability and implied odds vs a wide SB open. The pot is 400; calling 200 more to make the pot 600 gives me ~3:1 (25%) pot odds — 4-3s has sufficient equity vs a typical wide opening range. I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal 3-bet; calling keeps pots manageable and lets me realize equity and play aggressively on flops where I pick up a flush/straight draw or a pair. Plan: continue when I hit draws/pairs, defend selectively vs heavy action, fold to large turn/river aggression on dry boards.

Flop: Tc 4h 6s — I have 4♠3♠: a paired 4 with a tiny backdoor spade possibility. Heads-up vs a standard button open, my preflop plan was to continue when I hit pairs/draws, so I should take the initiative here. The board is fairly dry and many of his missed overcards will fold to pressure, while worse holdings and some draws will call. I’ll make a small value/protective c-bet of ~1/3 pot: bet 200 into 600. This charges overcards and draws, gets value from hands that will call a small bet, and keeps the pot manageable out of position. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll exercise pot control on the turn and re-evaluate based on the card and sizing. If he check-raises large (showing clear strength), I will fold — I don’t want to build a big pot out of position with a marginal pair.

Minni has led into me with a small bet (1/3 pot) on a relatively dry flop. Holding Ace-King, I have two overcards and currently have the best possible Ace-high. This 'donk bet' could represent a weak pair or even a draw like 87/75, but it could also be a feeler bet. Given the pot odds and my position, calling is the most efficient play. It keeps the pot manageable, keeps her range wide, and allows me to re-evaluate on the turn if I improve or if she checks.

I hold 4♠3♠ on Tc‑4h‑6s‑2c — second pair with a very weak kicker. Villain opened preflop and simply called my small 1/3-pot c‑bet on the flop, so his range is wide: Tx, overcards, small pairs and some draws. The 2♣ is a relatively harmless blank for my hand (it slightly helps any A2/72 combos), and the board is still fairly dry. With deep stacks and a marginal pair OOP, I want to control the pot and avoid committing more chips out of position to a hand that loses to any Ten, any 6, and many overcards. I’ll check to see what villain does — if he fires a small turn bet (~≤1/3 pot) I’ll call to extract value from worse pairs and some Ax holdings; if he fires a large bet or polarizing shove I’ll fold. If he checks back I keep the pot small and re-evaluate on the river. This line maximizes long‑term EV by protecting my stack from large bluffs or strong made hands while still allowing me to extract value from thin callers.

On the turn 2c, the board remains relatively disconnected (Tc 4h 6s 2c). Minni donk-bet the flop and has now checked the turn, which often indicates she is slowing down with a marginal hand or giving up on a bluff. My Ace-King high still has significant showdown value against her range and holds about 25% equity against a pair of Tens. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity for free, and can potentially hit an Ace or King on the river to take the lead. If she is trapping, checking prevents me from losing more chips; if she is weak, I can see a showdown or evaluate a river bet if she checks again.

The river 5♥ completes my straight. With 4♠3♠ on Tc‑4♥‑6♠‑2♣‑5♥ I have 2‑3‑4‑5‑6 — a very strong made hand heads‑up. Villain opened preflop and simply called my small 1/3‑pot c‑bet on the flop, then checked the turn and checked the river; that line contains lots of Tx, 6x, small pairs and broadway/air hands that will often call a small river bet but rarely beat a straight. Only holdings with a 3 split with me (few combos) — there are essentially no hands that realistically beat my straight on this board. Pot is 1000; I want a sizing that maximizes calls from worse hands without folding out thin value, so I’ll bet roughly one‑third of the pot. A 300 chip river bet extracts value from tens, sixes, paired fives, and many overcards while keeping the price reasonable vs a check‑raise. If he check‑raises small I’ll strongly consider calling (my hand is robust); if he overbets/shoves I’ll re‑evaluate but this is still a call‑heavy situation for me.

On the river, the board is 10c-4h-6s-2c-5h. Minni has led for a small bet of 300 into a pot of 1000. My Ace-King high is a strong Ace-high hand but has no pair. Minni's line (donk betting the flop, checking the turn, then leading the river) is often indicative of either a weak pair (like 4x, 6x, or a low pocket pair) or a missed straight draw (like 87 or 75). Given the pot odds (300 to win 1300), I only need to be right about 19% of the time to justify a call. However, since it's heads-up and her bet sizing is small, it's possible she's value betting a weak 10 or a small pair. Since I cannot beat any pair, calling is risky, but folding to such a small bet with AK is also difficult. Re-evaluating the board, 3-7 and 8-7 missed, but 3-4 and other small connectors might have improved. Ultimately, AK high rarely wins when an opponent leads into you on the river after showing interest on the flop. However, given the price and the nature of heads-up play, this is a borderline decision. I will fold here as I have no showdown value against her range.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the SB 50) with K♦8♠ — a strong, playable hand in position. Calling 50 keeps the pot small and gives the BB a free or cheap flop; I prefer to take the initiative and use positional advantage. Min raise is to 200, but a standard 3BB-style open to 300 (total committed) is a clear, table-consistent size: it applies fold equity, folds out many dominated/medium hands, and is small relative to our deep stacks so we retain maneuverability postflop. If called I'll continuation-bet a wide range on dry boards and play carefully on coordinated/flushy/ace-high textures. Raise to 300 to seize initiative and play the hand in position.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s6s, a strong suited connector that plays well post-flop, especially in a heads-up situation. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. With a stack of over 18,000 and the pot at 400, calling the 200 is a standard move to see a flop. This hand has good playability and I have the potential to hit a straight or flush, making it worth defending the blind.

I have landed a strong but vulnerable hand, bottom two pair (sixes and fives), on a very coordinated board of 7-6-5. This board texture is excellent for my range as the Big Blind, as I can have many more straights (89, 48) and two-pair combinations than a typical Button opening range. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor, Minni, to see if she continues with a bet. If she bets, I intend to check-raise to extract value from her overpairs and to protect my hand against the numerous straight draws that are present.

I opened to 300 preflop and was called; flop is 7♠-5♦-6♣, pot ~600. Holding K♦8♠ I have a clean semi-bluff: an open-ended straight draw (need a 4 or 9 — ~8 outs) plus a high card. In position versus a checked BB this is a good spot to take the initiative and extract fold equity while keeping the pot manageable because the flop also heavily favors the caller’s defending range (small pairs, 5/6/7, suited connectors). I’ll c-bet a modest 1/3 pot (200) — big enough to fold out some pure air and protect my equity, small enough to realize my draw cheaply if called. If called I’ll use position to evaluate the turn (continue with completed straights/top pair, or check/control on blanks; fold to large, polarized raises).

I have flopped bottom two pair on a highly coordinated board (7-6-5). This board texture is excellent for my range as the Big Blind. While two pair is a strong holding, it is extremely vulnerable to many turn cards (any 4, 8, or 9 completes a straight, and any 7 counterfeits my hand). Minni's small continuation bet of 200 (1/3 pot) allows me to check-raise for value and protection. I want to charge her draws and overpairs (like AA-88) now. I will raise to 800 to define the hand and build the pot.

I have K♦8♠ on 7♠-5♦-6♣ — an open‑ended straight draw (4s or 9s = 8 outs) plus a decent kicker. Pot is 1600 and Flash’s raise prices me to call 600 more, so I need ~27% equity to break even. My OESD (≈31–32% to hit by river) plus some extra showdown equity vs bluffs makes a call +EV. Flash’s check‑raise is polarized (sets/top pairs and some bluffs); re‑raising to 1400 is an option as a semi‑bluff but would commit a lot more out of position and is riskier against a value‑heavy check‑raise range. I’ll call, retain position and fold/control on turns that miss or continue aggressively if I improve or see clear weakness.

I hold bottom two pair (sixes and fives) on a board of 7s-5d-6c-2c. The deuce on the turn is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Minni's call of my flop check-raise indicates she likely has a strong one-pair hand like an overpair (88-AA) or a straight draw (98, 84, or 43). To maximize value from these holdings and to protect my hand against the various draws, I will lead out with a bet. A bet of 1400 represents roughly 64% of the pot, which is a standard sizing to keep the pressure on and build the pot before a potentially scary river card arrives.

Board: 7♠-5♦-6♣-2♣, my hand K♦8♠. Flash check‑raised the flop big (800) and now barrels a sizable turn bet (1400). My only clean outs to a straight are any 4 or 9 (8 outs). On the river that's 8/46 ≈ 17.4% equity. To call 1400 into a 3600 pot would make the final pot 5000, so I need ~28% equity to break even. My raw equity (≈17%) is well below the required pot odds, and Flash's line is heavily value‑weighted (sets, 8‑9 straights, strong pairs/combos) — not a range I can realistically draw against profitably. A raise as a bluff would be too risky here (likely called by many hands that beat me) and I have essentially no showdown value. Preserve the stack and fold.

I am holding King-Jack offsuit (Kd Jh) on the button in a heads-up situation against Minni. KJo is a strong hand in this position and is well ahead of a random range. I will raise to the standard 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and take control of the hand.

I’m in the big blind with 2♥9♦ facing a button open to 300 (pot 400, I must call 200). 2‑9 offsuit is one of the worst hands to defend — very poor post‑flop playability, frequent domination and weak equity vs a button opening range. Calling here requires roughly 33% equity (200 into a 600 pot) and 2‑9o doesn’t have that against a typical BTN range. A 3‑bet bluff is possible in theory, but this hand has terrible blockers and will lead to difficult multi‑street decisions when called. With a deep stack I’d rather preserve chips and wait for a much better spot or a hand with real playability. Fold.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦8♣ — a hand I want to play aggressively. I’ve already posted the SB (50) and the BB is 100; calling (putting 100 total) surrenders initiative and lets the BB see a cheap flop. With deep stacks (~200 BB) and position postflop, opening most hands is +EV. Q8o has decent high-card value and some backdoor straight/flush possibilities, so I’ll raise to take the lead, apply fold equity, and isolate the BB. I choose a standard 3x BB sizing to 300 total — big enough to discourage easy floats and consistent with table tendencies, but small enough to allow profitable postflop play if called. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold to heavy pressure; I’ll continue postflop in position if called.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc5d facing a raise to 3R from Minni on the Button. This is a weak hand and generally folds to a standard raise in a heads-up or small-blind vs big-blind dynamic unless there is a specific strategic reason to defend. Given the stack sizes are deep and I'm out of position, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

I have Queen-Ten offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a strong starting hand for this position. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the Big Blind, as Minni has folded to button raises several times in previous hands.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with pocket tens (T♠ T♣) and ~200BB effective — a premium hand heads-up and ahead of the SB’s opening range. I want to 3-bet for value and fold equity rather than just calling and playing a bloated pot out of position. Raising to 1200 (~4x the open) builds the pot against worse hands (broadways, mid pairs), protects against overcards and suited connectors, and gives a reasonable chance to take it down preflop. If Flash 4-bets big I’ll re-evaluate, but against the likely wide opening range this sizing maximizes expected value.

In this heads-up situation, I opened for 300 on the button with QTo, and Minni 3-bet to 1200 from the big blind. While QTo is not a premium hand, it has reasonable equity and playability in position. Given our stack depths and the fact that we are heads-up, folding here would be too exploitable against an opponent who has shown aggression in previous hands. Calling allows me to see a flop in position with a hand that can flop well, while also keeping the pot manageable.

Heads-up, deep stacks (~190BB) and I 3-bet preflop with TT into a wide button opening range; villain called. Flop is 2s–Qd–8s. TT is an underpair to a queen but still ahead of a large portion of his calling range (many broadways, weaker pairs, suited connectors). This is a relatively dry board with only two spades and one overcard (Q) that he needs to have to dominate me. I want to c-bet for value and to deny equity to overcards and backdoor draws while keeping the pot controllable given deep stacks.

Sizing: pot = 2400. I choose a compact, constructive c-bet (~40% of the pot) to get called by worse hands (weaker pairs, Jx, some draws) and to charge spade/overcard draws without bloating the pot unnecessarily. Bet 1000 gives good fold equity and keeps options open for turn decisions.

Plan: If he folds I take down the pot. If he calls, re-evaluate the turn — barrel a blank (non-Q, non-spade) for value; slow down or check-call if a Q or a spade hits (since those cards help his range more). If he raises large on the flop I will give a lot of credit to Qx/8x/sets and fold TT to big aggression.

I have top pair (Queens) with a Ten kicker on a relatively dry flop of Qd 8s 2s. Minni 3-bet pre-flop and has now led out with a continuation bet of 1000 into a 2400 pot. My hand is too strong to fold, as I beat hands like JJ, TT, and AK/AJ that she might 3-bet pre-flop. However, raising here might force out her bluffs and only get action from hands that have me beaten (AQ, AA, KK, or sets). By calling, I maintain control of the pot, keep her range wide, and use my position to see how the turn develops.

I 3‑bet pre with TT and c‑bet the dry flop (2s–Qd–8s); villain called the flop — his continuing range is wide (Qx, 8x, medium pairs, some two‑spade combos, and weaker broadways). The turn 7c is a blank: it doesn’t complete spade flushes and only meaningfully improves pocket 7s. I’m out of position and deep-stacked, so my priority with an underpair is pot control and to avoid getting blown off the hand or crushed by a turn check‑raise. Checking keeps worse hands and bluffs in, preserves fold equity on later streets, and lets me call reasonable turn bets to reach showdown. Plan: check now. If villain fires a small/medium bet (~<=1/3–1/2 pot, roughly 1.5k–2.2k) I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the river; if he barrels pot‑sized or large I’ll give him credit for Qx/88/set and fold; if he checks back I’ll go to the river with showdown value and choose a small value bet or another check depending on the river card.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker on a board of Qd-8s-2s-7c. Minni 3-bet pre-flop and continuation bet the flop for 1,000. Her check on the turn suggests she might be slowing down with a hand like JJ, TT, or a missed draw, or she could be pot-controlling a stronger hand like AA, KK, or AQ. By checking back, I control the pot size and ensure I get to see a river card. This hand has high showdown value but isn't strong enough to value bet three streets against a 3-betting range, so checking behind here is a solid way to realize my equity.

Board is 2s–Qd–8s–7c–9s, pot 4,400. I 3‑bet pre, c‑bet the flop and checked the turn; Flash called the flop and checked the turn — his continuing range is wide (many Qx, 8x, medium pairs, some two‑spade combos and missed draws). The 9s on the river is a relatively bad card for my tens: it completes possible two‑spade flushes (if he held two spades), gives straight combinations (J‑T types) and pairs/matches that often beat TT. Betting here would be thin value — many hands that call a river bet actually beat me, and there aren’t many worse hands that will reliably call. Checking and going to showdown preserves chips and is the higher‑EV line. (If Flash now bets a small probe I’ll call to see showdown; if he jams huge I’ll give credit and fold.)

I have top pair with a Queen on a board that now contains three spades and several straight possibilities (J-10, 6-5). After Minni 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop, her checks on the turn and river suggest she is either slowing down with a medium-strength hand like JJ or TT, or she has given up on a bluff like AK/AJ. While I might be able to extract a small amount of value from hands like JJ or TT, the risk of a check-raise on this specific board texture (where the flush and straight arrived) outweighs the potential gain. I have solid showdown value and will check back to realize my equity and win the pot against her weaker range.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 8♣3♥: it’s a very weak hand but we have the biggest preflop advantage — position and initiative. The cost to simply see the flop is small (50) but opening to 2x BB (200 total) is standard heads-up strategy to pressure the blind, build pots when we hit, and steal a lot of uncontested pots. With deep stacks I’ll raise to take the lead and play postflop in position; plan is to fold this specific hand to a significant 3-bet and otherwise C‑bet selectively on favorable flops or check/control on dry boards.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc2c. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind (a 2x raise). This is a very small raise, and I am getting 3:1 odds to call (100 to win a total pot of 300). While Q2 suited is a weak hand, it plays reasonably well in position or against wide opening ranges. Given the pot odds and the fact that we are heads-up, folding here would be over-folding. I will call to see a flop and use my stack advantage and position (although I'm technically out of position post-flop, the price is too good to pass up).

I have Queen-high on a board of 7d-8s-Ks. I currently have no pair and very limited drawing equity (backdoor flush/straight). Since Minni has checked, I have the option to check back. Betting into a King-high board with only Queen-high is risky as it is a board that favors the pre-flop raiser's range, and I have very little to fall back on if called. Checking allows me to see the turn for free and potentially improve or catch a bluff later.

I have top pair (8s) with a very weak 3 kicker on a K‑8‑7 rainbow/spade board after I opened preflop and Flash checked. His calling range from the BB is wide (Kx, broadways, small pockets, connectors and some straight draws like 9‑10/6‑9). Betting keeps initiative, gets value from worse hands (77, smaller pairs, some 8x that call), folds many overcards and charges the straight draws. I don’t want to bloßk the pot with a marginal kicker against a check‑raise range, so I’ll use a medium sizing to protect and extract — ~½ pot (200). Plan: if he calls, reevaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously; if he check‑raises big I’ll give him credit for Kx/sets/strong two‑pairs and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc2c. On a board of 7d-8s-Ks, I have Queen-high with a backdoor flush draw. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 200 into a pot of 400. This is a half-pot continuation bet on a board that generally favors the raiser's range (containing Kings and high pairs). My hand is currently weak, having missed the board entirely, and I have very little equity to continue against a bet. While I have a backdoor club draw, the 7 and 8 on the board connect well with many middle-card ranges, and any King or pair from Minni beats me. Calling here would likely lead to me folding on the turn unless I improve significantly. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Standard procedure here is to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. Minni has been active, but AQo is well ahead of her range for calling or defending. I will raise to 300 to maintain pressure and extract value.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with A♥9♣. Heads-up the button’s opening range is very wide, and A9o is a strong defensive hand — it has decent equity and plays well postflop. Pot is 400 and I face 200 to call, so I need ~33% equity to break even; A9o vs a typical wide button range exceeds that.

I’ll call rather than 3-bet: 3-betting OOP with deep stacks will inflate the pot and commit more vs an opponent who can play back aggressively. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me see a flop with a hand that hits a lot of relevant boards (A or 9) and lets me play postflop for value or fold to heavy action. Postflop plan: bet for value when I hit an Ace or pair, check/call or check-fold on dry/unfavorable boards, and apply pressure selectively when I have initiative or good blockers.

I have top pair with a good kicker (A♥9♣) on J♦-7♠-A♣. Against Flash’s wide button opening range I’m ahead of a lot of hands (weaker aces, Jx, Kx/Qx, many pocket pairs), so I should be betting for value and to deny equity. A moderate c-bet of 300 (~half the 600 pot) charges worse aces and jacks, gets called by thinner hands, and keeps the pot controllable OOP with deep stacks. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he raises very large I’ll proceed cautiously since he can have stronger aces or sets.

I have top pair, queen kicker (AQo) on an Ace-high board (Ac-Jd-7s). My opponent, Minni, has led into me (donk-bet) for 300 after calling my pre-flop raise. This lead often represents a marginal hand like a Jack, a seven, or a weak Ace, trying to see where they stand, or a draw like K10. While I could raise for value and protection, calling allows me to keep her bluffs and weaker made hands in the pot while controlling the size of the pot in case she has a monster (like AJ or 77). I will call the 300 to see the turn.

I have A♥9♣ and the turn pairs the ace (J♦-7♠-A♣-A♦) — I now have trips (three aces), which is an extremely strong, usually best hand heads‑up. Flash opened preflop and bet the flop then called my flop raise, so his continuing range contains many Ax, Jx, and medium pocket pairs. The only hands that currently beat me are quads with the remaining A (As) or full houses made from pocket Js or 77s — those combos exist but are rare relative to his overall calling range. I should be extracting value and protecting my hand. A pot‑sized bet (~1200) charges worse aces, Jx, and most pocket pairs while keeping the pot manageable versus the very small number of nut hands. If he calls I re-evaluate on the river; if he raises big, that typically polarizes to quads/full houses and I can fold/reassess depending on sizing. Betting now maximizes value from the hands that will call.

I have Trip Aces with a Queen kicker. The turn Ace (Ad) is an excellent card for me, as it makes it less likely Minni also has an Ace. Minni's pot-sized bet (1200 into 1200) is very aggressive. Given the board texture (Ac Jd 7s Ad), the only hands currently beating me are AJ, A7, JJ, 77, and AK. Minni has shown high aggression in past hands (like Hand #4 and #17), suggesting she could be overvaluing a weaker Ace, a Jack, or even bluffing. If I raise now, I might force out her bluffs or weaker made hands. By calling, I keep her range wide and can extract more value on the river, or catch her if she decides to fire a third barrel as a bluff. My hand is too strong to fold, and calling is the most deceptive and control-oriented play here.

I have A♥9♣ on J♦-7♠-A♣-A♦-T♣ — trips (three aces) on the river. Heads‑up this is an extremely strong hand; the only realistic hands that beat me are full houses/quads (pocket JJ, 77, TT or combos with the one remaining Ace + T/J/7). Those combos are rare relative to Flash’s continuing range. Flash opened preflop and then called my flop and turn bets, which typically contains a lot of Ax, Jx and medium pocket pairs — many of which will call a reasonable river value bet. Checking loses value to worse A’s and Jx and lets him realize equity for free. I want to extract value while keeping the pot from getting unnecessarily huge against the small subset of full houses. Pot is 3600, so I’ll make a modest-to-medium value bet (~55% pot) to get called by worse aces, jacks and pocket pairs but not price myself into an uncomfortable shove. If he check‑raises big (polarized), I’ll give credit to a full house/quads and fold; I’ll call smaller raises depending on sizing because trips are still far ahead of most of his range.

On a board of Jd-7s-Ac-Ad-Tc, I have Trip Aces with a Queen kicker (AQ). My opponent, Minni, has led into me on every street (donk betting), which is a line that can represent high-variance strength or a polarized range. While the river Ten (Tc) completes a potential straight for KQ and makes full houses possible for hands like AJ, AT, A7, JJ, 77, or TT, my hand is still exceptionally strong in a heads-up context. Minni has shown a tendency for high aggression and overvaluing hands in previous pots (notably Hand #17). Given the pot odds (calling 2000 to win a total pot of 7600), I only need to be correct about 21% of the time. AQ beats all of her bluffs and any weaker Ace (A9-A2) she might be value betting. A raise is risky as it likely only gets called by hands that beat me, so calling is the most profitable and safest way to reach showdown.

Heads-up on the button with J♦3♥ — a marginal holding but absolutely playable in this spot. Pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 to complete, but raising to the minimum (200 total) seizes the initiative, uses position, and forces weaker big-blind hands to fold while keeping the pot small relative to our deep stack. The raise is cheap (≈1.4% of my stack) and gives me fold equity and the opportunity to c-bet many flops. If Flash responds with a large 3-bet I’ll give extra respect to that and fold this hand; if he just calls I’ll proceed with standard continuation-bet lines. Raise to 200 (min-raise).

In a heads-up match, defending the Big Blind with a suited one-gapper like 6h4h against a minimum raise is a standard play. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to see a flop, and the hand has decent playability and potential to realize its equity. I will call the 100 additional chips to see the flop.

I have bottom pair with a 6 on a board featuring two high cards (K, Q). In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. Checking is the standard play here to see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts. My hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable, so I want to keep the pot small.

I was the preflop raiser with position and Flash checked the K♦ Q♠ 6♦ flop. I have J♦3♥ — no pair, no diamond, almost no showdown value — so I should be using my initiative to take the pot now rather than checking and realizing very poor equity at showdown. Flash’s check will include a lot of missed hands and medium-strength holdings (small pairs, Qx without a diamond, underpairs, and floats/draws), so a modest c-bet will pick up many of those hands while remaining inexpensive when called. I size to ~150 into 400 (~37.5% pot): big enough to force folds from many hands and to price some marginal calls correctly, but small enough to keep the pot manageable given my weak hand and the two-diamond texture (I’ll fold to a substantial check-raise). Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I re-evaluate on the turn and barrel only on favorable cards or controlled spots — fold to significant aggression.

I have bottom pair with a 6 on a board with two overcards (K, Q). Minni's continuation bet is relatively small (under half pot). In a heads-up situation, bottom pair is often strong enough to call one street, especially when getting nearly 4.7-to-1 on the call. While my kicker is weak, I want to see a turn card to see if I improve or if the action slows down.

On the turn, the Four of Spades has improved my hand to two pair (Sixes and Fours). While this is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation, the board remains coordinated with two high overcards (King and Queen). I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see if she continues her betting lead. This allows me to keep her bluffing range in and protects my hand while seeing how she reacts to the turn card. If she bets, I have a very strong candidate for a call or a check-raise depending on her sizing.

I was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop; Flash called. Turn is 4♠ — a blank relative to the flop (Kd Qs 6d 4s). I hold J♦3♥: no pair and only a backdoor diamond that needs both turn+river diamonds to make a flush (very unlikely). With pot = 700 and Flash checking to me, this is a clear pot‑control spot. Betting here as a bluff is thin — many hands in his calling range (Kx, Qx, small pairs, some diamonds) will call a turn bet and put me in difficult river decisions or check‑raise. I’ll check back, keep the pot manageable, and look to bluff or value on a favorable river (a diamond or a safe low card); fold to meaningful aggression.

On the river, the board is Kd Qs 6d 4s 8c. I hold 4h 6h, giving me two pair (sixes and fours). The action was: Minni raised pre-flop, I called; I check-called a small bet on the flop; then we both checked the turn. Minni's check on the turn suggests she might not have a King or better, or is pot-controlling with a marginal hand like a Queen. With two pair, I likely have the best hand. I will lead out with a value bet of 500 into the 700 pot to extract value from a Queen or a stubborn middle pair, as checking might lead to a check-back and lost value.

I’m on the button with J♦3♥. Final board K♦ Q♠ 6♦ 4♠ 8♣ and Flash leads out for 500 into a 700 pot (pot shown 1200; I must call 500). This is a pure river decision — I have no pair and no realistic way to improve. Calling 500 to win 1700 requires ≈29.4% equity. Flash’s line (call preflop, call my small c-bet on the two-diamond flop, check the turn, then bet the river) is much more consistent with value (Kx, Qx, 6x, small pairs) than frequent bluffs. Only pure bluffs or extremely thin river float-calls will fold to a call; my J-high is behind almost all of his value range. Raising as a bluff is too thin (I don’t block his major value combos and he’s deep enough to call or shove). Per my turn plan — “fold to meaningful aggression” — this river bet is meaningful aggression. Fold and preserve stack for better spots.

In heads-up play from the small blind/button, I have a very weak hand (2d 4s). While it is tempting to raise or call with almost any two cards to keep up pressure, 4-2 offsuit is near the bottom of the range. Given the stack sizes and the relative aggression of Minni, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot to engage.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range. 6♣3♦ is weak, but raising to the minimum is standard here: it applies pressure, generates fold equity and keeps the initiative while preserving my positional advantage postflop. Limping gives Flash cheap odds and invites a live call or squeeze; a small raise to 200 either wins the pot now or lets me see a flop in position against a wider defending range. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold this marginal holding and protect my 14.3k stack. (Amount of 200 is my total commitment including the 50 SB already posted.)

I am in the Big Blind with Kc9s. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 200 (a min-raise). I need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 400. K9o is a solid defending hand in a heads-up situation against a wide button opening range. Given the pot odds (3:1) and the strength of my hand relative to a typical opening range, calling is a standard and profitable play here.

On a very wet board of Tc8c9c, I have hit second pair with the 9s and also hold the Kc, giving me the Nut Flush Draw. This is a very strong semi-bluffing or catching hand. However, this texture is also extremely dangerous as it contains many straights (any 7, any J, QJ) and completed flushes. Since I am out of position and the pot is small (400), checking to the aggressor is the standard play to see how they react to this coordinated board. If Minni bets, I will likely call or raise depending on the sizing, given my pair and the nut flush draw.

Flop: Tc 8c 9c. I hold 6h3d — no club, no pair. This is a very bad flop for my raising range because the BB’s defending range contains lots of clubs, high cards and T/9/8 combos that either have a made hand or very strong draws. My only realistic clean outs are the four 7s (makes 6-7-8-9-10), giving ~16% to hit by river; pairing a 6 or 3 is almost never good enough. A c-bet here would have thin fold equity and will mostly get called by hands that beat me, putting me in tough spots out of position post-bet. I have position and a healthy stack, so I should keep the pot small, give myself a free look at the turn, and fold to any meaningful aggression. Action: check (will fold to a clear lead; if Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn).

On the turn, the Ace of Hearts is a card that theoretically favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, but Minni's check on the flop suggests she likely doesn't have a made hand or a strong draw that she wanted to protect. I have second pair (nines) and the nut flush draw (King of clubs). Given I am out of position and have significant showdown value plus massive draw equity, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to see a river for free if she checks back, or to evaluate her bet sizing if she decides to lead out now that the Ace has arrived. I don't want to turn my hand into a bluff here, as it still has a lot of value against her checking range.

Board is Tc 8c 9c Ah, my hand 6h3d. I have no pair, no club, and only four 7s as straight outs (~4/46 ≈ 8.7% to hit the river). Flash called preflop and checked both flop and turn, so his range is heavy in clubs, T/9/8 combos and broadway/Ax hands — many of which already beat me or have good showdown value. Betting here has very poor fold equity and will mostly get called by better hands, putting me in a losing spot out of position. With position, a healthy stack and such weak equity, I should keep the pot small and see the river for free. I will check and be prepared to fold to meaningful aggression; if Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the river (look for a 7 or a favorable bluffing opportunity).

The river 6c completes a 4-flush on the board. I hold the Kc, which gives me a King-high flush. The board texture is Tc-8c-9c-Ah-6c. While the Ac would make a nut flush and the 7c would make a straight flush, it is highly unlikely Minni holds these given the passive action. She checked both the flop (with a potential nut flush draw) and the turn (where the Ace appeared). I suspect she has a weaker flush, an Ace, or is simply giving up. I will bet 300 to extract value from those hands in a small pot.

I hit a very weak showdown hand (6h3d makes bottom pair on the river: Tc 8c 9c Ah 6c). Pot is 700 and Flash just bet 300; calling 300 to win 1000 requires ~30% equity. Flash checked flop and turn and now fires a small river bet — that line is consistent with a lot of value hands (any club for a flush, trips: TT/99/88, two-pair combos like A–T/A–9/A–8, or thin value/ blocker bets) and only a smaller portion of pure bluffs. My pair of sixes only beats bluffs and a few worse hands, and it’s unlikely I have the ~30% equity vs Flash’s realistic river-betting range. With a healthy stack and many hands left to play, preserving chips is correct here. I fold.

I am on the button with 9-6 offsuit. This is a marginal hand, but in a heads-up situation, opening or calling from the small blind is common practice to apply pressure or see a flop. However, Minni has been playing fairly proactively. Given the 2:1 pot odds to call (50 more to win a pot of 150), I will call and see a flop, keeping the pot small with a medium-strength starting hand.

Flash (button) just limped, showing a very wide, speculative range. I have J♠2♥ — a weak hand but in heads-up it has some showdown value and blockers to Jx. Min-raising to 200 is cheap (I add 100 into a 200 pot) and will often pick up the pot or isolate a single opponent rather than letting him see a free flop. If Flash folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously out of position — c-bet on dry/face-card flops and check/defend on coordinated or draw-heavy boards. If he 3-bets or applies significant aggression I’ll fold this holding. Small, standard isolation raise to take the initiative and punish limps.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 9-6 offsuit. Minni, in the Big Blind, has raised to 200 after my initial limp. The pot is currently 300, and it costs me 100 more to call. I am getting 3:1 direct pot odds. While 9-6 offsuit is a weak hand, in heads-up play, it's often worth seeing a flop with these odds, especially with the positional advantage for the rest of the hand. However, Minni has been quite aggressive in 3-bet pots. Since I already limped, calling the extra 100 is a standard defense to keep the range wide and utilize position.

Heads-up vs a limp-caller — Flash’s preflop range is very wide and often misses this K♠ 8♣ 7♦ flop. I was the preflop aggressor, so a continuation bet is standard: this board is K-high and relatively dry, which advantages my raising range and gives me good fold equity. My J♠2♥ has virtually no showdown value or backdoor equity, so I should try to take the pot now with a controlled c-bet rather than check and give free cards. I’ll bet half the pot (200 into 400). If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and fold to significant aggression unless I improve.

On a flop of 8c-Ks-7d, I have an open-ended straight draw with my 9-6. Minni has led out for 200 into a 400 pot. I am getting 3:1 odds to call, and with 8 outs to a straight, my equity is approximately 32% over the next two cards. Being in position, I can comfortably call to see the turn. If I hit, I have good implied odds against a King-X or a high pair in Minni's range. If she checks the turn, I can also consider a semi-bluff or taking a free card.

Check. I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the K‑8‑7 flop, but J♠2♥ has no pair, no real draw and virtually no equity. Flash’s call on the flop represents a wide but often-calling range (Kx, pocket pairs, broadways, speculative hands) that is now largely improved by the turn A♥ (Ax hands now have a pair, and many Kx/pair hands will prefer to see a river rather than fold). Out of position I want pot control — betting here would be a thin bluff with poor blockers and a high chance of being called or raised. I’ll check to avoid investing more with air; plan to fold to significant turn aggression and only entertain a very small probe bet, otherwise check back and reassess on the river.

The turn card is an Ace, which significantly changes the board texture. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor and flop bettor, has checked. I have an open-ended straight draw (9-6 on a board of 8-K-7-A). While the Ace is a good card to represent as a bluff, checking behind allows me to take a free card to hit my straight and avoids the risk of being check-raised off my equity by a sticky King or a slow-played Ace. I will check to see the river.

Check. I have J♠2♥ — no pair, no meaningful river improvement and very little showdown value. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the K‑8‑7 flop, but checked the Ace on the turn; Flash’s line (calling flop, checking turn and river) is passive and heavily weighted toward hands with real showdown value (Kx, pocket pairs, A‑x, or now Qx) or a speculative caller that’s not folding to a river bluff. A river bluff here is low EV: he called the flop and is unlikely to fold many hands that beat J‑high. With poor blockers and being out of position, pot control and taking the showdown cheaply is the best long‑term play — check and only continue if he makes a very small probe I deem beatable (otherwise fold to aggression).

The river has been checked to me. I have 9-high, having missed my open-ended straight draw (9-6 on 8-K-7-A-Q). Minni has checked the turn and the river after leading the flop. While her checks suggest weakness, 9-high has zero showdown value. However, the board contains an Ace, King, and Queen, which are better for the pre-flop aggressor's range. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and led the flop, her checking the turn and river could be a 'give up' with a hand that missed, or a weak showdown hand like a pair of 7s or 8s. In a small pot of 800, a bluff is risky as she might call with any pair given the board texture. I will check back and concede the pot, as I have no value and don't believe a bluff will be profitable enough here against her likely holding.

Heads-up on the button with J♥3♠ — weak but playable in this spot. In heads-up play I want to seize the initiative and use position postflop, so I’ll open-raise a wide range. A min open to 200 (total) is standard: it builds the pot, creates fold equity against a wide BB defending range, and lets me play the flop last. If Flash folds I take the pot; if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and exercise pot-control or fold to heavy resistance when I miss. I’ll re-evaluate if he 3-bets (likely fold to a large 3-bet given this holding).

I hold Ace-King offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand. Minni has opened to 200. I will 3-bet to 600 to build the pot and take the initiative, as AK is way ahead of a typical button opening range.

I opened wide to 200 on the button with J♥3♠, but Flash responded with a 3-bet to 600. The pot is 800 and it costs me 400 to call, so I’d need roughly 33% equity to make a call profitable. J3o is badly dominated by a typical 3-bet range (pocket pairs, broadways, suited aces) and won’t have that equity even with position. 4-betting would be an expensive bluff (min to 1000) and is poorly constructed with this holding. With deep stacks and many hands left, preserving chips and folding to this 3-bet is the correct, long-term profitable choice.

I am on the button with 5s4s, a suited connector that plays well in heads-up situations. I have a significant chip lead over Minni. Raising here is a standard play to apply pressure and take control of the hand pre-flop. A 3x open to 300 is a consistent sizing for this position.

Heads-up, Flash opened to 300 from the button and I’m on the BB with 4♦2♦. Calling costs 200 into a 400 pot (pot odds ~3:1, break-even ~25% equity). 4‑2s is a weak hand but suited and has reasonable equity vs a wide heads‑up opening range, and with deep stacks I can realize equity postflop. I’m out of position, so I won’t 3‑bet a marginal holding; a raise would require a much stronger or balanced 3‑betting range. Plan: call to see a flop, fold to a large 3‑bet preflop, and proceed cautiously OOP — continue only with clear draws or a pair, otherwise control pot size.

Check. I have 4♦2♦ on 3♦ 2♠ 5♦ — bottom pair (pair of deuces) plus a very strong 4-card diamond draw and a gutshot to a 6. Counting unique outs: 9 diamonds + 3 non-diamond sixes + 2 remaining deuces = 14 outs (~30% to hit the turn, ~51% to hit by the river). I’m out of position and the pot is small relative to stacks, so I want pot control and to realize equity rather than bloating the pot with a marginal made hand. Checking lets me call a normal c-bet with good equity, induce bluffs, and avoid committing chips to big aggression OOP — if Flash bets small/medium I’ll call; if they polarize with a large raise I’ll re-evaluate (often folding).

On this flop, I have top pair (fives) and an open-ended straight draw (A-2-3-4-5 or 2-3-4-5-6). This is a very strong position. Minni checked, and I want to bet for value against her draws, smaller pairs, or overcards, while also building the pot for my strong equity. A bet of 300 (half-pot) is a standard and effective sizing here.

Call. I have a pair of deuces plus a very strong 4-card diamond draw (4♦2♦ on 3♦2♠5♦) and a backdoor straight possibility. Unique outs ≈ 9 diamonds + 3 non-diamond sixes + 2 remaining deuces = 14 outs (~30% to hit the turn, ~51% to hit by the river). The call is 300 into a pot that will be ~1200, so pot odds (~4:1) require only ~20% equity — well below my hand’s equity. Out of position I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than inflate it with an OOP raise; calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in and lets me fold to extreme polarization on later streets. I’ll call the 300, then re-evaluate on the turn (continue with made/improved hands or the nut diamond, fold to large, polarized aggression if I miss).

I will check. I have 4♦2♦ on 3♦-2♠-5♦-7♣: a small made hand (pair of deuces) plus a very strong 4-card diamond draw and a gutshot to a 6. From the turn to the river I have roughly 14 clean outs (9 diamonds + 3 non-diamond sixes + 2 deuces) ≈ 14/46 ≈ 30% to improve. Pot is only 1,200 and stacks are deep, so betting OOP here is unnecessary and often wrong — I’m the drawing hand and want to realize equity cheaply rather than bloating the pot where a check‑raise or a call by dominating hands will punish me. Checking keeps worse hands and bluffs in, preserves implied odds if I hit, and lets me call reasonable river bets (I'll call small/medium sizes given ~30% equity and fold to large, highly polarized overbets). If the river completes my diamond or trips/boat I can extract value; if I miss I will fold to heavy aggression. For now: check and re-evaluate on the river.

I have top pair (5s) with an open-ended straight draw (any 4 or 6 makes a straight). The 7c on the turn is a relatively safe card that doesn't change much except for potentially giving Minni a straight draw with 6-4 or 8-6, or improving a hand like 7-5. Minni checked again, which signals weakness or a desire to control the pot. I want to continue betting for value from her lower pairs, gutshots, or ace-high hands that might call, and to protect my hand against overcards. A bet of around 600 (half the pot) is appropriate to maintain pressure and build the pot in case I hit my straight on the river.

Call. I have 4♦2♦ on 3♦-2♠-5♦-7♣: a small made hand (pair of deuces) plus a very strong 4-card diamond draw and a backdoor straight (outs: 9 diamonds + 3 non-diamond sixes + 2 remaining deuces = 14 unique outs). From the turn to the river that’s roughly 14/46 ≈ 30% equity. The pot is 1800 and Flash’s bet is 600, so calling 600 gives me ~25% break-even equity (600/(1800+600)=25%). My hand’s equity (~30%) > required pot odds, so a call is +EV. I’m OOP and stacks are deep (I have 13,750), so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a check-raise or a large raise as the drawing hand — calling realizes equity cheaply, keeps bluffs and worse hands in, and preserves implied odds if I hit. Plan: call the 600 to see the river; if I hit the diamond or trips/boat I can extract value, if I miss I’ll fold to large polarized overbets and call smaller river bets depending on sizing.

Final board 3♦-2♠-5♦-7♣-3♣. My hand 4♦2♦ is just a pair of deuces (my diamond flush missed). Flash opened, c-bet the flop and barreled the turn — that line is weighted toward value (3x, 5x, overpairs) though it can include some bluffs. He checked the river, which is ambiguous (pot-control with a medium hand, missed bluffs, or a trap). Against that line my pair is marginal and often behind his value combos, but it still has showdown value versus missed broadways/air. Betting now would be a thin probe with low fold equity and poor blockers; I risk being raised by hands that already beat me and won’t get enough calls from worse. The prudent, +EV play is to check and take it to showdown. If Flash leads into me I’ll call small/medium bets given the pot odds and fold to large polarized overbets.

On the river, the board has paired the 3. I have two pair, 5s and 3s. Minni has checked to me for the third time. While my hand is relatively strong in a heads-up pot, the 3 on the river is a card that could have easily improved her range if she was calling with a marginal hand or a draw that included a 3. Additionally, a straight (A-4 or 6-4) is possible. Since I have decent showdown value and Minni's calling range on the flop and turn suggests she has something, I don't want to risk a check-raise or value bet into a hand that has me beaten. Checking back allows me to see the showdown and take the pot if my two pair is good, without risking more chips against a potential trap or a better hand.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. 8♣2♣ is a weak hand but suited and playable in position — raising takes the initiative, gives fold equity, and lets me play postflop in position if called. The pot is 150 and calling 50 (limping) surrenders initiative; I’ll open to 3x the BB to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. Plan: if Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll proceed to c-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value or fold when clearly behind.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten of spades, a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. Minni, who has been active and aggressive, opened to 300 from the Button. To build the pot and take control of the hand, I will 3-bet to 1000. This is a standard value raise that also allows me to take the pot down immediately if Minni is opening light.

I opened to 300 with 8♣2♣ as planned, but Flash 3-bet to 1000 — a sizable re-raise that largely represents a stronger value-heavy range. The pot is 1300 and calling 700 would make it 2000, so I need roughly 35% equity to break even. 8♣2♣ has poor equity and is frequently dominated vs a standard 3-bet range; 4-betting as a bluff is too risky with such a rag and would bloat the pot deep-stacked. Consistent with my pre-flop plan to fold big 3-bets, I’ll fold and wait for a better spot in position.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with Jack-Nine offsuit. This is a solid opening hand for the small blind. I want to put pressure on the big blind and seize control of the pot early. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button while I’m in the big blind with Q♥2♠. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more. Q2o is a very weak hand here — frequently dominated by the button’s opening range and it plays poorly out of position on deep stacks. The call requires ~25% equity, but Q2o’s real equity vs a reasonable button range is typically lower and it rarely realizes that equity OOP. A min‑raise as a bluff is thin (bad blockers, large SPR), so folding and preserving chips for a better spot is the best play.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) K4s is a playable, aggressive open-raise hand. I want to seize the initiative, deny the big blind free equity, and build the pot with a hand that has decent postflop playability (suited king). With deep stacks (≈127 BB) I can continue on favourable flops or fold to heavy 3-bets; a standard 2x BB open to 200 is appropriate here. Raising also leverages fold equity against Flash’s likely wide calling/raising range from the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 82o, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 2x (200). Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, this hand has extremely poor playability out of position and is easily dominated. Given that I have a significant stack lead and there is no need to defend with the bottom of my range, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 3-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Even in a heads-up situation from the button, there is very little value in playing this hand against Minni, especially since the hand doesn't scale well post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with A♠Q♦ — a strong heads-up holding and I have positional advantage postflop. Calling 50 (to 100 total) is too passive; I want to isolate the BB, build the pot, and charge his wide calling range. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3×BB). That size extracts value from worse Ax/Qx and many broadways/draws, but doesn’t overcommit me if I face a large 3‑bet. If he calls I’ll c‑bet frequently on favourable flops and play for position; if he 3‑bets to a standard size I’ll mostly call and evaluate postflop, folding to an extremely large shove that prices me out.

I am holding King-Eight suited in the Big Blind. In a heads-up situation, this is a very strong hand. Minni opened for 300, and I need to call 200 more. Given my stack advantage and the strength of the hand, calling to see a flop is a standard play. Raising is an option, but calling keeps her wide range in and allows me to play a pot in position or check-call/check-raise based on the flop texture.

On a paired board of 9-4-9, I hold King-high. I am out of position in the Big Blind. King-high has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value. Checking is the standard play here to see how Minni reacts and to potentially catch bluffs or realize my equity for free.

Heads‑up I opened preflop with A♠Q♦ and Villain (BB) just called. Flop 9♥ 4♠ 9♣ is very dry and misses a large part of the caller’s range. Flash checked, showing weakness. My range advantage and the board texture make this a great spot to c‑bet for fold equity and to charge weaker Kx/Qx and underpairs that will call. I’ll size up — ~2/3 pot — to apply real pressure but still leave room to fold to a big raise. If he folds I take down the 600. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (bet for value on A/Q turns, check and control on blanks, and fold to heavy aggression since A‑high has poor showdown value against strong action). Betting 400 balances fold equity and protection vs the few hands that have me crushed.

On a paired board of 9-4-9, my King-high has limited showdown value and no direct draws. Minni's bet of 400 into a 600 pot (2/3 pot) is a polar sizing for such a dry board texture, often indicating either a strong hand or a dedicated bluff. Being out of position with only King-high and no backdoor flush potential, calling becomes marginal. Given my significant stack advantage, I will choose to fold and wait for a more favorable situation rather than calling down or attempting a high-variance bluff on this texture.

In the Small Blind with an extremely weak hand like 8s2c, and facing only a small call to complete, I must consider the cost-benefit. Although it's only 50 more chips to see a flop, 82o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni has been playing fairly aggressively in the Big Blind. Folding here preserves my stack and avoids being out of position in a pot with a very poor hand.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with AdTh — a strong, positionally advantaged hand. Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to just call. Against a wide BB defending range I want initiative and to play postflop in position, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp. Raising to 300 (3x BB): (1) builds the pot with a hand that performs well heads-up, (2) prices out some of the widest parts of his range, and (3) gives me flexible c-betting lines on many flops given my 132BB effective stack. A min-raise to 200 is too passive and calling surrenders initiative. If he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and stack depths, but the correct first action is to raise to 300 (total commitment — I add 250 more chips).

I am holding Pocket Nines (99), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni opened with a standard 3x raise from the button. To maximize value and gain initiative, I will 3-bet to 1000. This puts pressure on her wider opening range and allows me to define the hand strength early.

Heads-up on the button with A♦T♥, I opened to 300 and Flash 3-bet to 1000. Pot is 1300 and it costs me 700 to call (bringing my total committed to 1000). Stacks are deep (~13k effective for me), so I prefer to keep the hand and play postflop in position.
