Calling gives me roughly 35% break-even equity (700/(1300+700)=35%); ATo has reasonable equity vs a wide BB 3-bet range and it also blocks some strong Ax/AK combos. A small 4-bet to 1700 is too passive/polarized and a large 4-bet would bloat the pot with a non-premium offsuit ace. I'll call to realize equity and use my positional advantage — plan to c-bet favorable A/T/dry flops, control pot on connected boards, and fold to extreme aggression if necessary.

I have flopped top set (Nines) on a board of 9s-5s-2c. This is an extremely strong position. While there is a flush draw present, I want to start building the pot immediately to extract value from Minni's potential range (overpairs like TT/JJ/QQ, flush draws, or a Nine with a better kicker). I will lead with a standard continuation bet of 1000, which is half the pot, to maintain initiative and set up for larger bets on later streets.

Flash 3‑bet preflop so his range is weighted toward pairs and strong Ax, but his small c‑bet (1,000 into a ~2,000 pot) is a fairly wide-frequency continuation bet. The pot is 3,000 and calling 1,000 requires ~25% equity (1,000/(3,000+1,000) = 25%) — with A♦T♥ in position I have reasonable showdown equity vs a continuing 3‑bet range and I also block many A‑combos. With deep stacks and positional advantage I want to realize equity and keep pot control rather than bloat the pot with a marginal hand by raising. Folding here surrenders too much +EV equity given the price. Plan: call the 1,000 (total committed becomes 2,000), see how he turns the hand, and only apply aggression if I improve (pair or better) or can credibly bluff on a later street; fold or strongly re‑evaluate if he shows heavy strength.

I have a set of nines on a board of 5s-9s-2c-Qs. The Queen of spades is a potentially dangerous card as it completes a flush draw. However, a set is still an extremely strong hand in heads-up play. I will bet 2000 into the 4000 pot to continue extracting value from Minni's likely range, which includes top pair (AQ, KQ), overpairs (JJ, TT), or a high spade draw. If she has the flush, I still have 10 outs (three 5s, three 2s, three Qs, and one 9) to improve to a full house or quads on the river.

Fold. Pot is 6000 and it costs me 2000 to continue, so I need ~25% equity to justify a call (2000/(6000+2000)=25%). On this Q♠ turn my A♦T♥ has only clean outs: three Aces + three Tens = 6 outs, i.e. ~6/46 ≈ 13% to improve to a pair on the river — well below the break‑even threshold. More importantly Flash 3‑bet preflop, bet the flop and then turned up the aggression again on the Q♠ turn. That line is heavily weighted toward hands that currently beat me or remain ahead if I hit: two‑spade combos that already have a flush, Qx (now top pair), sets (5s/9s/2c), and strong pairs/AK/AQ. Even when I pair an Ace or Ten many of those hands still beat me (flushes, sets, two pair), so my raw “outs” are often non‑clean. I do block some Ax combos by holding the A♦, but not enough to overcome the poor pot odds and the line strength. Raising here would irresponsibly bloat the pot with a marginal hand and is exploitable. Fold and conserve my 11.25k stack for better, more profitable spots.

In a heads-up situation from the small blind/button, Q8o is a strong enough hand to raise for value and to seize initiative, as it's well above the average holding. A standard open-raise to 300 should put pressure on the big blind and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

Heads-up vs an SB open to 300. I have 8h3h (suited, low connector) and stacks are deep (~111bb). The pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to defend. In heads-up the opener's range is very wide and 8-3s has real postflop playability (flush + straight potential) and decent implied odds when it connects. Calling gives me good immediate pot odds (200 to win 600 → ~3:1, need ~25% equity), lets me see a flop cheaply, and keeps weaker hands and bluffs in. A min 3-bet to 500 is too small to generate meaningful fold equity and a larger 3-bet would be committing too much OOP with a speculative hand. Plan: call, play the flop cautiously — continue when I pick up hearts or strong equity, fold to heavy aggression when missed, and extract value when I hit. Therefore I will call the 200.

I have 8h3h on Kd Td Ac — no pair, no live straight, and only a backdoor heart flush that needs both turn+river (very low equity). The opener (Flash) raised preflop so his range is weighted toward A/K/T/broadways which this flop hits hard. With deep stacks I don’t want to bluff OOP into a range that connects. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity. Plan: fold to any substantial c-bet; consider calling only very small bets (≈≤1/3 pot ≈200) to see a cheap turn, but I will not raise as a bluff here.

On this highly coordinated broadway board (Kd-Td-Ac), I have a gutshot straight draw with my Queen. While I have the pre-flop initiative, this board texture hits a lot of the Big Blind's calling range (any Ace, King, Ten, or Jack). Since Minni checked, I will check back to take a free card and attempt to realize my equity without bloatng the pot, as a continuation bet is unlikely to generate many folds on such a wet board.

Check. With 8h3h on Kd Td Ac Th I have no pair and my backdoor heart draw is dead — with one card to come I cannot make a five‑card heart flush. I only have 4 clean outs (8s/8x/3s/3x) ≈ 4/46 ≈ 8.7% to pair on the river, so this hand has essentially no showdown value. Flash was the preflop raiser and checked the flop and turn; that line can be either pot control with a strong holding or a simple missed hand that will call a bluff. Betting OOP here is a low EV play — a half‑pot bluff would need >33% fold equity to be profitable and I don’t trust that against a preflop raiser who sees me check. I’ll check to keep the pot small, realize my tiny equity, and re‑evaluate on the river: call very small bets (≈≤1/3 pot ≈200) to see the river, fold to meaningful aggression, and only raise with a real made hand.

The board is now Kd-Td-Ac-Th. I have Queen high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). Minni has checked twice. This board is extremely dangerous as it contains three Broadway cards and a pair of Tens. Many of Minni's calling ranges pre-flop (Ax, Kx, QJ, Q9, diamonds) have either improved to trips, two pair, or a straight, or have significant showdown value. Given the pot size is 600 and the board is very wet, I will check behind to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity or evaluate on the final street. Betting into a paired board where the opponent has shown no interest yet might induce a check-raise if she's trapping with a Ten or an Ace.

I check. I hold 8h3h and missed completely — the river Qd doesn't help me and this hand has essentially zero showdown value now. The board Kd Td Ac Th Qd is heavily weighted toward the preflop raiser’s value range (A/K/T/Q/J combinations and straight/paired hands) and a small bluff here would need significant fold equity to be +EV (break‑even fold rate is ~25% for a 200 bet and ~33% for a 300 bet). Flash checked flop and turn, which can be either slowplay or pot control with a real hand, so I don’t expect enough folds from a wide portion of his range to make a bluff profitable. Betting risks needless chips from my deep stack with little chance of success; checking preserves chips and lets me fold only to a bet (or call a very small bet if I judged it cheaply). Therefore I check to take the showdown line without further investment.

On this highly coordinated board of Kd-Td-Ac-Th-Qd, the best five-card hand available using the board is Two Pair (Queens and Tens with an Ace kicker). My holding of Qh does not improve this. Any player with an Ace, King, or Jack (for a straight) beats the board. Since Minni has checked three times, it is possible she is weak, but there is no value in betting as she will likely call with any Ace or King and fold anything I already beat (or split with). Checking back to realize my showdown value or split the pot is the most prudent play.

Heads-up on the button (I posted SB) with Qc7c — a reasonably strong, suited queen with good postflop playability and blocker value. I want initiative and to isolate the big blind, so I’ll raise to 200 (standard 2x-ish open). This sizing applies pressure, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, and preserves position postflop. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call or flat small 3-bets to see a flop because of suit and queen-high, C-bet on most dry boards and proceed cautiously on coordinated or multi-way textures.

With 6s 7h in the Big Blind, I am facing a minimum raise (2x) from Minni on the Button. The pot odds are 3:1, requiring only 100 more chips to call a 300 chip pot. While 6-7 offsuit is a marginal hand, it has some connectivity and the price is right to see a flop in a heads-up situation.

I have a gutshot straight draw (requiring a 4) and two overcards on a very low board. As the big blind who called a pre-flop raise, checking to the aggressor is the standard play to see how they interact with this texture. I will decide my next move based on Minni's betting sizing.

Heads-up on a very dry 5h-3d-2c flop after I opened preflop. Qc7c has queen-high plus a backdoor club and a useful queen-blocker to top-pair combos. Flash checked — their range is wide and misses this board a lot, so I should seize the initiative with a small continuation bet to fold out unpaired overcards and small pairs and to deny free cards. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (150 into 400). If I get a sizable raise I’ll fold; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue only when I pick up meaningful equity (a queen, a club, or a pair/clean improvement).

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4) and two overcards (6 and 7) on a low, disconnected board. Minni's continuation bet of 150 into a 400-chip pot is quite small, giving me excellent pot odds (roughly 3.7:1) to see the turn. Calling is the most standard play here, as it allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the turn without over-committing to a marginal hand.

I am first to act on the turn. My hand remains a gutshot straight draw (needing any four) and the 8h is a relatively neutral card for both ranges. Since I have been playing a check-call line to realize equity and Minni was the aggressor on the previous streets, checking is the standard play to see how she proceeds.

I c-bet the very dry 5♣-3♦-2♣ flop (150 into ~400) and was called. The turn 8♥ doesn’t improve Q♣7♣ — I have no pair and the backdoor club route is effectively dead with only the river left (roughly 6 outs, ~13% to improve). Flash’s check is ambiguous but not a strong reason to barrel a second time with so little equity. Pot control is correct: don’t invest more as a bluff without real equity or strong blockers. I’ll check and give up to a turn bet; if Flash checks back I get a free river to re-evaluate or attempt a small river bluff, otherwise I fold to aggression.

I have 7-high and have missed my gutshot straight draw. The board is 5-3-2-8-K. Minni checked back on the turn, which suggests she has some marginal showdown value like a small pair or Ace-high, or she's giving up on a bluff. Since I have no showdown value, my only way to win is to bet, but the King on the river is a card that often hits a pre-flop raiser's range, making a bluff less likely to succeed if she has any piece of the board or even Ace-high. Given the small pot and the risk of being called by her marginal made hands, checking is the most prudent action.

Heads-up, pot is 700 and I hold Qc7c — queen-high with almost no showdown value. I opened pre, c-bet the very dry 5♥-3♦-2♣ flop for 150 and was called; both checked the turn and villain checked the river K♥. Flash’s line (check-call flop, check turn, check river) is consistent with lots of missed overcards and medium/small pairs rather than a strong value-heavy line. My Qc also blocks some Qx value combos, which helps my bluff equity. A small-to-medium river bluff is appropriate here to pick up the pot against a wide checking range. I’ll size to ~40% pot to exert pressure but keep risk controlled — at 300 the opponent needs to fold >30% for the bluff to be +EV. If I face a raise I’ll concede; if called I’ll lose the showdown.

I have 7-high and have missed my gutshot straight draw on a board of 5-3-2-8-K. Minni checked the turn but has now bet 300 on the river. My hand has no showdown value whatsoever, and while the King is a scare card, Minni's line of betting the flop, checking the turn, and betting the river often suggests a marginal made hand or Ace-high looking for thin value. Since I cannot win at showdown and a bluff raise is too risky given the board texture and previous action, I will fold.

I have been dealt pocket Aces (Ac Ad), the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My objective is to build a large pot and extract maximum value from my opponent. I am on the button and will initiate the action with a standard raise to 300 chips.

I’m in the big blind with 8♥5♠ facing a 3BB open to 300 from the button. This is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand and I’m out of position, so 3-betting OOP would be a mistake — I don’t have blockers or enough equity to force folds and I don’t want to build a big pot with junk. However, heads-up opening ranges are wide and the price is reasonable: calling 200 into a 400 pot requires roughly ~33% equity, and with deep stacks (I have 112BB, opponent ~284BB) there’s implied odds to realize equity on favorable flops. Plan: call to defend the blind, then play the flop cautiously — fold to strong continuation/pressure if I miss, continue only with a pair or a good draw, and fold to large 4-bets preflop.

Check. I hold 8♥5♠ on 6♥-5♣-Q♥: a marginal middle pair (pair of fives) with a weak backdoor heart equity. I’m out of position versus a wide, deep opener (Flash ~284BB) so betting OOP will often get called by better hands (Qx, Kx, Ax, draws) and will unnecessarily bloat the pot. Checking controls the pot, preserves stack, and lets me realize showdown value. Plan: check-call small continuation bets (≈1/3 pot ≈ 200) because I have showdown value and backdoors; fold to sizable C-bets or heavy turn aggression (≈≥1/2 pot ≈300+); if Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate the turn and only start betting for value if I improve to two-pair/trips or the board runout is clearly favorable.

I have pocket Aces on a board of 6h-5c-Qh. My opponent checked to me. While the board has some straight and flush draw possibilities, I currently have the best hand (overpair). I will place a continuation bet of approximately two-thirds of the pot to extract value from any Queens, mid-pairs, or draws my opponent might be holding, while also protecting my hand against those draws.

Call. I have second pair (5♠) with a weak 8 kicker on 6♥‑5♣‑Q♥. I’m out of position so 3‑betting or check‑raising OOP with this hand is a mistake — I don’t have blockers or fold equity. Flash’s c‑bet of 400 into a ~1000 pot is ~40% pot; the call is cheap (breakeven equity ≈28–30%) and my pair has decent showdown value against a wide button c‑bet range (lots of A/K/x and pure bluffs). I’ll call to control the pot and see the turn, then fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve to two‑pair/trips or pick up a real draw. If Flash checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn for value or pot control. No raise — just call and proceed cautiously.

Check. I have second pair (5s) with a weak 8 kicker on 6♥‑5♣‑Q♥‑9♣ — a marginal hand out of position versus a wide, deep opener. The pot is 1,400 and there is no turn bet yet. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a hand that is often behind thin value (Qx, 9x, pair+draws) and only ahead of pure bluffs. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize equity, and keeps worse hands and bluffs in to call. Plan: check and call a reasonable turn bet (roughly up to ~1/2 pot ≈ 600–700) because my hand has showdown value; fold to large turn overbets (≈2/3–full pot or bigger) unless I improve. If Flash checks back, re-evaluate the river — only start building the pot with two‑pair/trips or a clear line to extract value; otherwise keep pot small and call small river bets or fold big river pressure.

I hold pocket Aces and currently have an overpair on a board of 6h-5c-Qh-9c. After my continuation bet on the flop was called, Minni checked again on the turn. The 9c is relatively safe, although it completes a straight for 8-7 and adds a backdoor club draw. I will continue betting for value to extract chips from a Queen, middle pairs, or various draws. A bet of 1000 into a 1400 pot is a strong value bet that keeps the pressure on my opponent while building the pot.

I hold 8♥5♠ — second pair (5s) with a very weak kicker on 6♥‑5♣‑Q♥‑9♣. Flash has taken a value‑forward line (button open pre, flop c‑bet, now a sizable turn bet of 1,000 into the pot that is now 2,400). Calling 1,000 to win 3,400 gives me ~29.4% required equity. My realistic chances to improve on the river are small (5 outs: three 8s + two 5s ≈ 10.9%), and as‑is my hand only beats pure bluffs and some K/A highs — Flash’s line is weighted toward Qx/9x/other value hands that dominate me. Raising is a poor option (no meaningful blockers, OOP with a marginal pair, would bloat the pot unnecessarily). I already planned to fold to heavy turn aggression (>~1/2 pot) unless I’d improved. Preserve stack and fold.

Heads-up on the button with A♥2♥ — a strong heads-up hand: the nut ace plus nut-flush potential and excellent playability in position. Limping/giving a cheap flop loses initiative and allows the big blind to see flops with wide equity. I want to build the pot and apply pressure versus a wide BB defending range, while keeping postflop SPR manageable with my ~105bb stack. A standard 3x BB open to 300 extracts value from worse Aces/pairs and gets fold equity, yet keeps options to proceed postflop. Plan: if Flash calls I’ll play aggressively in position (c-bet favorable Ace/heart/low-connectivity flops, check back dry boards), call small-to-medium 3-bets and fold to very large 3-bets/4-bets unless the sizing/read justify a deeper commitment.

Holding T9o in the Big Blind, I am facing a 3x raise from Minni. T9o is a standard defending hand against a button opening in a heads-up or short-handed scenario. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), I have the necessary equity to see a flop and play post-flop.

On a board of 5h-8c-4c, I have two overcards and some backdoor straight potential with my T9o. As the Big Blind who called a pre-flop raise, the standard and most balanced play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see how Minni acts on this low, connected board before committing more chips.

I opened to 300 from the button with A♥2♥ and got a call. Flop is 5♥ 8♣ 4♣ (pot 600). I have the nut Ace (Ace-high) plus a backdoor heart/five-card wheel potential; the board is low and relatively disconnected — exactly the sort of flop where the preflop raiser should c-bet heads-up. Flash’s BB calling range is wide and contains many missed hands and club draws that I can charge or fold out. I want to take down the pot now or get value from worse A‑highs/small pairs while denying equity to clubs. I’ll make a half‑pot continuation bet (300): enough to generate fold equity and charge draws without overcommitting a large portion of my stack with only Ace‑high. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (barrel hearts/blanks selectively, check to control on dangerous cards). If he raises a large sizing I’ll fold; I may call a small‑to‑medium raise only when the sizing/read suggests a wide bluffing range.

I am facing a half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600) on a low, somewhat connected board. With 9h Tc, I have two overcards to the board and a backdoor flush draw (Tc). Given the pot odds (3:1), I need approximately 25% equity to continue. Against a standard wide button-opening range that C-bets frequently on this board, my hand has enough equity to see a turn card. I will call here and evaluate the turn, looking to improve to a pair or pick up a more significant draw.

On the turn, the 9d has improved my hand to top pair (Nines). Since I am in the Big Blind and was the caller pre-flop and on the flop, the standard play is to check to the aggressor, Minni. This allows her to continue her semi-bluffs with flush draws or overcards, while also keeping the pot manageable in case she has a stronger hand like a straight or a set. If she checks behind, I am likely ahead and can evaluate the river.

I have A♥2♥ on 5♥ 8♣ 4♣ 9♦ with the button and the pot is 1,200. I opened and c-bet the flop; Flash just called and checked the turn. The turn 9♦ is a relatively clean card (not a club and not a heart) and is a good spot to barrel — I still have the nut Ace (only Ace-high, no pair) and want to charge club draws, worse A‑highs and small pairs that will call. Betting keeps initiative, denies free cards to club draws, and extracts thin value while keeping my stack commitment reasonable. I’ll fire a half‑pot turn bet of 600. Plan if action changes: if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river and proceed cautiously (value-bet thin on safe rivers, check vs obvious club/paired/9 runouts); if he check‑raises large (pot-sized or bigger) I’ll fold — that sizing is usually polarized to two-pair/sets and I have poor equity. I may entertain calling a very small check‑raise to see a river because I’m in position, but I will not commit large chips with only Ace-high.

On the turn, the 9d gives me top pair with a ten kicker. Minni has followed up her flop continuation bet with a half-pot bet of 600 into 1200. While the board is somewhat connected and there are several hands that beat me (overpairs, sets, or a straight like 67), folding top pair here would be too exploitable, especially given the pot odds of 3:1. Raising is risky because it likely only gets called by better hands while folding out her bluffs. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how she acts on the river, where I can reassess the strength of my hand depending on the final card and her action.

On the river, the board has paired the four, making my hand two pair (Nines and Fours) with a Ten kicker. Given the action, checking is the most prudent move. I have been the caller throughout the hand, and checking allows Minni to continue her aggression with her entire range, including potential missed draws (like clubs or open-ended straight draws) that she might decide to bluff. If she checks back, I likely have the best hand against her missed draws or smaller pairs like eights or fives. If she bets, I will have to evaluate the size and the likelihood of her having a nine with a better kicker, a straight (67), or a four. At this point, checking is the best way to control the pot size and maximize value from her bluffs.

I have A♥2♥. Heads‑up I opened, c‑bet the flop and barreled the turn; Flash called both streets. The river (4♥) pairs the board, leaving me with only Ace‑high (no flush). With the board paired, Flash’s calling range on flop/turn likely contains many made hands (pocket pairs like 88/99/55, occasional 5x sets, and the rare 44 quads) plus missed club draws and some A‑x or K‑x hands. A river bet here is thinly profitable at best — it will gain calls from a small number of worse A‑highs but will be folded by many worse hands and called or check‑raised by hands that beat me. Given the real risk of running into sets/quads (which can easily check‑behind to trap) and the poor value extraction versus the risk of a check‑raise, the lowest‑variance, +EV line is to check back and take showdown. This preserves most of my 9,400 stack and avoids getting exploited by a raise or losing more to stronger made hands.

I am on the button with Ks 8c, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. My standard strategy is to open-raise to 3x the big blind to build the pot and exert pressure on the big blind. Minni has a relatively short stack compared to mine (around 93bb), so maintaining aggression is key.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a button open to 300. I have T♦8♦ — suited, connected enough to flop straights and flushes and a strong defend candidate in heads-up. Calling 200 (into a 400 pot) is the right play: it gives good pot odds and keeps my range wide without bloating a pot I’ll be OOP in. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a marginal hand against a deep, aggressive opponent — that often creates difficult postflop spots. Plan postflop: be aggressive on diamond/connected flops or when I pick up equity; on dry, uncoordinated boards I’ll check and fold to heavy pressure. Call 200.

I have T♦8♦ on a Q♠4♥7♥ flop — no pair and only backdoor diamond and runner straight possibilities. I’m OOP against a wide button c-betting range; leading here is unnecessarily vulnerable to a raise from a very deep, aggressive opponent. I’ll check to control the pot and see how Flash reacts. Plan: call a small-to-medium c-bet to realize my backdoor equity and keep his bluffs in; fold (or heavily re-evaluate) to a large raise. If I pick up a diamond or a strong improving card on the turn I can convert to a semi-bluff or a value line, otherwise keep pot small and avoid bloating with a marginal hand.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with King-high. The flop of Qs-4h-7h is relatively dry, although a heart flush draw is present. Since Minni checked to me, a standard continuation bet of 50% pot is appropriate here to represent the Queen or a high pocket pair and to force folds from her weaker range that missed this board.

Call 300. Heads-up vs a wide button c-bet and I’m OOP on a very dry Q♠-4♥-7♥ flop. T♦8♦ has no pair and, contrary to my earlier quick note, no realistic diamond flush possibility (with zero diamonds on the flop you can’t make a 5-card diamond flush by river). I do have 6 immediate outs to a pair and tiny runner-runner straight possibilities — chance to improve by the river ≈24%. The bet is small (300 into a 900 pot ≈ 1/3 pot), so calling keeps a lot of bluffs in, gives correct implied odds with deep stacks (my 9,100 vs his 30,000), and avoids bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Plan: call and reevaluate on the turn — continue aggressively if I pair or pick up a clear equity card; fold to large turn aggression if I miss and he shows strength.

Check. I missed on the turn (T♦8♦ on Q♠-4♥-7♥-3♣): no pair, no real draw, only 6 clean outs (~24% to pair on the river). I’m out of position vs a very deep, aggressive opponent who already c-bet the flop and I don’t want to lead OOP with essentially air and no fold equity. Checking controls the pot (current pot 1,200) and lets me realize my equity cheaply. Plan for the river: if I pair or otherwise improve I can bet/raise for value; if Flash bets on the river I’ll call reasonably-sized bets because of my ~24% equity — breakeven bet size solves to roughly b ≈ 550 (b/(1200+2b)=0.24), so I’ll call small-to-medium river bets and fold large ones. If Flash checks back, I’ll either check down or bluff-catch on a favorable river card rather than firing a bluff OOP now.

On the turn, the 3c is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. I have King-high with no real draw. After my continuation bet was called on the flop, checking back here is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the pot size and potentially realize my showdown value or see if I hit a King on the river for free. Betting again (double barreling) with King-high against a range that called the flop is unlikely to generate enough folds from better hands like a 7 or a Queen at this stage.

I missed — T♦8♦ has no pair on Q♠–4♥–7♥–3♣–3♦ and very little showdown value. Flash c‑bet the flop, I called, then both checked the turn; his check‑turn line reduces my fold equity and makes a river bluff thinly profitable. Pot is only 1,200 and I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with air versus a deep, sticky opponent. I’ll check down and preserve my stack; if Flash now leads a small bet (roughly ≤~550) I’ll consider calling as a bluff‑catcher, but I won’t fire a river bluff myself here.

On the river, the board has paired with a 3, and Minni has checked for the third consecutive time (after calling the flop). My King-high has very little showdown value, but it does beat some of Minni's potential missed heart draws. Given that she called the flop and has checked twice since, she likely has a marginal made hand like a 7, a 4, or a weak Queen that she is unwilling to fold but doesn't want to bet. Turning my hand into a bluff here is risky as she might call with any pair on this board texture. Checking back allows me to see the showdown for free and potentially win against a missed draw.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with Q♠6♣. In heads-up play the button should open a wide range — Q6o is fine to raise for initiative and fold equity. A standard 2x BB open (raise to 200 total) puts pressure on the big blind, keeps the pot manageable with 87.5BB effective, and lets me play post-flop in position. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call small 3-bets cautiously, and use position to c-bet or check-call on favorable flops.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 5d. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Button/Small Blind. With 3:1 pot odds and a suited hand that has some connectivity, this is a standard defend. I have a significant chip lead, and calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position.

The flop is 6h-7h-Ah, which is very coordinated and favors a range that contains many Aces and hearts. I have 9d-5d, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8), but no hearts. Minni is the pre-flop aggressor. On this texture, she is likely to continuation bet a wide range. My hand has very little showdown value and only a weak draw that is vulnerable to flushes. Checking is the standard play here to see how she reacts. If she bets, I will likely have to fold unless the sizing is extremely small, given the lack of hearts in my hand.

I opened preflop and I have Q♠6♣ on A♥7♥6♥ — a pair of sixes (middle pair) with no hearts. Heads‑up as the preflop aggressor this is a standard c-bet spot: I have showdown value and need to charge/deter the many heart draws and worse Ax/Kx-type hands in his calling range. A half‑pot bet (200 into 400) gives good fold equity, extracts value from worse pairs and non‑heart holdings, and keeps the pot manageable given the deep effective stacks. Plan: bet 200; if he folds I take it, if he calls I reevaluate on the turn (continue on blank turns, be cautious or fold to big aggression if a heart or a second Ace appears), and if he check‑raises large I will respect that line and fold — my hand can’t comfortably commit versus a likely made flush or strong Ace.

On a monotone flop of 6h-7h-Ah, I have a gutshot straight draw with my 9d-5d but no hearts. Minni's 200 bet into a 400 pot is a standard continuation bet. Calling here is unattractive because even if I hit an 8 for a straight, any fourth heart on the board makes my hand extremely vulnerable, and I currently have zero heart protection. Raising as a bluff on an Ace-high monotone board against the pre-flop aggressor is high-risk. Given my stack lead and the poor equity realization of this specific hand on this board, folding is the most disciplined play.

In heads-up play, T7o is a standard opening hand from the Button. Given my significant stack advantage over Minni, I want to maintain pressure. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent sizing for pre-flop opens.

J♠T♥ is a playable heads‑up hand vs a wide button open to 300. Flash’s 3x open is standard but still a fairly wide range — JTo has decent equity and good postflop playability. I don’t want to make a tiny min‑3bet to 500 (ineffective) or overcommit with a larger 3bet out of the blue with a marginal hand; with deep stacks I’ll keep the pot manageable and realize equity. I’ll call (commit a total of 300) and plan to proceed postflop: play aggressively with top pair/draws, float small c‑bets when appropriate, and exercise pot control or fold to heavy action on dry boards. This line balances EV and variance in heads‑up play.

Flop: Th Js on 8h Qh 2h — I have no pair but a very strong draw: four hearts to a flush (9 hearts) plus three remaining tens and two non-heart jacks for ~14 unique outs (≈30% to hit on the turn, ≈51% by river to make at least a pair/flush in raw card equity). Flash opened wide preflop, so his range contains a lot of hands that will fold to pressure (weak Qx, unpaired broadways, small pairs), but also some made hearts/Qx. Betting here as a semi‑bluff gives me fold equity, denies free cards, and builds a pot when I hit. I want a size that accomplishes those goals while keeping worse hands and weaker draws in — half‑pot is appropriate heads‑up. If he calls I still have good equity and can proceed based on turn action; if he raises big I will respect a strong made hand and re-evaluate (likely fold or call very selectively depending on sizing). Action: bet 300 (≈1/2 pot).

On a monotone flop of three hearts, I have absolutely no connection to the board and no hearts in my hand (Td 7c). Minni's lead (donk bet) on this texture often indicates she has at least one heart, a made flush, or a pair. Given I have zero equity and no reliable way to bluff on later streets without a heart, folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term play.

Heads‑up on the button with A♥8♥ — a premium heads‑up open: suited ace, great playability and dominating many BB calling hands. Opponent (BB) is deep but I have position and initiative, so I should open‑raise rather than limp or just call. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB): a standard size here that builds the pot, creates fold equity versus weaker hands, and keeps SPR reasonable postflop. If Flash calls I’ll use position to c‑bet appropriate flops and extract value; if he 3‑bets large I’ll fold or re‑evaluate depending on sizing (can call smaller 3‑bets occasionally given the suited ace). Raising maximizes EV in this spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c2c. Minni, who has a relatively short stack of 9000 compared to my 30600, has raised to 300 from the Button (SB). While 62 suited has some playability, it is a very weak hand to defend against a 3x raise out of position, even in a heads-up situation. My strategy has been to play cautiously and avoid unnecessary risks against aggressive betting patterns. Given the low strength of my hand, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9h 7c, a mediocre hand. However, I have a massive stack advantage over Minni (30,550 vs 9,300). In heads-up play, maintaining pressure is key. Raising is generally better than just calling from the SB/BTN to seize the initiative and potentially take down the blinds immediately. 9-7 offsuit is strong enough to open for a standard raise against a shorter stack.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with 7♦10♥. Heads-up the button’s opening range is wide, and T7o has reasonable equity, but I’m out of position postflop and stacks are deep (~93bb), so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a light 3-bet. Calling the extra 200 (300 total committed) gives me ~33% pot odds and lets me see a flop to realize equity, float C-bets, or fold to strong aggression. A min 3-bet to 500 is an option as a rare bluff, but with this hand and OOP I prefer to keep the pot controlled and play postflop.

Flop: Tc 4h 9s. I have T♥7♦ — top pair, weak kicker, out of position, stacks ~91bb effective. Button opened wide preflop, so his range contains a lot of hands I beat, but also many better tens, 9x, and two-card straight draws (J8/QJ/etc.). With a marginal but showdown-viable hand and deep stacks I don't want to inflate the pot OOP. Checking controls the pot size, lets me realize equity, and lets the button fire c-bets/bluffs so I can call or pot-control later. Plan: check and call small-to-medium continuation bets (≈≤40–50% pot) to keep weaker holdings and bluffs in; fold to heavy overbets/raises that polarize toward better tens or strong two-pair/sets. If Flash checks back I can re-evaluate turn for a small value bet or another pot-control check.

On this flop of Tc-4h-9s, I have second pair (nines). My opponent, Minni, has checked to me. While I have a marginal made hand, this board is fairly connected and could have hit her range. However, checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity, as well as protect my checking range with a hand that has showdown value. Betting here might force out worse hands but get called or raised by better hands (like Tens, better Nines, or straight draws). I will check behind to keep the pot small.

I have top pair (T♥7♦) with a very weak kicker on a J♠ T♣ 9♠ 4♥ board, heads-up and out-of-position against a wide button opening range. Both players checked the flop and Flash checked the turn — that line often represents a wide mix of weak hands, underpairs, and some turned-made hands, but the J on the turn also completes dangerous broadway straights (KQ, Q8, etc.). With deep stacks (~91bb effective) I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a thin value bet that can be raised or exploited by straights/two-pair/Jx. I’ll check to control the pot, allow bluffs to show themselves, and plan to call small river bets but fold to large/polarizing aggression. If Flash checks through I’ll re-evaluate the river and potentially thin-value bet a safe card.

On this turn (Js), my pair of nines has dropped to third pair on a coordinated board (Tc-9s-Js). Minni has checked for the second time, which could indicate weakness or a trap with a marginal hand like a Ten or a Jack. Given that the board is quite connected and my showdown value is mediocre, checking back is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the pot size and see a river for free, preserving my stack advantage while still having a hand that can potentially win at showdown if the river is safe.

I check. I have top pair (T♥) with a very weak kicker (7♦) and I'm out of position against a wide preflop opener who checked flop and turn — a line that usually contains many missed hands, underpairs and some medium strength holdings. Betting the river would bloat a deep OOP pot, fold out many worse tens and thin value, or induce raises from two-pair/sets/Jx that beat me. Pot-control and taking it down at showdown is the higher EV line here; I’ll check and be prepared to call small-to-medium river bets but fold to large polarizing raises.

I have third pair (Nines) on a board of Tc-4h-9s-Js-2d. Minni has checked on every street after calling my pre-flop raise. My hand has decent showdown value, but it is unlikely to get called by many worse hands if I bet, and I could potentially be value-owning myself against a better Nine or a slow-played Ten/Jack. Checking back is the most reliable way to realize my equity and win the pot at showdown.

Heads-up on the button with K♠8♥ — a playable, often-favored hand versus the big blind’s wide calling/defending range. I want initiative and fold equity, so I’ll open to a standard 3x (300 total). Size 300 puts pressure on Flash, reduces the frequency of cheap flops and light calls, and looks natural given table tendencies. Postflop plan: c-bet favorable textures, play cautiously vs strong resistance; fold to large 3-bets unless I pick up meaningful equity (pair/straight/flush draw) or pot odds justify a call.

I have Pocket Queens, the third-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My opponent, Minni, has raised from the button to 300. Given our stack sizes (I have a significant lead, but she only has 9400 chips left), I want to build a pot immediately and potentially get her to commit her entire stack if she has a strong range. I will 3-bet to 1000 to extract value and put pressure on her button opening range.

Flash 3-bet me to 1000 after my 300 open. I have K♠8♥ on the button — not strong enough to 4-bet bluff profitably, but playable heads-up with position. The 3-bet size is moderate and calling 700 more (1000 total) keeps the pot reasonable, preserves position and lets me realize equity on many flops against a likely wide 3‑betting range. Folding is too tight here; a small 4‑bet would risk a lot with a marginal hand. Plan: call, see the flop, c-bet favorable textures, fold to heavy resistance when I miss and continue when I hit or pick up strong draws.

I have flopped top set with Pocket Queens on a monotone club board. While I don't hold a club, which means Minni could potentially have a made flush or the nut flush draw, top set is an extremely strong hand that must be played for value and protection. The SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) is roughly 4.35, making this a committed situation. I will bet 1000 (half the pot) to extract value from hands like AcX, KcX, or JxJx and to charge any single-club draws to see the turn.

Flop is Qc-Tc-6c — a monotone club board. I hold K♠8♥: no pair, no club, just a K-high overcard. Flash 3-bet preflop and now bets 1,000 into the 3,000 pot, so calling 1,000 gets ~20% equity to continue. His line and the small sizing are consistent with a wide c-bet range (made clubs, Q/T pairs, some broadways and bluffs), so folding here would be too tight and raising is a poor, high-variance bluff with little fold equity against a 3-bettor. I’ll call to use position, realize equity, and see a turn where I can fold to strong action or continue if I pair my K or pick up favorable runout/turn play.

The turn card is a disaster for my hand. With four clubs now on the board and me holding no clubs, any single club in Minni's hand gives her a flush, which beats my set of Queens. While I still have 10 outs to a full house or quads on the river, leading out into a 4-flush board is extremely risky. Minni called a 3-bet pre-flop and a bet on a monotone flop, which strongly suggests her range contains many clubs (like the Ac, Jc, or 9c). Checking is the most prudent action to control the pot and see how she reacts, as I am currently drawing to a boat if she has the flush.

I have K♠8♥ — the turn Kc gives me top pair but the board is Qc-Tc-6c-Kc: four clubs on board. Flash 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the flop, so his range contains a lot of club combos that already have a made flush; his check on the turn can be pot control with a flush or simple weakness with non‑club Q/T or missed broadways. Betting here charges mainly hands that are still behind (Qc/Tc non‑club combos) but gets called (or raised) by almost any club, which is disastrous. With a medium kicker and a highly polarized opponent range, pot control is the prudent, +EV line. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs or thin value on the river, and re-evaluate there: if he bets a small/medium amount on the river I can call for thin value vs worse hands, but I’ll fold to large overbets that strongly represent the flush.

On a four-club board, my set of Queens is effectively a bluff catcher. Since I do not hold a club myself, any single club in Minni's hand beats me. Minni's check on the turn after the fourth club appeared could mean she's pot-controlling with a low flush or she doesn't have a club at all. There is no value in betting here because hands I beat (like a straight or two pair) are unlikely to call on such a dangerous board, and any hand that calls me will almost certainly be a flush. Checking is the best option to reach showdown and hope Minni was also missing a club.

Board is Qc-6c-Tc-Kc-3s, I hold K♠8♥ (top pair, medium kicker) and the board has four clubs. Flash was the preflop 3-bettor and c-bet the flop, then checked turn and checked river. His range includes a lot of club combos that make the flush (any club in his hand is a made flush on this runout), so a river bet mainly gets called by better club flushes or the occasional Q/T non-club — and worse hands that will call are relatively thin. Betting here turns over a lot of equity to a raise and creates unnecessary risk; checking controls the pot and gets to showdown for minimal cost. Given the high frequency of flushes in his 3-bet range and his passive turn/river line, pot-control and check to showdown is the lowest-variance, +EV play.

In this heads-up scenario, I am on the button with K2o. This is a standard hand to open or call from the small blind against a shorter-stacked opponent. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Minni, who has a stack of 7600 (76 big blinds).

Facing a standard button open to 300 (I’m the big blind, to call 200) with 8♠5♥ offsuit. This is a weak, poorly-connected, unsuited hand that will be out of position postflop and is frequently dominated by the button’s opening range. A 3-bet here would be purely a light bluff and unnecessary risk with no blockers or equity; calling gives poor playability OOP against an aggressive opponent. Pot odds / stack dynamics don’t justify playing a marginal hand out of position — I preserve my 7.6k stack for better spots. Fold.

Heads-up on the button (small blind). Qc4s is marginal, but opening here is standard — calling 50 is passive and leaves me out of position postflop. A min-raise to 200 seizes initiative, applies fold equity vs the big blind and keeps the pot manageable with ~75bb effective stacks. Flash has shown willingness to defend preflop, but I can fold Q4o to a large 3-bet; if called I’ll play cautiously and look to c-bet favorable flops or check back when out of position. Raise to 200 to take the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind holding King of Spades and Seven of Hearts (K7o). Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Small Blind. To call, I only need to put in 100 more into a pot that will be 400. This gives me 3-to-1 odds (25% equity required), and K7o is well above the threshold to defend the big blind against a small open from the button/SB, particularly given our stack depths. I will call to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high on a board of Jack-high with two clubs. As the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard strategy is to check to the raiser. I have no made hand and no significant draw, so leading out would be an unnecessary risk. I will check and see how Minni responds.

Flop: Jc 4h 9c. I hold Qc‑4s — a made pair of 4s with a decent queen kicker and I hold a club blocker (Qc). Heads‑up as the preflop raiser I should continue frequently on this board to take the initiative, fold out pure overcards (AK/AQ/KQ) and charge any backdoor draws. My pair is vulnerable, so I don’t want to overcommit — a half‑pot c‑bet (200 into a 400 pot) preserves fold equity while keeping the pot controllable. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn (check‑back blanks, value or bluff only when I improve or when odds are favorable); if he check‑raises large I’ll fold this thin pair.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high (Ks 7h) on a board of Jc 4h 9c. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 200 into a 400 pot. While the pot odds are 3-to-1, I have no pair, no draw, and no backdoor flush potential on this coordinated board. Continuing out of position with a marginal King-high hand is likely to lead to further losses on the turn and river. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9s 5s, a playable suited hand in a heads-up situation. My opponent, Minni, has a shorter stack of 7700 (77 BB). Raising from the button is a standard aggressive play to take control of the hand. I'll stick to my usual sizing of 300.

I’m on the big blind with K♦3♣ facing a button open to 300 heads-up. The pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (300 total including my posted BB). Versus a wide SB opening range in heads-up, K3o has reasonable equity and the call buys me a cheap look — I’m risking 200 to win a ~600 pot if I call (need ~33% equity to break even). I don’t want to 3-bet with a weak, unsuited K that’s often dominated, so I’ll defend by calling and see the flop, playing cautiously postflop (fold to heavy aggression with no improvement, continue/raise if I pair my king or get a favorable texture).

I defended preflop with K♦3♣ to see a cheap flop versus a wide button open. The flop (5♦-7♠-4♥) is very dry and I only have a lone overcard with no real draws (one diamond on board + my K♦ is not a flush draw). I’m out of position and don’t want to bloating the pot with a weak hand that’s often behind (A‑x, pairs) or easily turned into a bluff-catcher. Optimal, low-variance line here is to check and let the button either c‑bet small (call and reevaluate the turn) or take it down; fold to sizable pressure. If Flash bets small (≈1/3 pot ≈200) I will call and re-evaluate; if he bets large (>~1/2 pot) I’ll fold. No raise — no value or fold equity to justify it.

On a flop of 5d-7s-4h, I have middle pair (fives) with a nine kicker. This is a decent hand on a relatively low, connected board. Minni has checked to me. I want to bet here for protection and value, as there are many overcards and straight draws (like 6x) that could cause trouble on later streets. A small bet of half the pot should be sufficient to define where I stand and potentially take the pot down now.

I defended preflop with K♦3♣ to see a cheap flop, but the 5♦-7♠-4♥ texture is very dry and I have only a lone overcard and no real draws. Flash puts in 300 into the 600 pre-bet pot (pot now 900); calling 300 to win 1,200 requires ~25% equity. Against a heads-up c-bet range he has a lot of Kx/Ax and pocket pairs that dominate or are ahead, and out of position I’ll face further pressure on later streets. Raising as a bluff here is thin and likely -EV. Preserve stack and fold this weak, unimproved hand.

Heads-up on the button with K♦Q♥ — a strong, position-sensitive hand. With ~74BB effective and a single opponent in the big blind, I want to take the initiative, build the pot when I'm likely ahead, and apply fold equity. A standard 3x open to 300 accomplishes that: it pressures weaker hands, keeps the pot manageable versus heavy 3-bets, and gives me positional advantage postflop to extract value or control the size. If Flash 3-bets large I'll re-evaluate (often folding to very large 3-bets), but vs a call I can play comfortably in position. Raising to 300 is the optimal balance here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit (Th 2c). Minni, the small blind, has raised to 300. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call, Ten-Deuce is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given Minni's stack size and general aggression shown in previous hands, it is better to fold this bottom-tier hand and wait for a more advantageous spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 2c 4s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While I have a significant chip lead over Minni, there is no strategic advantage to playing this hand. I have folded similar trash hands in previous rounds (like Hand #60 and #68) and will continue to do so here to wait for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 4c9d — a weak offsuit hand but playable in position. In heads-up play you open-raise very wide to seize initiative and apply pressure to the BB. Limping/passive allows the BB to see cheap flops with better equity; a small, standard open (min-raise to 200) gives fold equity and keeps the pot manageable if Flash calls. I’ll min-raise to 200 (2x BB). If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold — this hand has poor vs. reraise equity — but if he calls I’ll use position to proceed cautiously postflop, c-betting when appropriate and folding to heavy action on bad boards.

I am in the Big Blind with T6o. Minni has raised from the Button/Small Blind to 200 total (a min-raise). My stack is very deep compared to hers, but T6o is a weak hand and plays poorly out of position. However, against a min-raise, the pot odds are 3-to-1. Given Minni's short stack and the low cost to see a flop, I will call and see if I can hit something, but I won't commit too much with this weak holding.

I have flopped middle pair (Tens) with a weak kicker on an Ace-high board. As the Big Blind, I should check to the pre-flop aggressor. The Ace is a significant card for the button's range, so I want to control the size of the pot and see how Minni proceeds before committing more chips.

I opened wide preflop heads-up so I have the range advantage. With 4♣9♦ on A♥ T♦ 2♣ I have missed completely (only distant backdoor club/runner possibilities) — this is a pure continuation-bluff spot. Flash checked, so a standard ~50% pot c-bet will fold out many of his medium-strength hands (Kx/Qx/junk, unpaired broadways and some overcards) while keeping the pot controllable if called. I’ll bet 200 into the 400 pot. Plan: take it down if he folds; if he calls I’ll check the turn to control the pot and give up to significant aggression unless I improve (pair or club), and I’ll fold to a sizable check-raise on this street since my hand has virtually no showdown value.

I have flopped middle pair (Tens) on an Ace-high board. While the Ace is a scare card and likely hits Minni's range as the pre-flop aggressor, a middle pair is too strong to fold to a single half-pot continuation bet. I will call to see a turn card and re-evaluate based on the action and whether the board texture changes. Raising here would likely only be called by better hands (Aces or better Tens).

On the turn, the board pairs the Ten, giving me trips (Three of a Kind, Tens). While my kicker (6) is weak, this is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Since I am out of position and Minni was the pre-flop and flop aggressor, I will check to her. This allows her to continue bluffing or to bet for value with an Ace. If she bets, I plan to raise to extract value from her stack, as the board is relatively dry and I want to get paid while she likely still views an Ace as the best hand.

I c-bet the flop (200 into a 400 pot) and was called; the pot is now 800. I hold 4♣9♦ — I have no pair and, with only one club in my hand and only one card to come, I cannot make a five-card club flush. The turn (Ten of Clubs) pairs the board, which actually helps the caller more than me: A-x hands become two-pair and any Ten in hand becomes trips. Given I was called on the flop, Flash’s range is weighted toward Ax, Tx, and other hands that now have much stronger showdown value. Double-barreling as a bluff here has poor equity and is unlikely to fold out the hands that called the flop. With a large-stacked opponent and a weak hand, the prudent play is pot control — check the turn and fold to meaningful aggression. I will check and give up unless I face a very small probing bet (in which case I might consider a targeted call), but otherwise fold to a bet.

I have trips (Tens) with a 6 kicker on a board of Td-2c-Ah-Tc-Ks. After calling a flop bet and seeing the turn go check-check, it's likely Minni has a marginal hand like a weak Ace or perhaps a King that improved on the river. By checking the turn, I missed an opportunity to build the pot, but I also kept her range wide. On the river, I should lead for value. A check might result in another check from her, whereas a bet can get called by an Ace, a King, or even a pocket pair. I will bet 500 into the 800 pot, which is a sizing that looks like it could be a value bet or a missed draw, inviting a call from her marginal holdings.

I hold 4c9d and missed every street. I c-bet the flop and was called; the pot is 1300 and Flash fires a smallish 500 on the river. Calling 500 into a final pot of 1800 gives ~28% pot odds, but my hand has essentially no showdown value and only beats pure bluffs. Flash’s line (call flop, check turn, small river bet) is consistent with Ax, Tx, Kx or other thin value hands that now beat me. I’ll fold and preserve my stack — only worth calling if I had a strong read that he bluffs this river far more than 28% of the time.

In heads-up play, K9o is a strong starting hand from the button. I will follow my established pattern of opening for 3x the big blind to 300 total chips to put pressure on Minni and maintain initiative.

Heads-up vs a 3x open to 300 from the button, I’m in the big blind with Jh7s. Pot is 400 and it’s 200 to call (to make my total contribution 300), giving ~3:1 pot odds (need ≈25% equity). Against a typical wide heads-up opening range J7o has decent equity (~40% vs a wide range), so defending is +EV. With deep stacks (≈71.5BB) I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand by making a small 3-bet that can be either called or 4-bet; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me play the flop reasonably (float/continue on favorable textures, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards). So I will call the 200 required (total 300 in the pot).

Heads‑up on a fairly dry flop (2♥ T♠ 7♦). I have middle pair (7♠) with a decent jack kicker. Versus a wide HU opening range the button will have a lot of worse one‑pair and Jx hands that I beat, plus many overcards that I still block somewhat. I prefer to seize the initiative and get value/protection rather than check and allow a free turn card or let a wide range continue for free. A small, controlled c‑bet (~1/3 pot) puts weaker hands to a price, charges overcards, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. If I get significant raise size (near‑pot/large) I’ll fold or re‑evaluate since my pair is vulnerable to Tens/overpairs. Therefore I will bet 200 to extract value and deny equity.

On a dry flop of Ts-7d-2h, Minni has led out with a small donk bet of 200 into a 600 pot. With King-high and two overcards (K and 9), I have a decent amount of equity against her likely range, which could include middle pairs, weak Tens, or even air. Given that I am getting 4:1 pot odds and have position, calling is the most profitable play. It allows me to see a turn card cheaply and potentially improve or take the pot away on a later street if she shows weakness.

I hold J♥7♠ — second pair with a decent kicker on 2♥ T♠ 7♦ 4♣. Heads‑up Flash opened wide preflop and called my small flop c‑bet, so his call range contains a lot of worse one‑pair hands (Jx, small pairs) plus some tens/overpairs. The 4♣ is a fairly harmless card; with him checking the turn I want to charge overcards and extract value from worse pairs while keeping the pot controlled. Pot is 1,000, so a ~40% pot continuation bet (400) puts the correct price on his marginal hands and denies free cards — if he check‑raises large I’ll fold or strongly re‑evaluate, if he calls I’ll re‑assess on the river.

On the turn, the board is 2h-Ts-7d-4c, which is quite dry and hasn't changed the situation much from the flop. Minni has continued her aggression with a second lead (donk bet) of 400 into a 1000 pot. While the price is relatively cheap (requiring me to call 400 to win a total pot that would be 1800), my King-high has very little showdown value and only about 13% equity to improve to a pair on the river. Given Minni's history of folding even strong hands when the board gets scary, I could potentially call with the intent to bluff on an Ace or King river, but calling down with air against a double-barrel is generally a losing strategy in the long run. Since I have no draws and King-high is unlikely to be the best hand here, I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB). 5♦2♦ is garbage, but heads-up the button opens extremely wide because I have postflop position and can apply pressure. The pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 to call, but a min-raise to 200 (2×BB) buys a lot of fold equity and keeps the initiative. I’ll raise to the min (200 total) to steal the blind and play the hand in position. Plan: if Flash folds I scoop the pot; if he 3-bets big I’ll fold this junk; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously postflop (small c-bet or check back depending on texture) since 5-2o has poor equity.

In this heads-up situation, I'm in the big blind holding 10s 7d. Minni has raised to 200 from the small blind/button. Given the pot odds (calling 100 to win a total pot of 400), T7o is a standard defending hand. I have a significant chip lead (32150 vs 7550), but there's no need to play overly aggressive pre-flop with this marginal hand. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity.

On this flop of 3c 7s 5c, I have top pair with a Ten kicker (Ts 7d). This is a strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni has checked, and as the big blind who defended pre-flop, I have the option to check or bet. Given the board is somewhat coordinated (straight and flush possibilities), I want to protect my hand and extract value from worse hands like smaller pairs, straight draws, or even overcards. However, checking back can also be a viable strategy to control the pot and induce bluffs on later streets. In this specific scenario, checking to see how the turn develops is a balanced play that keeps the pot manageable while I have a solid showdown value hand.

I opened wide preflop and now hit middle pair on 3c-7s-5c (5h in hand). Heads-up in position this is a hand to seize the initiative — it has value versus overcards and will deny free cards to club draws. I want a small c-bet to extract value and keep the pot controllable, so I’ll bet roughly 1/3 pot. If Flash folds I scoop; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and play cautiously (check/mediate sizing unless I improve); if he raises large I’ll fold this vulnerable mid pair to avoid getting overcommitted.

I have top pair (Sevens) with a Ten kicker on a 7-5-3 board. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni's bet of 150 into 400 is small, likely a continuation bet that could include a wide range of hands, including draws or overcards. I will call to see the turn, keeping the pot manageable and allowing Minni to continue bluffing with weaker hands while I maintain a strong position with my pair.

On this turn of Jh, the board is 3c-7s-5c-Jh. I currently hold middle pair (7s) with a 10 kicker. Minni has checked to me. This card doesn't change much for my relative hand strength, although a Jack could be in Minni's range. However, checking back seems like the most prudent play. It allows me to control the size of the pot, keep her wide range of bluffs or weaker hands in, and realize my equity for free. Betting might force out hands I beat and only get called by hands that have me beat or have significant equity against me. I will check to see the river.

Flop I c-bet 150 and got called; on the turn a Jh is a scary overcard but Flash checked, which signals weakness or pot-control. I have a vulnerable but real pair of fives that beats lots of his missed overcards and draws. Heads-up I should both deny equity to turn/river draws and extract thin value from worse (A/K/Qx, some Tx and floats) while keeping the pot manageable. A small turn bet (~1/3 pot) accomplishes that — it charges club/straight draws and worse hands without committing my stack. Plan: bet small (200); fold to a large check-raise, call small-to-medium raises only with appropriate pot odds and re-evaluate on the river if called.

On this turn (Jh), the board is 3c-7s-5c-Jh. I have middle pair (7s) with a 10 kicker. Minni has bet 200 into a pot of 700. This is a relatively small bet (under 30% pot). Given my hand strength in a heads-up situation and the pot odds, I have a clear call. My hand is too strong to fold to such a small sizing, but not strong enough to raise for value, especially since the Jack may have improved her range. Calling allows me to reach the river and potentially catch a bluff or win at showdown.

On the river As, the board is 3c-7s-5c-Jh-As. I have a pair of Sevens with a Ten kicker. Minni has checked to me after betting the flop and turn. The Ace is a significant overcard that likely improves Minni's range if she had overcards or a backdoor draw, but it's also a card she might check if she was bluffing or has a marginal hand. My pair of Sevens has decent showdown value against missed straight or flush draws. There is no value in betting here, as better hands will call and worse hands will fold. I will check to see the showdown.

I hold second pair (5♣ on board + my 5♥) after the river A♠. Villain checked the river after calling a small flop and turn bet — a line consistent with missed draws, weak pairs or pot-control hands. Heads‑up my pair still has thin value against a wide calling range, so I want a small value/block bet to extract from Kx/Qx/Tx and other one-pair hands while folding out pure air. I’ll bet about one-third of the pot (350 into 1100). If he check‑raises big I’ll fold — I’m not committed with a vulnerable middle pair; if he calls I go to showdown.

On the river, the Ace of Spades is a significant scare card as it fits well into Minni's pre-flop raising range. However, Minni's bet sizing has been consistently small (around 1/3 pot) on all streets. On the river, she is betting 350 into a 1100 pot, giving me pot odds of approximately 4:1. My pair of Sevens has some showdown value against missed draws (like the club flush draw or straight draws like 64 and 86) and low-frequency bluffs. Given the excellent price and the nature of heads-up play, folding middle pair to such a small bet is usually too exploitable, even with the overcard appearing. I will call to see the showdown.

Holding King-Six offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation against a relatively short-stacked opponent (Minni has about 67 big blinds), I will follow standard poker strategy by opening with a raise. K6o is well within the top range of hands to play from the small blind/button. A 3x raise is standard to apply pressure and build a pot when I likely have the better hand.

Heads-up vs a frequent SB opener to 300. I have Ac5s — a strong enough hand in heads-up to play aggressively. Pot is 400 and it only costs 200 to call (good pot odds), but calling keeps me passive and out of position on all postflop streets. With stacks (I have ~67.5BB) a moderately-sized 3-bet is the best play: it extracts value from worse Aces/broadways, gets folds from lots of junk, and keeps options open if called. I’ll raise to 1,200 total — a meaningful raise that preserves fold equity but is a small portion of my stack. Plan: fold to a large 4-bet, continue with standard c-bet strategy if called (c-bet dry boards, proceed cautiously on coordinated/diamond-heavy flops).

I opened to 300 with K6o, and Minni has 3-bet to 1200. Minni is playing a relatively short stack of 5650 (including the 1200 already committed). While K6o is a decent opening hand, it performs poorly against a 3-bet, especially from an opponent who has shown aggression. Calling would commit nearly 20% of Minni's remaining stack and leave me in a difficult spot out of position post-flop with a marginal hand. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my large stack lead.

Heads-up on the button with 8♥6♣ — a weak but playable hand in HU. Button has post-flop positional advantage and should open a wide range to seize initiative and pick off the blind. The pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 to call, but a min-raise to 200 (2x BB) builds the pot with position, gains fold equity, and puts pressure on Flash. Stacks are deep (~71bb), so if Flash calls I can play post-flop in position; if he 3-bets big I'll fold and preserve my stack (this hand is not strong enough to continue vs a large 3-bet). Raise to 200 to take the lead and leverage position.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd8s. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 200. This is a standard min-raise from a short stack (about 70BB). Given our massive chip lead (327.5BB vs 69.5BB), I can afford to defend my blind wide, especially against a small sizing. Qd8s is a reasonable hand to see a flop with in a heads-up situation. Calling is the most prudent action here to keep the pot controlled and utilize position post-flop, as a 3-bet might force Minni to either fold hands I dominate or jam with a superior range.

On a paired board of 7-9-9, as the player out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play. My hand (Qd 8s) contains two overcards to the 7 and a backdoor straight draw. While not a strong hand, it has enough equity in a heads-up pot to see how the opponent reacts to the board. Checking allows me to control the pot and potentially realize my equity or catch a bluff if Minni decides to bet with air.

Flop 7♦ 9♥ 9♦ with 8♥6♣ on the button. I have no pair and only a few weak outs (three 8s, three 6s), so this hand has poor showdown value. I opened preflop so I have a range advantage and Flash’s check looks like weakness or pot-control. I want to take the initiative and fold out unpaired overcards/marginal hands while keeping the pot small if I’m called. Make a small continuation bet (~150 into the 400 pot, ≈37% pot). If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and will fold to meaningful check-raises or large aggression — I won’t bloat the pot with this marginal holding.

On a paired board of 7-9-9, Minni has led out with a small continuation bet of 150 into a 400 pot after my check. My hand, Qd8s, is currently just Queen-high, but it has two overcards to the board's 7. The pot odds are very favorable; I need to call 150 to win a total pot of 700, which is approximately 21.4% equity required. Given that this is a heads-up match and the bet sizing is small, my hand has enough equity against a wide range of bluffs or air to warrant a call. Folding would be too exploitable against such a small sizing, and raising would turn a hand with decent showdown value into a unnecessary bluff. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn with a board of 7d-9h-9d-5s, I have Queen-high (Qd 8s). In the previous street (flop), I checked and called a small continuation bet of 150 from Minni. The turn card is a 5s, which does not significantly change the board texture. Since I am in the Big Blind and acting out of position, checking is the standard play with my current hand strength. Queen-high has some showdown value against bluffs but is not strong enough to bet for value or to realistically fold out better hands like a 7 or small pocket pairs. Minni's check on the turn (as noted in the log) suggests she may be slowing down with a medium-strength hand or giving up on a bluff. I will check to keep the pot small and attempt to reach showdown or see a river card for free.

Check. After leading preflop and firing a small c‑bet on 7♦ 9♥ 9♦, Flash called and then checked the 5♠ turn. I hold 8♥6♣ — no pair, no backdoor flush, and very poor showdown equity (pairing an 8 or 6 still loses to most 9x). Flash’s line looks like pot‑control with a made hand (9x, 7x) or a marginal holding; a second barrel from me would have little fold equity and only bloats a pot I’m not comfortable playing big. Checking keeps the pot manageable (700), preserves my stack, and lets me fold to genuine aggression on the river. If Flash checks the river I can re‑evaluate (and possibly bluff on a favorable card); if he bets I’ll fold to meaningful pressure.

On the river with a board of 7d-9h-9d-5s-4d, I hold Queen-high (Qd 8s). The action on the flop (check-call) and the turn (check-check) suggests that Minni likely has a weak made hand (like a 7, a small pair, or Ace-high) or has given up on a bluff. While my Queen-high has very little showdown value, betting into this board is unlikely to force a fold from any 7 or small pair, and Ace-high might even call a small bet. Since I am out of position, checking is the most standard play here. If Minni checks behind, I have a slim chance of winning the pot if she was on a lower high-card draw; if she bets, I will likely have to fold as Queen-high is not strong enough to catch a river bluff in this specific sequence.

I have 8♥6♣ on 7♦ 9♥ 9♦ 5♠ 4♦. I never improved — no pair and no diamond — so my showdown equity is very poor. Flash called my small flop c‑bet, then both of us pot‑controlled the turn and he checked the river. His flop call narrows him toward real pairs (9x, 7x, 5x), small sets, or occasional two‑diamond combos that now make a flush; many of those will happily call a river bluff. A river bluff here has very low fold equity and risks a meaningful portion of my 6.8k stack for little expected gain. I preserve my stack and avoid an unprofitable bluff: check/fold to any river bet and only continue if he shows weakness. Action: check.

I am on the button with K9o, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play against a relatively short-stacked opponent. Minni has 7,400 chips (74 BBs). I will raise to 300 to maintain pressure and build the pot in position.

Heads-up vs the button's 300 open with A♠Q♣ is a clear 3-bet spot. Flash has been opening a lot from the button in this game, so AQs is ahead of a large portion of his opening range and has good postflop potential. I want to take the initiative, get fold equity, and charge weaker broadways and suited connectors rather than letting him keep a wide range for cheap. I'm out of position postflop, so I keep the 3-bet size big enough to command respect but not so large that I overcommit — raising to 1000 (total) is a standard, constructive size here. Plan: fold or pot-control vs a very large 4-bet; call smaller 4-bets depending on sizing and opponent tendencies. If called, continuation-bet most flops for value/fold equity and proceed cautiously on heavily coordinated boards.

In heads-up play, K9o is a strong enough hand to see a flop, especially when in position. Minni has shown a tendency to 3-bet with a variety of hands, including marginal ones like A4o in previous hands (Hand #46). Given that I am getting nearly 2:1 odds on the call (700 to call into a pot of 1,300) and have the advantage of position, I will call the raise and evaluate on the flop. Her stack is relatively short (65BB), so I need to be careful not to commit too many chips if I don't hit the board well, but folding now would be too tight.

I have A♠Qc on Q♠5♠3♠ — top pair (queen) with the nut kicker and the nut spade draw. This is one of the strongest flop combinations: it has large raw equity (pair+nut draw) and also needs protection because the board is 3♠ heavy and many of Flash’s cold-calling 3‑bet combos contain spades. I want to take the initiative, charge single-spade hands and weaker Qx/5x/pocket pairs, and deny free cards that could give him a made flush. A bet of ~1.3k into the 2k pot (~60–70% pot) builds the pot when I’m likely ahead, prices in draws, and still leaves room to fold or call if he check‑raises big. Plan: if he calls I’ll evaluate the turn (continue barreling non‑spades for value/protection; if a spade hits I’ll extract maximum value), and if he check‑raises small I’ll call or 3‑bet with my strong equity but respect a massive overbet that likely represents a made flush.

On a monotone flop of Qs5s3s, Minni has led out for 1300 into a 2000 pot. I hold the Kc9h. While the Kc is a good blocker to have if I were to bluff, I currently have no piece of this board and no flush draw. Minni's 3-bet pre-flop followed by a large continuation bet on this board texture suggests she either has a strong hand (like a set, a flush, or a high pocket pair with a spade) or is semi-bluffing with a spade. Since she only has 5200 chips remaining, calling would pot-commit me against a range that likely crushes my King-high. Without a spade in my hand, I have very little equity to continue.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with T5o. Pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to call, but in heads-up I want to seize initiative and apply pressure to the BB’s very wide defending range. A min-raise to 200 (200 total committed) is standard here — it gives fold equity, keeps sizing small versus a deep-stacked opponent (Flash has ~31k) and preserves postflop flexibility. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll concede with this marginal hand; if called I’ll C-bet appropriate dry flops and use position to realize equity or control pot size on dangerous boards. Raise to 200 to open the hand and play position aggressively.

I am in the Big Blind with JT suited, a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 200. With a stack of 31,400 against Minni's relatively short 8,300, I want to play a pot here. JT suited plays well post-flop, and I can either call to keep the pot smaller or 3-bet. Given the stack sizes and the strength of my hand, I will 3-bet to put pressure on her 2bb open and potentially take it down or build a pot with a hand that has good equity.

Flash 3-bet me to 600 after my button open to 200. Pot is 800 and I must put in 400 more (600 total) to continue. T5o is marginal, but this is heads-up and I have position — calling keeps the pot manageable, gives me postflop initiative in position on many flops, and offers the right price (I need ~33% equity to break even). A 4-bet bluff to 1,000 is too spewy with this hand (no suitable blockers and Flash will call often), and folding is too passive given the cheap price to see a flop heads-up. I’ll call 400 more (600 total) and plan to c-bet thin on very dry flops, control pot size or fold to heavy resistance on wet coordinated boards, and re-evaluate if Flash shows more aggression.

On a paired, dry board like 6h-6s-3c, I maintain a range advantage as the pre-flop 3-bettor. My hand, Jack-Ten of clubs, has two overcards and some backdoor straight and flush potential. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot is effective here to pressure non-paired hands and small pocket pairs while keeping the pot manageable and maintaining the initiative.

Call. The flop bet is small (400 into the pot) and I’m getting ~5:1 pot odds (need ~20% equity). With T5o I have ~6 immediate outs to a pair (T or 5) and ~24% to hit by river — borderline but enough given position and the cheap price. Flash’s line (preflop 3-bet then a modest flop stab) contains a mix of overcards and pocket pairs; many of the overcards I can outdraw or bluff later, while the big pocket pairs will continue and I should fold to heavy action. Raising here with no blockers or a made hand is too spewy — he’ll call or 4-bet too often. Folding now is too passive given the small call. Plan: call 400 to see the turn, fold to significant turn/river aggression unless I improve (pair a T or 5) or pick up a clear bluffing spot if the turn is very favorable and he shows weakness.

On the turn card 4s, the board remains relatively dry with a pair of sixes. I have Jack-high and no direct draw, although I have two overcards to the board. Having been the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, checking here allows me to see how Minni reacts and potentially see a free river if she checks back. Since she called the flop bet, she likely has a piece of the board or a pocket pair that may not fold to a second barrel. Checking is the most cautious and pot-controlling move in this spot.

Flash checked the turn after a modest flop stab. Pot is 2,000 and I have T5o in position with virtually no showdown value — the board (6h 6s 3c 4s) is very dry and has little draw potential. Flash’s line (3-bet preflop, small flop stab, then check) contains many missed overcards and medium hands that will fold to continued pressure. I’ll bet for value as a bluff/semi-steal: 1,000 (~50% pot) is large enough to credibly represent a made hand and to fold out a lot of his checking combos, but not so large that getting called leaves me committed. Plan: fold to a significant raise, call small calls and re-evaluate the river (barrel only with a good runout or clear bluffing story; otherwise check back or fold to heavy action).

On the turn card 4s, the board is 6h-6s-3c-4s. I have Jack-high with no direct draw. After I 3-bet pre-flop and continuation-bet the flop, Minni's calls suggest she has a piece of the board, a pocket pair, or a strong ace-high.
