When I check the turn and she bets 1,000 into a 2,000 pot, she is either betting for value with a 6 or a mid-pocket pair, or she is attempting to take the pot away seeing my weakness. Given that my J-high is currently losing to almost her entire range (including her bluffs like AQ/AK) and I lack the equity to call for my overcards, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

I am holding Ace-Jack offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a premium starting hand. My opponent, Minni, has a relatively short stack of 94 big blinds. Raising is the standard play here to build the pot and take control of the hand while in position. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is consistent with my previous patterns and prices in a wide range of hands from the big blind while allowing me to fold comfortably to a shove if necessary or play a strong pot post-flop.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♣7♠ facing a 3bb open to 300 from the SB/button. A7o is strong enough to defend in position-disadvantaged spot, so I’ll call and keep the pot controlled. 3-betting here would commit more chips OOP with a marginal kicker and isn’t necessary against a frequent opener — I prefer to see a flop and extract value on ace-high boards or fold/control on dry, unfavorable boards. Calling 300 (adding 200) keeps my stack deep for postflop play and minimizes variance over the session.

I have top pair (A♣) with a weak 7 kicker on 9♦ A♠ Q♠. Heads-up versus a wide button opening range this is often ahead of many of Flash’s holdings (worse A’s, Qx, broadways and single pairs). The board has two spades but I hold a spade blocker (7♠) and there is no made flush on the flop, so betting extracts value and protects my hand. I’ll make a value/protect bet about half the pot (300 into 600) to charge worse aces, queens and any draws while keeping the pot manageable OOP. If Flash raises big I’ll strongly consider that he has two pair/set and will re-evaluate (likely folding to a large-sized raise); if he just calls I’ll continue based on the turn card.

I have top pair with an Ace-Jack (AJ) on a board of A-Q-9 with two spades. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. My opponent, Minni, has led out with a donk bet of 300 into a 600 pot. This 'probe' bet could indicate a weak Ace, a Queen, a flush draw, or even a straight draw (like JT). While my hand is strong, raising here might only get called by hands that beat me (like AQ, A9, Q9, or sets) and fold out the bluffs I want her to keep making. By calling, I maintain the pot's size, keep her range wide, and allow myself to re-evaluate the turn based on the card and her subsequent action. My total contribution to the pot after this call will be 600 (300 pre-flop + 300 flop).

I have top pair (A♣7♠) but a weak kicker and I’m out of position. The turn (T♣) completes deadly two-card combos (K–J makes a Broadway straight) and pairs the board (T) so my relative hand strength drops vs. the villain’s calling range. Flash called my half‑pot flop raise, then checked the turn — showing either weakness or a trap (two pair/straight). Betting here would extract from worse Qx/Tx and some weaker Aces, but it also invites a check‑raise from the exact hands that currently beat me (KJ, sets, two‑pair) and would force a difficult fold with a large portion of my stack. With deep stacks and a goal of pot control OOP, I’ll check back to keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and see a river where I can better price value calls or fold to aggression.

I have top pair (Aces) with a Jack kicker and a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway straight (needing a King). The board has become quite coordinated with the Ten of Clubs (9-A-Q-T). This card potentially completes straights for hands like KJ or J8, and improves hands like Q-T, A-T, or T-9 to two pair. Minni led out on the flop but has now checked the turn. While my hand is strong, the board is very 'wet' and betting might invite a check-raise from a range that now includes many hands that beat me. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity for free, and can evaluate the river action based on the final card.

I’ll check. I raised the flop half‑pot to protect and extract, checked the turn for pot control OOP, and the K on the river is a dangerous card — it completes KQ/AK two‑pair combos and makes Jx straights. Flash’s calling line (preflop open, called my flop raise, checked turn) is wide and includes worse aces and Qx that I can beat, but it also contains enough hands that now beat me (AK, AQ, KQ, sets, Jx straights) to make building a bigger pot risky. With 8.9k effective and only 1.2k in the pot, betting risks a check‑raise or a big call from better two‑pair/straights; a small blocker bet could get thin value but invites costly mistakes. Pot control and a showdown with my top pair + blocker is the lowest variance, highest EV line here, so I’ll check and show down rather than bloat the pot OOP.

On this river, I have completed the Broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-T) using the Jack of Hearts in my hand. This is the absolute nuts on this board, as no flushes or higher hands are possible. Minni has checked to me. To maximize value, I need to place a bet that she might call with a weaker hand like two pair or three of a kind, or even just a high card if she suspects a bluff. Since any Jack also makes the straight, there is a possibility of a split pot if she also holds one. I will bet 800 into the 1200 pot to extract value.

I hold top pair A with a weak 7 kicker on 9♦ A♠ Q♠ T♣ K♦. Flash checked the turn after calling my half‑pot flop bet, then makes a small river raise (800 into a 2000 pot). Calling 800 to win a 3600 pot requires ~22% equity. Heads‑up my A7 still has good showdown value and blocks several strong Ace combos (I hold A♣), while Flash’s line (call flop, check turn, small river bet) is consistent with a wide river raising range — thin value (AK, KQ, KJ, sets) plus a fair number of bluffs and worse A/Q hands. Raising OOP with a marginal kicker is high variance and likely to get punished by better hands; folding throws away too much equity. Given the pot odds, blockers and the line, calling is the +EV play. I will call the 800.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB). 5♦3♠ is a very weak hand, but calling is cheap: I only need to put in 50 more to make the total 100 and the pot will be 200 — pot odds 4:1 (need ~20% equity). In heads-up play and with position postflop, 5-3o often has enough equity to justify a call and lets me play the flop in position. A min-raise to 200 is unnecessarily bloating the pot with a marginal holding and folding surrenders my posted 50 too often. Plan: call, see the flop, c-bet or check/board-dependently fold to heavy action if I miss.

I have Ace-Seven offsuit in the big blind. Minni limped from the button. A7o is a strong hand in heads-up play and is likely ahead of a standard limping range. I will raise to 400 to build the pot and take the initiative in the hand.

I’m on the button (SB) and already put in 50. Flash raised to 400 so I must call 300 more (400 total). The pot is 500; calling makes it 800, so I’m getting ~2.67:1 (need ≈27% equity). 5♦3♠ has reasonable heads-up equity against a wide raising range and I have positional advantage postflop. The extra 300 is small versus my 8000 stack (~3.75%), so a call is a cheap and correct way to see a flop and exploit position. Plan: call, evaluate the flop and c-bet when favorable; control the pot or fold to heavy action on clearly hostile boards.

I have hit top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a relatively safe but somewhat coordinated board (7-6-2 with two hearts). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor after Minni's limp, I should continue betting for value and to protect against potential draws (straight or flush). A bet of 400 (50% of the pot) is appropriate to build the pot while keeping Minni's range wide enough to call with weaker hands.

Call. The pot is 1,200 and I must call 400 (calling makes the pot 1,600) so I’m getting ~4:1 (need ≈20% equity). I have a single‑gutshot to the 4 (4 outs) — roughly 16.4% to hit by the river — slightly below the raw pot‑odds threshold. However this is heads‑up with position and Flash’s small flop sizing indicates a wide c‑bet range; implied odds and post‑flop playability favor seeing the turn. 400 is a small fraction of my 7,700 stack (~5%), so I’ll call to realize equity and keep the pot manageable. If I hit the 4 I’ll extract value; if I miss I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and fold to large aggression. Raising to 800 as a bluff is an option but too risky with only a gutshot and limited fold equity, so I’ll just call and re‑assess.

The turn 9h is a very dangerous card for my hand. It completes potential heart flushes and several straight possibilities like 5-8. My flop top pair of 7s is now effectively middle pair with an overcard on board. While I hold the 7h, it is a weak blocker to a flush. Given I am out of position and the board has become extremely wet, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot and see how Minni reacts to the board texture change.

I missed my draw on the turn (5♦3♠ vs 7♠ 6♥ 2♥ 9♥). I only have a 4 as a clean river out (~4/46 ≈ 8.7%), so my raw equity to improve is tiny. Flash checked the turn after c-betting the flop — that looks like pot control with a lot of medium/strong hands or a check with a made heart/flop draw; a bluff here would be thin and vulnerable to getting called or check‑raised into a fast fold. I’ll control the pot and preserve chips: check and see the river, then only bet or bluff on very favorable runouts or fold to significant aggression.

On the river, the board shows four hearts (6h, 2h, 9h, 5h). I hold the 7 of hearts, giving me a 7-high flush. While this is a strong hand in most contexts, on a four-flush board, it is extremely vulnerable to any single heart higher than a 7 (Ah, Kh, Qh, Jh, Th, 8h). Minni checked back on the turn, which could indicate a lack of a flush at that point or a desire to control the pot with a marginal hand. By checking, I can evaluate Minni's response. If she checks behind, I likely have the best hand or she is also afraid of the board. If she bets, I will have to decide based on her sizing whether she is value betting a higher heart or bluffing with no heart at all. Given the risks of being out-kicked in the flush, checking is the most cautious and strategic play.

I have 5♦3♠ — a lone pair of fives on 7♠ 6♥ 2♥ 9♥ 5♥. That pair is very thin: the board has four hearts, so any single heart in Flash’s hand is a flush and beats me, and any 7/6/9 (or better) also beats my pair. Flash’s line (preflop raise, small flop c‑bet, then checking turn and river) is consistent with pot control with a made hand or a slow-played/controlled flush; it’s also consistent with a wide c‑bet range that can check back missed hands, so there’s ambiguity — but the risk of value‑betting is high because most hands that call a river bet here beat me. Betting risks getting called by all flushes and stronger pairs; a small bluff won’t fold enough and a large bet commits too many chips with a marginal holding. Stack preservation and exploitative play favor checking and folding to serious river aggression. I’ll check and fold to meaningful bets rather than turn this thin pair into a bluff or overvalue it.

I am on the Button with Five of Spades and Deuce of Diamonds. This is a very weak starting hand. While it is standard to open a wide range in heads-up play, 52o is at the bottom of the range and doesn't play well post-flop, especially against an opponent who has shown they are willing to defend and play aggressively. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be aggressive and open a very wide range. 7♦2♣ is garbage heads-up, but folding every marginal hand makes me exploitable — a min-raise to 200 is small relative to our deep stack and buys the pot a lot of the time while keeping postflop options open. Plan: if Flash folds we take down the 150 pot; if called we play postflop in position and give up to heavy resistance (fold to a large 3-bet), c-bet selectively on favorable textures and check/realize equity when out of position on dry boards. Raise to 200 to apply pressure and preserve fold equity while keeping the risk modest.

I am in the Big Blind with 94o. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the button. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call, 94o is a very weak hand to play out of position, even in a heads-up situation. Given my significant chip lead and the weakness of the hand, there is no reason to defend here and risk being outplayed post-flop.

Holding Ace-Five suited on the button in a heads-up situation is a premium starting hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure and build a pot with a hand that has significant equity and playability.

Facing a 300 open from the button (SB) while I’m in the big blind with T♠4♣. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 (total 300). Heads-up I should defend a fairly wide range, and this open size gives good pot odds: calling 200 to potentially win ~600 (~3:1) means I only need ~25% equity. T4o is marginal but has enough showdown and hit potential versus a wide opening range. I’ll avoid a 3-bet — this hand has poor blockers and a min 3-bet to 500 would be too thin to commit chips; folding is too tight in heads-up. Plan: call and proceed cautiously on the flop (fold to heavy continued aggression on bad runouts, continue with top pair or strong draws).

Heads-up, I called preflop and now have top pair (T♠) with a weak 4-kicker on a J♣ J♥ T♥ board. Villain checked to me, which can indicate weakness or a slowplay with a jack/overpair, but their opening range from the button is wide. There are almost no strong draws on this flop, so betting extracts value from worse tens and many broadway/medium hands that will call a small c-bet, while also denying free cards that could improve some hands. I don't want to inflate the pot with a marginal kicker, so I choose a small value bet (~1/3 pot) to get called by worse and fold out pure air. If Villain check-raises large, that will usually represent a very strong holding (Jx or a set) and I can fold; if they call I will proceed cautiously on the turn and fold to heavy aggression on dangerous runouts. Bet 200 into the 600 pot to achieve that plan.

I have Ace-high with a backdoor spade flush draw on a board of Th-Jc-Jh. Minni has led out for 200 into a pot of 600 (making the pot 800 total). This 'donk bet' could represent a Jack, a Ten, or a draw like KQ or 98. Given the pot odds (1:4) and the fact that I have position, I will call to see how the turn develops. My Ace-high still has significant showdown value against her bluffs and potential to improve.

Turn (Js) pairs the board — with T♠ I now have J J J T T: jacks full of tens, a very strong full house. The only holding that beats me is a Jack in Flash’s hand (quad J’s). Flash’s line (raise preflop, call my small flop bet, then check the turn) looks more like a non‑Jack calling range (pocket tens, Tx, broadway pairs, some Ax) than a slow‑played Jack. I should be extracting value rather than checking and giving a free river. A half‑pot to two‑thirds‑pot value bet gets called by worse full houses, sets, tens and many medium pairs while keeping bluffs and weaker hands in. I’ll bet modestly to maximize calls and avoid bloating the pot in case he does have the nut quad and tries to trap. Bet 600 into the 1000 pot. If he check‑raises very large I’ll re‑evaluate (large check‑raises are likely Jx); against a moderate raise I’ll consider calling for value.

The board has now tripled the Jacks (Jc, Jh, Js). I have Ace-high (As 5s), which currently plays as full house, Jacks full of Aces (J-J-J-A-T). Minni has led out for 600 into 1000. If Minni has the fourth Jack, I am drawing dead. If she has a Ten, her hand is J-J-J-T-T (Full House), whereas mine is J-J-J-A-T (Full House). My Ace-high is actually the best possible full house besides a pocket pair of Tens or better, or the case Jack. Given that I have the Ace of Spades, I am beating any other Ace-high that doesn't have a Ten or a pocket pair. This board state makes it very likely we both have a full house, but mine with an Ace kicker is extremely strong. I will call to keep the pot manageable and see the river, as raising might only get called by hands that beat me (like a Ten or the last Jack).

River: board Th Jc Jh Js Ah. My hand Ts4c = Jacks full of Tens (JJJTT) — a very strong second‑nut full house. The only hands that beat me are a Jack in Flash’s hand (the lone remaining jack → quads) or any Ace (JJJAA), and with three jacks on board + the river Ace the combinations that can beat me are non‑negligible (~15–18% unconditional; conditional on Flash calling flop & turn it's lower but still meaningful). Flash called both my flop and turn bets, which leans his range toward Tens and medium pairs (hands I beat) rather than an unpaired Ace, but an Ace or the one remaining Jack are still possible. With a 6.35k effective stack and a 31k opponent, I don't want to inflate the pot where a check‑raise or a value raise from a locking hand (A or J) can cost me a large chunk. Optimal play is pot control: check the river to induce bluffs and to realize equity vs weaker holdings, and call small/medium river bets for value but fold to a large shove that represents quads/A‑full. Checking preserves stack and extracts value from worse hands that will often bet or check‑call the river, while avoiding getting peeled off by the rare nut holdings.

On the river, the board is [Th Jc Jh Js Ah]. My best hand is Jacks full of Aces (Js-Jh-Jc-As-Ah). This is an extremely strong hand, only losing to the case Jack (Jd) for quads or pocket Aces for Aces full of Jacks. Minni led out on the flop and turn but checked the river once the Ace appeared. This check suggests she might have been betting a Jack previously and is now wary of the Ace, or more likely, she had a Ten or a straight draw and the Ace is a 'scare card' for her. Since I have an Ace, I am either splitting the pot with another Ace or beating any hand she has that isn't the Jd or AA. While I could bet for value against a Ten, the risk of a check-raise from the quad-Jack or the likelihood of a split pot against another Ace makes checking back the most prudent play to guarantee a showdown and claim my share of the pot.

Heads-up on the button with 8♥7♥ — a very playable, suited connector with strong post-flop potential and good blocking/flush equity. In heads-up play I want initiative and to play in position, so I will raise to apply pressure and build a pot when I hit. A raise to 300 (≈3x BB) is standard here: it gives solid fold equity, is small relative to my 6,300 stack, and keeps the SPR reasonable for realizing equity postflop. If Flash calls I'll use position to c-bet favorable textures (especially hearts and connected boards) and control pot size on dry/unfavorable boards. If Flash 3-bets small I'll consider calling to play post-flop; I will fold to very large 4-bets. Raise to 300 to take the lead and maximize expected value with this hand.

I am holding a pocket pair of deuces in the Big Blind. Minni, who has been quite active and currently has about 60 big blinds left, has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. While pocket deuces are a small pair, they are ahead of many hands in a heads-up range. However, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling to see a flop is the most prudent move here; I can look to hit a set or potentially win if the board runs out favorably without too much pressure.

I have flopped bottom set (deuces) on a very dry board (2-9-4 rainbow). This is an incredibly strong hand. Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and has shown a tendency to be active. On such a dry board, leading out (donk betting) might scare her off her range of overcards or air. Checking is the most effective way to induce a continuation bet from her, allowing me to either slowplay or check-raise to build the pot against her remaining stack.

I opened preflop and have position; flop 2♦ 9♥ 4♠ is very dry with a single heart. Holding 8♥7♥ I have no pair but decent semi-bluff equity (backdoor heart + runner-runner straight possibilities) and I can leverage position to take the pot. A continuation bet here denies equity to many overcards and small pairs, and often wins the pot immediately. I'll size to ~1/2 pot to apply pressure without bloating the pot with a marginal hand. If called I'll re-evaluate on the turn and only continue aggressively if I improve or the turn is favorable; fold to a large raise since my showdown value is low.

I have flopped a bottom set of deuces on a very dry board (2-9-4 rainbow). At this point, I have the virtual nuts, as it's unlikely Minni has 99 or 44 given the pre-flop action. Minni is the pre-flop aggressor and has just continuation bet 300 into a 600 pot. While I want to build a pot, a check-raise here would look extremely strong and likely force her to fold her overcards or air. Given her relatively short stack of approximately 57 big blinds, I can easily get the rest of her chips in on the turn and river. I will call to keep her range wide and encourage her to continue bluffing or value betting a hand like top pair on the turn.

I have bottom set (deuces) on a board of 2d-9h-4s-6s. This is an extremely strong hand. I checked and called on the flop to keep Minni's range wide. On the turn, the 6s doesn't significantly change the board texture, though it does introduce some straight draws and a backdoor spade draw. Since I am out of position and Minni has been the aggressor, I will check again to induce a second barrel from her bluffs or marginal hands like top pair. With her stack at approximately 57 big blinds, I want to keep the pot growing without scaring her off by leading out.

Preflop I opened from the button and took the lead; on the flop I c-bet and Flash called, which narrows his range to lots of one-pair hands, small pocket pairs, and some floats. The turn (6♠) actually improves my equity: with 8♥7♥ I now have an open-ended straight draw (5s or 10s complete) plus occasional pair-outs. Heads-up aggression is profitable here — a turn semi-bluff both builds the pot when I hit and often folds out weaker hands/overcards that called the flop.

Sizing: the pot is ~1,200; I’ll bet roughly half-pot (~600). That size buys fold equity versus many of Flash’s marginal calls while keeping the pot controllable if I’m called. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I still have ~8 outs to a straight and can re-evaluate the river. If he raises large I’ll give it up — I’m defending a draw, not a made hand.

I have bottom set on a board that is still relatively dry, although the six of spades adds some potential straight draws (7-8, 5-3) and a backdoor flush draw. Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand, betting both the flop and the turn. Given her previous history of folding to raises (Hands #90, #92, #93) and her overall aggressive post-flop style, calling here is the most effective way to keep her entire range of bluffs and marginal hands (like a pair of nines or overcards) in the pot. By just calling, I allow her to potentially fire a third barrel on the river, maximizing the value of my set against her remaining stack of approximately 4,500 chips. A raise now would likely only be called by hands that have me beat or have significant equity, whereas calling induces further aggression from her bluffs.

I have bottom set (deuces) on a board that has become slightly more coordinated with three spades and potential straight draws. Throughout the hand, I have played passively (checking and calling) to keep Minni's range wide and induce bluffs. On the river, the Queen of spades is an overcard that might have helped her range if she was betting overcards or has a hand like AQ/KQ. By checking now, I allow her to continue her aggressive line and fire a third barrel with her bluffs or value bet with a marginal hand. If she bets, I will have a decision on whether to just call or raise, but checking is the best move to maximize the chance of her putting more chips in the pot.

The river Q♠ is a complete miss for my 8♥7♥ — I never pair and I have no spade blockers. Flash called my flop and turn bets, which narrows him heavily toward one-pair hands, small pocket pairs and spade draws. The Q♠ on the river also completes many spade draws that profitably call or check–raise; bluffing into that range without blockers is very thin. Pot is 2,400; to make a bluff profitable I’d need an implausibly high fold frequency from a player who has shown willingness to call multiple streets. Betting here risks a sizable portion of my 5,150 stack for a low chance of forcing a fold. I’ll protect my stack and go to showdown (or fold to a lead) by checking. If Flash now bets a small probe I can decide based on sizing, but I will not fire a pure river bluff into the completed spade board.

I am on the button with 6d 8d, which is a solid hand for heads-up play. My opponent, Minni, is relatively short-stacked with about 50 big blinds. I have been consistently opening for 300 from the button in previous hands to maintain pressure and build the pot with playable hands. I will continue this strategy here.

Facing a 300 open from the button while I’m in the big blind with 4♦8♠. I’ve already got 100 in; calling 200 more (300 total) gives me ~3:1 pot odds (need ≈25% equity). In heads‑up the button opens very wide, so this hand has enough equity to defend cheaply. I’ll call to see a flop and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards since I’m out of position and the hand plays poorly postflop; if I pair or pick up a strong draw I’ll continue, otherwise I’ll release the hand vs significant pressure. I won’t 3‑bet as a bluff with this weak offsuit holding — better to defend and keep pot control.

Check. I only have a single eight on Jc Tc 8c and I’m out of position against a very deep, wide-opening Button. This is a marginal hand — it has showdown value but is easily dominated by Jx/Tx and vulnerable to straights/flushes. I’ll keep the pot small and see a turn: call small c-bets (≈≤1/3 pot) to realize equity, fold to large pressure or shove unless I improve to two pair/strong draw. Preserving my 4,850 stack versus his huge stack is the priority here, so pot control is optimal.

On a highly coordinated board of Jc-Th-8c, my bottom pair (8s) is quite vulnerable. Minni has a relatively short stack, and this board texture hits a lot of her defending range (any Jack, Ten, Nine, or Queen, plus flush draws). By checking back, I control the size of the pot and allow myself to realize equity or see if the turn improves my hand or clarifies Minni's strength without risking a check-raise on this wet texture.

Check. I have only middle pair (pair of 8s with a 4 kicker) and I’m out of position against a very deep, wide-opening Button. The turn 7 completes many straight combos for any 9 in his range and leaves me vulnerable to Jx/T x, better 8x kickers, sets, and straights. Flash checked the flop, showing pot-control/weakness, so there’s no need to bloat the pot and risk a big raise from a deeper stack. I’ll keep the pot small, see the river, and call small bets for showdown value but fold to large aggression — preserve my ~4.85k stack rather than get committed with a marginal hand.

On the turn, the board is 8c-Jc-Th-7d. I have a pair of eights and a gutshot straight draw (needing a nine for a 7-8-9-10-J straight). Minni has checked twice. While this board is very coordinated and likely hits her range, her second check suggests she might be giving up or playing a marginal hand cautiously. However, since my pair of eights has some showdown value but is vulnerable, and I have a draw, checking back again to see a free river is a low-risk way to realize my equity or reach showdown without bloating the pot on a dangerous board.

I hold only a single pair (8s) with a 4 kicker on Jc Tc 8c 7d Qh. Flash checked the flop and turn and checked back the river — showing a passive line that can be missed draws or medium pairs, but his wide opening range heads‑up contains many hands that beat me (Qx, Jx, Tx, 9x straights, sets). A river bet would risk getting raised or getting value‑called by hands that already beat me while rarely folding better hands; there’s minimal thin value to extract. Pot control and stack preservation are priority with this marginal hand, so I’ll check to take showdown rather than bloat the pot or bluff into a likely stronger range.

On the river, the board is 8c-Jc-Th-7d-Qh. My pair of eights is now the fourth pair on an extremely connected board where any 9 or K9 makes a straight. Minni has checked for the third time, which suggests she either has a very weak hand, a missed draw, or is potentially trapping with a straight. Given the board texture, my pair of eights has very thin showdown value, but it is unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call if I bet. Checking back is the most prudent play to reach showdown and realize whatever equity I have in this 600-chip pot without risking a check-raise.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) — T5s is a suited, playable hand with good postflop playability and blockers. In heads-up play I should be opening a wide range from the button to generate fold equity and play pots in position. A standard 2x BB open to 200 denies free flops, keeps the pot manageable with my ~51BB stack, and puts pressure on Flash’s wide BB defending range. If he 3-bets big I can fold this marginal hand; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet or realize equity on spade/ten draws. Raise to 200 (total).

I am in the Big Blind with Td 2d. Minni has raised to 200 from the small blind/button. Minni is quite short-stacked with only 4950 remaining. While T2 offsuit is a weak hand, it is only 100 more to call into a 300 pot (offering 3:1 odds). However, against a 2x raise from a short stack that has been playing somewhat aggressively, this hand is at the bottom of the range and will be difficult to play out of position. Given the stack sizes and the hand quality, I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my significant chip lead.

I am on the button with 8h6h, a decent suited connector. My opponent, Minni, is playing with a relatively short stack (5150 chips compared to the blinds of 50/100). Standard strategy in heads-up play from the button is to raise a wide range of hands, especially suited connectors, to put pressure on the big blind. A raise to 300 (3x) is a standard opening size.

I'm the big blind with A♣4♠ facing a button raise to 300. Calling costs me 200 more (I already have 100 in) for a total commitment of 300 and the pot will be ~600 — so my break-even win % is ~33%. In heads-up play the button's open will be wide, and A4o has reasonable equity versus that range, so folding would be too nitty. A small 3-bet to the min (500) is unattractive: it commits extra chips out of position and can be exploited by a deeper-stacked, aggressive opponent with 3-bets or flats. I prefer to call, see a flop, and proceed cautiously OOP — continue with top-pair/ace or use position reads to fold to heavy action if I miss.

Check. I'm OOP with A♣4♠ on 6♦-8♣-9♠ — I missed the flop and only have ace-high plus a remote backdoor club/fold equity. Flash opened wide from the button, so many of his hands will be worse than a made pair, but out of position I don't want to bloat the pot with no pair or clean draw. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize showdown equity, and induces bluffs from his wide continuation range. Plan: if he makes a small c-bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈200) I will call to see the turn; fold to large, committed bets (> ~1/2 pot) unless I improve; if he checks back I can take a free card and evaluate turn. I will avoid bluff-raising OOP with just A-high.

On a flop of 6d-8c-9s, I have flopped two pair (Eights and Sixes). This is a strong hand, but the board is very coordinated, with many potential straight draws available for my opponent (any 7 makes a straight, and cards like 5, 10, or Jack also provide straight potential). Since Minni checked, I should bet for value and protection to charge any draws or weaker pairs. I will bet 400 into the 600 pot.

Call 400. I'm OOP with A♣4♠ on 6♦-8♣-9♠; I checked and Flash bets 400 into a 1000 pot. Calling 400 into the pot-if-called (P after call = 1800) gives me a break-even equity of ~22%, and the call is only ~8% of my 4,950 stack — cheap enough to see a turn. Versus a heads-up button opening range there are a lot of missed overcards, weaker K/Q hands and floats that I beat at showdown, so my ace-high has reasonable equity above the price. I have no realistic flush/straight draw here (one club in hand + one on board cannot make a flush by river), so raising OOP is a poor play and folding would be too nitty given the pot odds. Plan: call to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn — if I pair an ace or villain checks I can proceed; if I miss and face a large (committing) barrel on the turn I will fold.

Flashed checked the turn after c-betting the flop — that looks like weakness or a marginal hand. I have A♣4♠ with no pair, so I don’t want to bloat a pot OOP with nothing, but a small turn stab can often pick up the pot right now against missed overcards, floats and random bluffs in his wide opening range. Pot = 1,400; a bet of 450 (~1/3 pot) requires him to fold ≈24% to be +EV (450/(1400+450)=~24%). That’s an attainable fold-frequency against a player who checked the turn. If he folds I win the pot now; if he calls I can still realize showdown equity cheaply and re-evaluate river (bet for value if I pair an ace, check/call light or fold to big raises). If he raises significantly I will fold — I won’t commit more with no pair. Small aggressive probe now maximizes fold equity while keeping risk limited, so I bet 450.

I have flopped two pair (Eights and Sixes) on a coordinated board. Minni has led into me for a small amount (450 into 1400) on the Turn after calling my Flop bet. This 'donk bet' often indicates a marginal made hand or a draw. Given the straight possibilities (any 7 makes a straight), I need to raise for value and protection. I want to charge her draws and extract value from top-pair hands like 9x. With her relatively short stack of 4100, raising now sets up a potential shove on the river.

I bet 450 on the turn and Flash responded by raising me to 1,350, so I must call 900 into a pot of 3,200. Calling 900 requires ~22% equity (900/(3200+900) ≈ 22%). With A♣4♠ on 6♦‑8♣‑9♠‑3♦ my realistic outs are only the three remaining aces and three remaining fours (6 outs) — about 6/46 ≈ 13% to pair by the river. That equity is well below the break‑even requirement. Flash’s line (flop c‑bet, check turn, then raise my turn probe) is consistent with a strong or polarized range (pairs, sets, straights or a committed value hand), so I should not turn this into a big bluff by raising OOP against a much deeper stack. Preserving my stack for better spots and avoiding a poor‑odds call is the highest EV play here, so I fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 6c3h — a weak holding, but in heads-up position is very valuable and I should open very wide. I don't want to just limp and give the big blind a free, cheap flop or allow them to dictate play. I also don't want to jam 40+BB with a marginal hand. I raise to 300 total (3x BB) to maximize fold equity, keep the pot manageable, and retain postflop initiative. If called I'll play the flop based on texture (c-bet dry boards, proceed cautiously on coordinated/overcard boards and fold to heavy aggression when I have no equity).

I am holding 2h 6d in the Big Blind. Minni, who is short-stacked with only 3,800 chips, has raised to 300 from the Button. While I have a massive chip lead overall, 2-6 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially against a short stack where the SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) will be low. There is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand and potentially give the opponent a double-up opportunity.

I am on the button with T7o. Minni is in the big blind with a relatively short stack of 4,100 chips. Given the stack sizes and the head-up nature of this situation, T7o is a reasonable hand to open for a raise to apply pressure on the short stack. Raising is standard here to try and take down the blinds or see a flop in a position of strength.

Facing a standard BTN open to 300 while I'm in the BB with 6h4h. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total). Heads-up the button opens very wide, and 6h4h has decent playability (backdoor hearts and straight potential). With ~41bb effective, I don't want to 3-bet OOP with a weak hand — a reraise is thin and will put me in difficult spots, and folding is too tight given the price. Calling keeps the pot controlled, gives me correct pot odds (2:1) to see a flop, and lets me proceed cautiously postflop (check/fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, continue when I hit or pick up good draws). I will call (commit 300 total).

Check. I have 6♥4♥ on 5♥ 4♠ 8♥ — currently a small pair (pair of 4s) plus a very strong heart flush draw and a straight-runout to any 7. Unique improvement outs on the turn: 9 hearts + 3 non-heart 7s + 2 remaining 4s = 14 unique outs (≈14/47 ≈ 30% to hit on the turn, ≈51% to hit by the river). That gives me lots of equity vs a wide BTN opening. Betting here would fold out many worse hands and charge my own draws; I prefer to keep the pot controlled and let the preflop raiser c‑bet a wide range so I can call with good equity. Plan: check and call small-to-medium continuation bets, fold to very large polarized overbets if the action suggests only value hands, and consider a check‑raise as an occasional mix. If Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate the turn and either take a free card or fire a small stab when appropriate. For now, check and realize equity.

On a board of 5h-4s-8h, I have an inside straight draw (needing a six) and one overcard (the Ten). Minni checked to me. This is a good board to continuation bet (C-bet) because it favors a wide opening range from the button, and my gutshot + overcard provides some equity if called. I'll bet half the pot to test her strength and try to take it down immediately.

I call 300. I have 6♥4♥ on 5♥ 4♠ 8♥ — a made pair of 4s plus a very strong heart flush draw and a straight possibility (a 7 completes 4-5-6-7-8). Unique outs: 9 remaining hearts + 2 remaining 4s + 3 non-heart 7s = 14 outs (~30% to hit the turn, ~51% to hit by the river). Calling 300 into the current pot gives roughly 25% break-even equity, so my combined pair+draw equity is more than enough to continue. The BTN’s 50% pot c-bet looks like a standard continuation on a wet board; I’d rather keep the pot controlled and realise equity OOP than thin‑value/commit with a raise. Plan: call now, re-evaluate on the turn — extract value/raise if I hit (flush/trips/straight), call small bets, and fold to very large polarized overbets on blank turns.

I now have 6h4h on 5h 4s 8h 4d — that’s trips (three 4s) plus a strong heart draw (one heart away). Heads‑up vs a wide BTN continuation range this is a very strong holding. Pot = 1,200 and I’m first to act on the turn, so I should be building the pot and charging the many hearts/straight draws that will call. Checking risks giving a free river or letting weaker hands escape; a value/protection bet is best. I size to ~2/3 pot (800) — big enough to charge draws and get called by worse pairs/top‑pair + draw combos, but not so large that I fold out too many callers or commit myself unnecessarily. If Flash raises very large I’ll re‑evaluate (call smaller raises for the odds, but be cautious vs polarized overbets representing full houses/quads).

Minni has led into me on the turn after the board paired (4d). My hand, T7o, is currently just a Ten-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6). The pot is 2000, and Minni has about 2800 left in her stack. Calling 800 requires approximately 28.5% equity, but a gutshot only hits about 8.7% of the time on the river. Given that Minni called the flop and is now leading the turn, it is highly likely she has at least a pair or potentially trips. Since I do not have the proper odds to chase the draw and I am significantly ahead in the overall match, the most prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button, open-raising is standard. I have T♥2♥ — not a premium but suited with a ten-blocker and decent postflop playability. The pot is 150 and calling costs only 50 more, but limp-calling is passive and invites pressure/3-bets. A min-open to 200 (2x BB) gives me initiative and fold equity, keeps my range wide, and lets me play the flop in position. With ~47.5BB I can continue or fold to big 3-bets as needed. Standard, small heads-up raise to steal the blind and take control of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qc 3c, which is a decent suited hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has raised to 200 from the small blind (button). The pot is 300 and I only need to call 100 more. Given the pot odds (3:1) and the playability of suited queens, this is a standard defend. Minni has a relatively short stack (4600), so I should be careful about pot commitment later, but calling pre-flop is the most appropriate action.

On this flop of Ac-Kc-8s, I have missed completely with my Qc-3c. I have a backdoor flush draw, but the board contains two higher cards (Ace and King) that are very likely to have hit Minni's opening range. Since I am out of position and Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play. I will evaluate based on her action, but I'm prepared to fold to most significant bets given the lack of direct improvement.

Preflop I opened to 200 and got called; pot is 400. Flop Kc 8s Ad — I have T♥2♥, a complete miss with only backdoor heart/straight potential. Heads-up in position my continuation-bet frequency should be high: my raising range contains lots of broadways and bluffs, and Flash’s defending range from the big blind will include many unpaired hands, Kx, and floats that I can fold out. I’ll fire a modest c-bet to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable against likely Ax/Kx holdings. Betting 150 (~37.5% pot) preserves fold equity, leaves room to fold to a big check-raise, and allows us to continue if called and the turn is favorable. If Flash check-raises big on the flop I will fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (check for pot control on blanks, barrel selectively if favorable).

On a board of Ace-King-Eight, Minni is very likely to have connected with her pre-flop raising range. I have Queen-high with only a backdoor flush draw and virtually no showdown value. Even though the bet size is small (150 into 400), my hand has very little equity to improve to the best hand, and I am out of position. It is better to fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with K8o. Minni has a relatively short stack of 4,900 chips compared to the blinds (50/100). Standard procedure is to open for a raise to apply pressure and pick up the blinds. Since I am the small blind/button in this heads-up/short-handed scenario, raising is the most aggressive and profitable long-term play with a King-high hand.

I’m in the big blind with A♥J♥ facing a standard BTN open to 300. AJs is a strong, playable heads-up hand with suit and Ace-blockers vs a wide button opening range. With ~49BB effective, a small 3-bet to 500 (min-raise) achieves multiple goals: takes the initiative, builds the pot when I’m likely ahead of many of his opens, and preserves fold equity while keeping the pot controllable. Plan: if called I’ll continuation-bet favorable A-high or heart-containing flops and control the pot on dry boards; if Flash 4-bets large (very heavy sizing ~1500+), I’ll fold to avoid playing deep-pot dominated spots. Raise to 500 to exploit his wide opening range and maximize expected value.

Minni's raise to 500 is a very small click-back 3-bet. With King-Eight offsuit, I have extremely favorable pot odds, needing to call only 200 more into an 800 pot. Given I have position for the rest of the hand and the price is so cheap, folding would be a mistake. I will call and evaluate the flop.

I opened the action preflop and was called, now OOP on a very dry A‑high flop (Ac 2s 7c) with top pair, good kicker (AhJh). This is a textbook spot to take the initiative and bet for value: Flash's continuing range contains many worse A‑x hands, small pairs and broadways that will call a reasonably sized c‑bet, and there are virtually no strong draws to worry about. I want a size that extracts value while keeping the pot controllable with ~45bb effective. Bet ~40% pot (400 into 1000) to get value from worse Aces/pairs and deny free cards. If he flats I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously; if he check‑raises very large (represents sets/boats) I’ll fold or narrowly call depending on sizing and read.

On this Ace-high board (Ac 2s 7c), Minni's lead of 400 into a 1000 pot after a pre-flop 3-bet strongly suggests she has an Ace or is continuing with a range that hits this board much harder than mine. Holding only King-high (Kd 8h) with no real draw or backdoor equity, there is very little reason to continue. While the pot odds are roughly 3.5:1, I am essentially drawing to three Kings at best, and calling would likely require me to fold to further aggression on the turn. Since I have no pair and no draw, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

Heads-up on the button (I am the SB) with 5♦8♦ suited. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to complete — but in heads-up I should be opening a wide range to take the initiative and exploit position postflop. 5♦8♦ has suited and straight potential and plays well postflop in position against a single opponent. I will raise to the minimum allowed open (200 total) to build a pot when I have position, get some fold equity, and keep the opponent’s calling range wide and manageable. Plan: if Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold (hand is marginal vs a big 3-bet); if he 3-bets small I’ll consider a call to realize equity; if he calls I’ll c-bet appropriate flops (bet with diamonds/connected flops and check/float on dry boards).

I am in the Big Blind with Jd5h. Minni, who has a relatively short stack of 5300, has raised to 200 (a min-raise). My hand is quite weak and does not play particularly well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the pot odds, I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, which is tempting, but with J-high trashy offsuit cards, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot to exploit her stack size rather than bleeding chips in marginal situations.

I am on the Button with a pocket pair of sixes (6h 6s). Minni has a relatively short stack of 5500. Pocket sixes are a strong heads-up hand. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure, as is standard play for a small pocket pair on the button.

Heads-up vs Flash who opened to 300 from the SB. I’m in the BB with J♠7♠ and 5,500 (≈55bb). J7s is a playable, suited hand in heads-up and deserves a defense — it has decent equity and postflop playability. Calling 200 more (300 total) keeps the pot manageable and preserves stack depth; I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand by 3-betting. Postflop I’ll proceed cautiously: continue with pairs, strong draws or backdoor combos; fold to heavy pressure on dry boards. Calling maximizes my long-run EV here.

I have top pair (J♠) with a weak 7 kicker on an A–Q–J rainbow. Flash opened wide from the SB in heads‑up, so his range contains many A and Q combos that dominate me. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal top pair by leading; checking keeps the pot controlled, lets me gather information, and allows me to call a reasonable c‑bet to realize equity against worse Jx/Kx/Tx hands. Plan: check here; if Flash bets a standard c‑bet I will call and re-evaluate on the turn (folding to heavy pressure on bad turns or double barrels that strongly represent A/Q), and if he checks back I can consider a delayed turn or river bet for value on safe runouts.

The flop of Ace, Queen, and Jack is very coordinated and favors a lot of the broadway cards that my opponent might be calling with from the Big Blind. My pocket sixes are now an underpair with almost no equity against a made hand and very little chance of improving. Since Minni checked, I will check behind to keep the pot small and see a turn for free, as a continuation bet is unlikely to force a fold from any Ace, Queen, Jack, or Ten.

Board: A♣ Q♦ J♥ 8♠. I have top pair (J♠) with a weak 7 kicker, out of position vs the preflop raiser who checked both flop and turn. Flash’s check-check line is consistent with pot control with Ax/Qx or with missed/medium hands — a lot of his range actually dominates me. Betting the turn only charges hands that are often behind (thin value) while getting called by A/Q hands that beat me or risking a raise out of position. With effective stacks large relative to the pot and no strong redraws, the +EV play is to keep the pot small and realize equity. I’ll check and evaluate the river: call small-to-medium river bets for value or fold to very large, polarized shoves.

The board is heavily coordinated with broadway cards (Ace, Queen, Jack), which hit much of my opponent's calling range. My pocket sixes are currently an underpair. Minni has checked both the flop and the turn. While she might be weak, my hand has marginal showdown value against her 'air' and very little equity against anything that connected with the board. Betting here is unlikely to fold out a better hand given her stack size and the board texture, and checking allows me to get to a cheap showdown or evaluate the river. I will check back.

Check. I have top pair (J♠) with a very weak 7 kicker on an A–Q–J board; the river pairing of 8s doesn’t help me and actually brings in more possible two‑pair/boat/8x combos for the preflop raiser. Flash checked flop and turn, which is consistent with pot control with A/Q or with a lot of hands that already beat me; a thin value bet now would largely fold out worse hands and get called or raised by hands that beat me (Ax, Qx or better Jx). The pot is only 600 and my stack is deep enough to preserve postflop options, so the lowest-variance +EV line is to check to showdown. If Flash now bets/raises big over my check I’ll fold to polarized river pressure because his range is weighted to hands that beat my J7.

The board is highly coordinated (A-Q-J-8-8) and heavily favors any broadway cards my opponent might be holding. My pocket sixes are currently playing as two pair (8s and 6s with an Ace kicker). Since Minni has checked on every street, she may have a weak hand or missed entirely. However, my hand has marginal showdown value, and betting is unlikely to fold out any hand that beats me (A, Q, J, or any 8) or get called by anything worse. I will check behind to take the hand to showdown.

Heads-up on the button with QhJh suited — a strong, playable hand with good straight/flush potential and useful blockers to broadway combos. I have position and a healthy stack (≈58BB), so I want to take the initiative and deny the BB cheap equity. Min-raise to 200 is too small (gives too good odds to call); I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to build the pot, extract value from worse Kx/Ax and broadway hands, and charge draws. If Flash flats I’ll use position to play postflop aggressively (c-bet many textures); if he 3-bets lightly I’ll call and continue postflop; I’ll fold to an over-aggressive shove that commits most of my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 3c 8s, a weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. Minni is quite short-stacked with only 5600 chips (about 56 big blinds). Given my weak hand and the fact that I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand, there's no reason to call or defend here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 7c 4c, a speculative suited hand. Minni is in the big blind with a relatively short stack of 5900 compared to the blinds (59 BB). Standard strategy from the button is to raise a wide range, including suited connectors/one-gappers, to put pressure on the blinds and take initiative. A standard 3x raise is 300.

Flash (SB) opened to 300 heads-up. I’m in the big blind with Qh4s — a marginal offsuit queen with very poor post‑flop playability out of position. Calling 200 into a 400 pot requires ~33% equity, but versus a standard SB opening range my hand is often dominated and won’t realize that equity profitably OOP facing frequent c‑bets. 3‑betting as a bluff with Q4o is weak (few blockers) and the min‑raise to 500 would just bloat the pot out of position against a deeper, aggressive opponent. With a healthy 59BB stack I’ll preserve chips and fold this marginal hand preflop.

Heads-up on the button with 4♣5♦ — a marginal but playable, connected hand that benefits from position. With ~58BB I want to take the initiative and open a wide range instead of checking/calling; a standard 2xBB (min) raise balances fold equity and keeps the pot manageable. Raise to 200 to pressure the big blind; if Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold, if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on bad boards.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 7s. Minni, on the button, has raised to 200. It's a small raise, and I'm getting 3-to-1 odds to call. This hand has decent playability heads-up, and while it's not a powerhouse, defending the blind here is standard against a button open, especially with the favorable pot odds.

On a flop of 6h-9s-6c, I have an open-ended straight draw (7 and 8). The board is paired, which reduces the likelihood of Minni having a monster unless she has a 6 or 9. Given I'm out of position and Minni has a relatively short stack compared to the pot, checking to see how she reacts is the most prudent move. I have solid equity with the straight draw and can evaluate based on her bet sizing.

I opened a wide range from the button preflop, so my range is strong enough to continuation-bet here and the big blind’s check shows weakness. My actual hand (4♣5♦) completely missed the 6♥9♠6♣ flop — only tiny backdoor equity — so I don’t want to build a big pot. I’ll fire a small C-bet ~1/3 pot (150 into 400) to take the pot down against overcards and other unpaired hands, while keeping the pot manageable if I get called. If Flash folds I win; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn and only continue with real improvement or a favorable card, and I’ll fold to a substantive flop check-raise since I have no pair.

I have an open-ended straight draw on a 6h-9s-6c board. Minni's 150 bet into a 400 pot is small (roughly 37% pot). With 8-high and a straight draw, I have enough equity to call and see a turn. Since Minni's stack is relatively short (5550), I don't want to bloat the pot by raising and possibly getting shoved on, but I certainly want to realize my equity and potentially catch a 7 or 10 on the turn.

I am out of position with an open-ended straight draw (any 7 or Jack completes it) on a paired board. Minni's small continuation bet on the flop suggests she might have a marginal hand or is testing the waters. The 10 on the turn is a good card for my drawing range but doesn't improve my current 8-high. Checking is the most flexible play here; it allows me to see how Minni reacts to the turn and potentially realize my equity for free if she checks behind, or call a reasonable bet given the pot odds and my 8 outs.

I missed this turn. I opened wide preflop and fired a small flop c-bet (150) to take down lots of flats — Flash called, showing either a weak pair, a float, or a slow-played hand. The turn (T♥) doesn’t improve 4♣5♦ — I only have 6 bare pair-outs (~13% to pair on the river) and no real redraw to a straight. With pot = 700 and deep stacks, I don’t want to commit more chips with almost no equity. Flash checking the turn looks like weakness or a call-down range that will gladly call a bluff, and a second barrel here would require a balanced range (value + bluffs) I don’t have. I’ll check back to control the pot, preserve my stack, and re-evaluate on the river — folding to meaningful aggression on the turn or river unless I pick up a real improvement or a very cheap bet to call.

On the river, the board is 6h-9s-6c-Th-7h. I have 8h-7s, giving me a straight (6-7-8-9-10). While the river 7h completes a possible heart flush and there are higher straight possibilities if Minni holds a Jack, a straight remains a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni checked back the turn, suggesting she might not have a monster. I will bet for value, hoping to get called by a 9, a 10, or trips (a 6). A bet of 500 into a 700 pot is a standard value size to extract chips from medium-strength hands.

I have 4♣5♦ on 6♥ 9♠ 6♣ T♥ 7♥ — I missed everything (no pair, no straight, no flush). Preflop I opened a wide range and c-bet the flop, but Flash’s line (call flop, check turn, raise 500 into a 700 pot on the river) is exactly the sort of line that gets rewarded: river completes T/7/78 straights and gives two-heart combos a flush. To call 500 into a 700 pot I need ~41.7% equity (500/1200). My hand has essentially zero showdown equity versus the sensible value portion of his range and only beats pure air/bluffs. With 55bb effective I should preserve my stack and not hero-call a large river bet with no showdown value. Fold.

I have JTo on the button (SB) against Minni in the Big Blind. JTo is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. My stack is significantly larger than Minni's, but the goal is to maximize value. A standard open raise to 300 is appropriate to build the pot and exert pressure.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300, I’m in the big blind with A♠6♦ (A6o). Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more; calling puts 300 total in the pot. With 54 BB effective this is a clear defend — A6 blocks many of the button’s strong A-combos and has good showdown value against a wide opening range. Pot-odds are attractive (risk 200 to win ~600 → need ~33% equity), so defending is +EV. 3-betting OOP with A6o is marginal (not strong enough for a clean value 3-bet and creates difficult postflop spots), so I’ll call to see a flop and proceed based on texture: play aggressively when I hit an Ace or get favorable draws, otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to heavy pressure.

Heads‑up on a very dry flop (6♠‑8♦‑3♣). I hold A♥6♦ — a made middle pair with the best possible Ace kicker. Versus a wide button open this hand is ahead of most unpaired overcards, many broadways and smaller pocket pairs, and only really behind 8x/3x and bigger pocket pairs. Betting takes the initiative, extracts value from worse (A‑highs, Kx, small pairs) and charges the few backdoor draws. I’ll make a standard half‑pot value/protection bet (300). If Flash calls I’ll evaluate the turn; if he raises big I’ll give him credit for a very strong hand (8x/set) and proceed cautiously (call small‑to‑medium raises to realize equity, fold to an overcommitting shove).

I have Jack-Ten offsuit (Jh Jd) and the flop is 6s 8d 3c. This board is relatively dry. Minni, who is in the Big Blind and has a relatively small stack (~50 BBs), has led out for 300 (a 'donk' bet) into a 600 pot. This often represents a marginal hand like a mid-pair, a straight draw (like 79 or 57), or sometimes a complete air bluff. Since I have two overcards and a back-door straight draw, I am not ready to fold yet. Calling allows me to see how the turn develops while keeping the pot manageable against a shorter stack. Raising would likely only get called by better or force out hands I'm ahead of.

I have A6 (A♥6♦) — middle pair (pair of sixes) with the best possible Ace kicker on a relatively dry board 6♠‑8♦‑3♣‑7♠. Flash opened the button and just called my flop continuation, so his range is wide (many Ax, Kx, broadways and small pairs, plus some 7x/8x/3x combinations). The turn 7 slightly improves some of his value combos (7x, 78, 73) but there are essentially no safe flush draws and straight possibilities (4+5) are rare. Betting for value/protection is correct here: I beat most of his continuing range and want to charge worse pairs and A‑high hands while denying free cards to the few unlikely straight combos. I’ll make a half‑pot value bet (600 into 1200) — big enough to extract value and get information but small enough that I can fold to an overcommitting raise. If Flash calls I proceed to the river planning another value/lead based on the river card; if he check‑raises a small/medium amount I’ll call to realize equity, but I will strongly discount and likely fold to a large shove which represents two‑pair/set given the action.

The turn 7s makes the board quite connected (6-8-3-7). Minni led out again for 600 into a 1200 pot. I hold JT, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9) and two overcards that might be good if she's betting a draw or a smaller pair. The pot odds are 3:1, requiring roughly 25% equity to continue. Between the 4 outs for the straight and the possibility that a Jack or Ten could improve me to the best hand, I have enough equity to justify a call, especially with position. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate based on the final card and her action.

River paired the 7 — my hand (A♠6♦) is now two pair (7s & 6s). Flash called my flop c‑bet and my turn bet, then checked the river, which signals a fairly wide, often medium‑strength continuing range (Ax, Kx, broadways, small pairs and the occasional showdown hand). Two pair is strong vs that range but is beaten by trip/full house/quads (any 7, pocket 66/88/33, or combinations like 7+6). I want to extract value from worse (A‑highs, Kx, single pairs that will call a reasonable river bet) while keeping the pot controllable if I face a check‑raise. A half‑pot value bet is the right balance here — big enough to get calls from worse but small enough to fold to a large shove. I will bet 1,200 into 2,400; call small/medium check‑raises to realize equity but fold to an overcommitting shove that strongly represents a full house/quads.

I currently hold Jack-high (Jd Th) on a board of 6s 8d 3c 7s 7h. Minni has led out on all three streets (flop, turn, and river). This line, known as 'donking' when the caller leads into the pre-flop aggressor, typically indicates a made hand or a strong draw that has now completed. Specifically, on this board, Minni could easily have a 7 (now trips), a straight (95 or 54), or a full house (87, 67). While there is a small chance she is bluffing, my Jack-high has zero showdown value; I even lose to her potential Ace-high or King-high bluffs. Given her short stack and the strength shown by betting three consecutive streets into me, I cannot justify a call with only Jack-high. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Open-raise to 200 (2BB). In heads-up the button’s opening range is very wide — 8♣3♥ is garbage but still fine as a steal candidate. Raising to 200 gives maximum fold equity, keeps the pot small if called, and leaves me post‑flop position (important for playing marginal hands). Limping/calling simply invites wider, more aggressive defense from the big blind and surrenders initiative. Plan: if Flash folds I scoop the 150 chip pot; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously — c‑bet on many dry flops but fold to credible aggression; if he 3‑bets I’ll fold this hand and wait for a better spot. This line maximizes long‑term EV while protecting a 67BB stack.

In this heads-up situation, I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five offsuit. The button, Minni, has a relatively short stack of 65.5 big blinds (6550 chips) and has raised to 200 (a min-raise). My hand is marginal but decent enough to defend against a wide button-opening range, especially getting 3-to-1 odds on a call. However, given that Minni has been playing relatively aggressively and the effective stack is short, I will opt to call to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high on a board of Queen-Ten-Four. This board is quite favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range (Minni), which likely contains many broadway cards and high pairs. Since I have no made hand and very little drawing equity, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading here would be ill-advised as I would likely be called or raised by better hands.

I raised preflop with a very wide heads‑up range; on Th‑4d‑Qs I have 8♣3♥ — no showdown value and no meaningful draws. This is a textbook c‑bet spot: the board is fairly dry and misses most of BB’s calling range, so a continuation bet will fold many hands and pick up the pot. I’ll use a medium size to preserve my stack while giving myself good fold equity: ~60% pot. If Flash folds I scoop; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and fold to significant aggression since I have virtually no equity. If he check‑raises I’ll fold immediately.

On a board of Queen-Ten-Four, my Jack-Five offsuit has no made value and virtually no drawing potential. This board is very favorable for Minni's button opening range, which includes many broadway hands and pairs. Since I have no equity to continue and Minni has followed up her pre-flop raise with a significant continuation bet (60% pot), the most profitable play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 4c 3s. This is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up situation. Minni is in the big blind with a relatively short stack of 6850 chips. While I have a massive chip lead, there's no reason to enter the pot with such a poor hand from the small blind when I can simply fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

Heads-up on the button I should be raising a very wide open range. 4♣9♦ is a weak offsuit holding, but raising here to seize the initiative and use my post‑flop positional advantage is standard. I’ll make a minimum open to 200 (total) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet with this hand; if Flash flats I’ll c‑bet selectively on dry flops and proceed cautiously on connected/flushy boards. Calling or folding preflop is too passive in this heads‑up spot — a min‑raise is the best exploitation over many hands.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc9h. Minni has raised to 200 from the Button. My hand is a decent defense hand in a heads-up situation, especially since Minni's stack is quite short (6800 chips, 68 big blinds). I need 100 more to call into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. Given the pot odds and the playability of J9, calling is the most standard and effective play here.

I have a gutshot straight draw to the nut straight (needs an 8) and two overcards (Jack and Nine). In this heads-up pot, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most flexible play, allowing me to see how Minni acts on this coordinated board before deciding whether to call a bet or take a more aggressive line.

I opened wide preflop to seize initiative heads‑up and was called. The flop 7♦ 10♥ 6♠ is dry and misses my 4♣9♦, but it is an excellent c‑bet spot: the board connects with only a small portion of the caller’s range (pocket tens/pairs, some 8x straights, occasional 7/6) and will fold out many overcards and weaker holdings. I have only a 4‑out straight (any 8) and tiny backdoor diamond equity, so my direct showdown equity is low — but I can leverage position and initiative. I’ll bet ~50% pot to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable: bet 200 into 400. If Flash folds we take it; if called I’ll control the pot on the turn and fold to significant aggression (check‑raise or a large turn shove) since my equity is limited.

I have a gutshot straight draw with an eight giving me the nuts, and my two overcards (Jack and Nine) provide some additional equity. Minni's bet of 200 into a 400 pot offers me 3:1 pot odds (25%), which is a reasonable price to see a turn card. Given the board texture and my position, calling is the most profitable play to realize my equity without over-committing against a short stack.

On the turn, I have improved to a pair of Nines with a Jack kicker. The board is highly coordinated (6-7-9-10), and any 8 completes a straight. Given the board texture and my position, checking is the most prudent action. It allows me to see how Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, evaluates this turn card. If Minni bets, I can decide whether to call based on the bet sizing, as I still have equity with the open-ended straight draw (any 8) and my pair of nines.

I improved to a pair of 9s on the turn (4♣9♦ with board 7♦ 10♥ 6♠ 9♠). Flash called my flop c‑bet and then checked the turn, which looks like a lot of medium pairs, floats or missed hands — not an overwhelmingly strong line. With a marginal pair and a weak 4 kicker I want to extract value from worse hands (7x/6x/small pairs) and charge any running draws while keeping the pot controllable. A half‑pot bet of 400 into 800 accomplishes that: good chance to fold out pure air and get called by worse, but small enough that I can fold to serious aggression without risking too much of my 6,600 stack. Plan: bet 400; fold to a significant raise, and if called proceed cautiously on the river (pot control / call vs small bets).

On the turn, the 9s improves my hand to a pair of Nines with a Jack kicker. The board is now extremely coordinated (6-7-9-10), meaning any 8 completes a straight. Minni has bet 400 into a pot of 800, giving me 3:1 pot odds (25%). Given that I have a pair, a Jack kicker, and an open-ended straight draw (any 8), calling is the most profitable decision. Raising would be risky against a potential straight or a higher pair (like 10x), and folding is out of the question given the strength of my draw and the current pair.

On the river, the board is 5-6-7-9-10. I have a pair of Nines with a Jack kicker. This board is extremely dangerous as any 8 makes a straight. Since I have check-called the flop and turn, checking the river is the most consistent and safest play. I have some showdown value, but betting would likely only get called by better hands (like a Ten, two pair, or a straight). By checking, I can potentially see a cheap showdown or decide whether to call a final bet from Minni if she chooses to bluff or value bet.

I have a marginal value hand — pair of 9s (9♦) with a 4 kicker — on a river of 7♦ 10♥ 6♠ 9♠ 5♥. Flash checked the river after check‑calling my flop and turn bets. The turn 9 completed the main dangerous draw (any 8 made the straight on the turn), and the river 5 doesn’t meaningfully improve my relative equity vs the opponent’s calling range. 

Betting here mostly achieves two outcomes: (1) fold out air and some floats (rarely profitable heads‑up), or (2) get called by better hands (Tx, straights, sets) that already beat me — which is negative EV. With a weak kicker and a line where my opponent has shown willingness to call, pot‑control and taking this to showdown is the prudent play. I’ll check and show down; if Flash bluffs into me I can win the pot, if he bets small I’m inclined to call, but I’ll fold to a large raise since my kicker is poor and I’m likely behind.

I am on the button with a medium-low connector (5h 6c). While the hand is weak, standard heads-up strategy involves opening a wide range from the button to put pressure on the big blind. Minni has a relatively short stack of 61 BBs, making it easier to navigate post-flop or fold if she decides to shove. I will stick to my standard open size of 300.

Heads-up vs a 3bb open from the button, A9o is strong enough to defend the big blind but not a hand I want to turn into a light 3-bet out of position. Stacks are ~61bb effective — deep enough to realize equity postflop — and calling 200 more (300 total) keeps the pot controlled while letting me play for position disadvantage on favorable boards. A min-raise to 500 is too small to credibly take it away and a larger 3-bet would be over-committing with a marginal hand. Plan: call, see the flop, and play actively on ace-high or safe boards, fold to heavy aggression on very unfavorable textures.

Heads-up on a dry 4c-2d-8c board I hold Ace-high (Ah9s) after defending the big blind. Flash’s open is wide in heads-up, so leading here takes the initiative, extracts value from worse A/x and K/x hands, and folds out some small pairs and overcards. Two clubs are on board but I don’t have a club; a half-pot sizing keeps the pot manageable with ~59bb effective while retaining fold equity and denying free cards. Plan: make a half-pot c-bet (300). If Flash folds I take the pot; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn; if he makes heavy, large-sized aggression I’ll re-evaluate and be prepared to fold since my hand has limited improvement potential.

On a board of 4c 2d 8c, Minni has led out (donk bet) for 300 into a pot of 600 after I raised pre-flop. I have 6h 5h for an open-ended straight draw (any 3 or 7 gives me a straight). While my hand is currently just high cards, the draw is strong enough to continue. Minni's stack is quite short (under 60 BBs), so I don't want to inflate the pot too much yet. Calling allows me to realize my equity and see a turn card cheaply. If I hit my straight or a 6 or 5 on the turn, I can then consider raising or betting.

Check. The King on the turn is a scary overcard to my flop c-bet calling range — Flash called my flop lead, so his continuing range contains Kx, pairs/sets and some floats. I only have Ace-high (with an AK blocker), so I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot (current pot 1,200, my stack 5,600), lets me realize equity vs bluffs/floats, and keeps the decision flexible. Plan: check and fold to heavy, polarizing aggression; call a small-to-medium bet to see a river if the sizing is reasonable (e.g. ~<=1/2 pot), but give up to pot-sized or larger pressure since I’m behind most Kx and made hands.

On the turn, the King of Hearts is an overcard that likely doesn't help Minni's flop leading range. After she leads the flop and checks the turn, I have a choice between betting as a semi-bluff or checking behind to realize my equity. With an open-ended straight draw (3s and 7s), I have about a 17% chance to hit my straight on the river. Checking back allows me to see the river for free without the risk of being check-raised off my draw, which is a prudent play against a shorter stack who might be looking to commit.

Board: 4c 2d 8c Kh Ac, I hold Ah9s — river pairs me for top pair but the board now has three clubs (so flopped two-club combos could have made a flush). Flash opened wide heads-up and called my flop lead, then checked the turn; his line is still heavy with Kx, mid pocket pairs and some draws, and less likely to be a slow-played flopped nut flush or set that would check back every street. A thin value bet is appropriate: I beat many of his calling hands (Kx, Qx, Tx, single-pair hands) and can fold to heavy, polarized aggression. Bet half-pot (600 into 1,200) to extract value from worse hands while keeping the commitment manageable. If he check-raises pot or jams, that polarization is consistent with a nut flush or set/full house and I will fold.

On the river Ace of clubs, the board is 4c 2d 8c Kh Ac. I started the hand with 6c 5h, which gave me an open-ended straight draw on the flop. I called Minni's lead on the flop and checked back on the turn King of hearts. The river Ace of clubs is a significant card that completes potential flush draws and is an overcard. Minni has bet 600 into 1200. My hand is 6-high and has zero showdown value. While I could potentially bluff by representing the flush or the Ace, Minni's relatively short stack (approximately 50 BBs) and her line (donk bet flop, check turn, bet river) suggests she is unlikely to fold a pair or better to a raise. Since I have no equity to win at showdown, folding is the most prudent and disciplined play here.

Heads-up on the button with 9♠2♠ — a weak hand but suited and very playable in position. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call; I’ll open-raise to the min (200 total) to leverage my positional advantage and a lot of fold equity vs the big blind. The small raise keeps the pot manageable if called; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold (this hand has poor showdown value vs a strong 3-bet). If called, I’ll use position to c-bet favorable, dry flops and control the pot on dangerous boards, folding to strong resistance. Overall this is a standard heads-up steal attempt with a plan to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Given that we are heads-up and I am being offered 3-to-1 odds on a call (100 more to win a total pot of 300), I will defend my big blind. Although Q5o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, the price is low enough to justify seeing a flop.

I am in the Big Blind and have hit bottom pair (fives) on a King-high flop (Kd 5c on a 5d-Ks-9h board). As the Big Blind in a heads-up pot, I act first on the flop. Checking is the standard play here to see how the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) reacts. Leading out (donking) would likely fold out her bluffs and get called or raised by her better hands. I will check and evaluate based on her bet sizing if she chooses to bet.

I was the preflop aggressor and the flop is K♠ 9♥ 5♦ — I have second pair (9♠) with a very weak kicker. Flash checked, so this is a good spot to c‑bet: I’m ahead of a lot of unpaired hands (A‑x, Q‑x, missed broadways) and some weaker pairs, and a bet charges overcards while still keeping the pot under control against a King. I’ll size to extract value and keep fold equity (and fold to clear strength). Bet 200 into the 400 pot (half‑pot). If Flash folds I take it down; if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and fold to a large raise that represents a King or better.

I have bottom pair (Fives) on a board of Kd-5d-9h. The pot is 600 and Minni has bet 200, which is 1/3 pot. This is a small continuation bet often used with a wide range including air, draws, or weak top pairs. While I am behind any King, Nine, or middle pocket pair, I cannot fold bottom pair to such a small bet heads-up. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate.

On the turn (5d-Ks-9h-3h), I have bottom pair (Fives) and a Queen kicker. The turn 3h is a blank that shouldn't change the equity significantly. The pot is 800. My opponent, Minni, has checked behind on the turn after I checked to her. This check-back suggests she might be giving up on a bluff or has a marginal hand like a weak Nine or a pocket pair lower than Kings that she wants to take to a cheap showdown. Since I have some showdown value but am likely behind a Nine, and she has shown aggression previously, checking is the most prudent play. I want to get to showdown cheaply or catch a bluff on the river.

I have second pair (9♠) with a very weak kicker on a relatively dry board (5♦ K♠ 9♥ 3♥). Flash checked the turn after calling my flop c‑bet, so his range contains lots of worse one‑pair hands (A‑x, small pairs, 5x, maybe 9x) and some Kx. I want to extract value and charge any backdoor heart combos while keeping the pot controlled. A half‑pot value bet (~400 into 800) accomplishes that: it gets called by worse hands and draws but keeps my risk limited.
