If Flash check‑raises big I'll fold — that line will usually represent a King or better that my weak kicker can't beat. If he calls, I’ll reevaluate the river and proceed cautiously.

I have bottom pair (fives) on a King-high board. Minni raised pre-flop and has bet both the flop and the turn. Her 400 bet into a 800 pot on the turn gives me 3-to-1 odds to call. While my hand is weak, in a heads-up situation, bottom pair can still be the best hand against a wide range of semi-bluffs or air. I will call the turn bet and look to see a river card, as folding a pair for this price would be overly tight in a heads-up match against an active opponent.

On the river, the board is 5d-Ks-9h-3h-7c and I have bottom pair (Fives) with a Queen kicker. I have checked and called small-to-medium bets on the flop and turn. The river card (7c) is relatively safe, though it does complete a gutter (68). Since I have some showdown value but am likely beaten by any King, Nine, or middle pocket pair, I will check. I want to see if Minni checks back her marginal hands or tries to bluff one last time. Betting myself would likely only get called by better hands.

I have second pair (9♠) with a very weak kicker on a fairly dry board K♠-9♥-5♦-3♥-7♣. Flash called both my flop (200) and turn (400) bets, so his line is weighted toward value hands (Kx, 9x, 5x, small pairs) and not a high frequency of bluffs. The pot is 1600 — a half‑pot river bet (~800) would be called by most hands that currently beat me and only fold the weakest air, so a value bet is thin and likely -EV. I’ll check and take the showdown for free rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with 8h6s, a speculative hand. My opponent Minni has a relatively short stack of 7500. I'll make a standard 3x open raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and maintain an aggressive image.

Heads-up vs Button open to 300 — I’m in the BB with 5♥10♥. It’s a marginal but suited hand with decent heads-up playability. The call is only 200 into a 400 pot, so required equity is ~33% — reasonable to defend. 3-betting to the min is an option but unnecessary here: it bloats the pot against a much deeper opponent and commits more out of position. I’ll call to see a flop and play post‑flop: continue aggressively with top pair, two pair, strong draws or improved holdings; on dry, missed boards I’ll control pot size and fold to large aggression.

Board is Kc 9h 3h and I hold 10h5h — I have a strong 4-card heart draw (9 clean heart outs ≈ 35% to make a flush by river) plus some weak paired-card outs. I’m out of position and Flash opened preflop, so leading out OOP turns this into a bloated pot against a deeper, aggressive stack. Checking preserves pot control, gives me correct pot odds to call a typical c-bet, and keeps the option to check‑raise as a semi‑bluff if he bets small. Plan: check; call reasonable-sized c-bets (I’ll continue with most half‑pot or smaller bets), reserve raises for very thin folds or when I improve on the turn — fold to clear large show-of‑strength if he barrels big and I don’t improve.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the flop has come King-high, which is generally better for my perceived range. Minni has checked to me. I will place a standard continuation bet of half the pot to try and take the hand down immediately, as I have no showdown value with eight-high.

I have T♥5♥ on Kc 9h 3h — a strong 4‑card heart draw (9 clean heart outs, ~36% to make by the river, ~19% to hit on the turn). Flash’s bet is small (300 into a 900 pot ≈ 1/3 pot), so calling 300 gives me ~25% pot odds and is +EV to see the turn. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a semi‑bluff raise that can be easily called or 3‑bet/shoved by a much deeper stack. Plan: call to realize my equity, continue vs small/medium turn bets or fold to very large aggression if I miss; if I hit my flush I can extract value on later streets.

Turn A♥ gives me a made flush (A‑high using Ah,Th,9h,5h,3h). This is a very strong hand heads‑up but not the absolute nut — any single heart J♥/Q♥/K♥ in Flash’s hand beats me. Pot is 1,200 and I’m out of position against a deep, aggressive opener who c‑bet the flop. I want to extract value from Kx, worse pairs/straight/flush draws and charge any turn/river full‑house/board‑pair outs while also denying a free river that could turn a better hand. A 2/3‑pot value/protection bet (~800) accomplishes that: it keeps many worse hands and weaker draws calling while making thin draws pay to continue.

Plan if action changes: if Flash check‑raises turn large, it often represents a better heart or a disguised boat — I’ll re‑evaluate (call small/medium turn raises to avoid folding the best hand too readily, but fold to an overcommitting shove that clearly polarizes to hands that beat me). If he checks back I’ll re‑assess river for a second‑street value bet or pot control depending on the river card and his tendencies. Given my 7,000 stack, an 800 bet is a comfortable, value‑oriented size that preserves stack flexibility.

Minni has led into me on the turn after calling my flop continuation bet. The turn card is an Ace of hearts, which is a very polarising card; it either hits my range as the pre-flop aggressor or completes a flush/improves her holding (like Ax). Given that I have only eight-high with no draws and Minni's lead suggests she has at least a pair or a strong draw she is willing to commit with, I have no reason to continue in this hand. Bluffing against her relatively short stack is risky as she may be pot-committed with any decent hand.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Ac4s — Ace-high in position is a standard open. I want initiative and fold equity rather than limp/calling and giving the big blind free or cheap flops. With ~81bb effective and Flash deep, a 2x BB open is the right balance: builds the pot when I’m ahead, denies equity, and lets me c-bet favorable flops. Plan if 3-bet: call smaller 3-bets and play postflop; fold to very large 3-bets/shoves since A4o performs poorly against polarized 3-bet ranges. Postflop I’ll c-bet A-high and dry boards for value/protection and use pot control or check-call on wet boards. Raising to 200 keeps the line flexible and maximizes long-term EV in this spot.

With 7c 5d in the big blind, I am facing a small raise from Minni (SB) to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300. This is a clear defend given the pot odds and the fact that we are heads-up. Minni's stack is relatively short compared to mine (8000 vs 31700), and seeing a flop with middle connectors is standard in this position.

On this flop of Jd-Qs-3d, I have 7c-5d, which is air. There are no straight draws or flush draws for my hand. Minni has checked, and the pot is small (400). Given I am in the big blind and Minni is the pre-flop aggressor who has now checked, I could potentially bet to take the pot down, but checking back and seeing a free turn card is more prudent since my hand has virtually no equity. I'll check and see if I can improve or if the action stays cheap.

I was the preflop aggressor and I have position. The flop Jd Qs 3d is relatively dry (two overcards and a small side card) and favors a continuation bet from my raising range. Holding Ac4s I have the Ace-high blocker to some A‑x hands and no diamond draw, so I’ll c-bet for fold equity and to charge weaker hands and backdoor draws. I don’t want to commit too much with only Ace-high, so I’ll size to about half the pot to deny equity and fold out Kx/Tx and many unpaired hands, while keeping the pot manageable if I’m called by Q/J/pair combos. If I face a large raise I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to heavy aggression without improvement).

My hand [7c 5d] has completely missed the flop of [Jd Qs 3d]. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a half-pot continuation bet of 200 after I checked. I have no draws, no pairs, and no significant backdoor equity on this board. Since I am essentially holding air against a range that hits this board frequently, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with a weak hand, 9s 4h. The stack sizes are significantly different, as I have over 31k while Minni has 8300. In a heads-up situation, I typically want to apply pressure, but 94o is near the bottom of my range. However, standard play from the small blind/button in a heads-up match involves raising a wide range. Looking at previous hands, Minni has been active and sometimes folds to raises or calls and plays post-flop. Given the weak nature of my hand, a raise to 300 is a standard attempt to take down the blinds, but folding is also perfectly acceptable. Since I am in a dominant position chip-wise, I will attempt to steal the blinds with a standard 3x raise.

Facing a Button (SB) open to 300 heads-up, A♦4♠ (Ah4s) is a clear call. The opener’s range is wide in heads-up and I get correct pot odds (call 200 into 400 -> ~33%); my Ace has blocker value and sufficient equity vs a wide opening range. With ~83bb effective I don’t want to 3-bet light OOP and inflate the pot with a marginal hand. Plan: call to see the flop (total commitment 300), proceed cautiously postflop — play aggressively if I pair the Ace or get good draws, otherwise fold to large pressure on dry boards.

Heads‑up vs a wide button open (Flash). Flop is Jh Tc Jc — a paired, relatively wet board for the opener: lots of Jx and many Tx/JT/KJ/JT/club combos in his range. I have A♥4♠ — no pair, only an Ace‑high and a tiny backdoor heart; essentially no fold equity and very little equity vs a realistic cbet range. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and lose to his value combos (or get called by hands that still beat me). Plan is to pot‑control and keep the pot small: check and evaluate his reaction. If he checks back I’ll take a free card to the turn; if he bets I will be prepared to fold to significant pressure (call only very small probes where I think his range is wide).

On a highly coordinated board of Jh-Tc-Jc, I have 9-high with no draw. Although I was the pre-flop aggressor, this board is likely to have connected with Minni's calling range (broadway cards, pairs, and suited connectors). Betting here into a paired board with a straight draw present is risky with zero equity. I will check behind to see a free turn card and keep the pot small.

Both checked the flop and the turn; I have A♣4♠ (A♥4♠) with no pair. The Q♠ on the turn actually hurts me — it gives many of Flash’s wide button combos a pair (Qx, KQ, QJ) and I have almost no fold equity. My only realistic backdoor is catching a King on the river to make the Broadway straight (4 outs out of 46 ≈ 8.7%), so chasing is a thin, low-ROI play unless I’m getting excellent pot odds. Betting or raising here bloats the pot with a near-showdown-losing hand and will be called by most hands that beat me. I’ll check to keep the pot small, take a free river, and plan to fold to meaningful river aggression; I will only call very small river probes that give the correct pot odds to chase the one-card straight.

The board is extremely coordinated (Jh-Tc-Jc-Qs), and my 9-high has very little showdown value. However, after Minni checked both the flop and the turn, it's possible she is also weak or trapping. Given I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a King to make a straight), I will check behind to take the free card and avoid a potential check-raise on such a wet board.

Pot is 600 and I hold A♥4♠ on J♥ T♣ J♣ Q♠ 9♦. I never improved — I have only Ace‑high and essentially zero showdown equity vs a realistic button opening range (many Jx, Qx, Tx, 9x, pocket pairs, and the river 9 also completes straights for K or 8). Flash has been passive (checked flop and turn), so I have very little fold equity and betting would only bloat the pot with a huge underdog and expose me to a value bet or a check‑raise. Optimal line is pot control: check to showdown and fold if he now produces meaningful river aggression. Therefore I check.

On a board of Jh-Tc-Jc-Qs-9d, the best five-card hand I can make using my 9s 4h is J-J-Q-T-9 (the board itself). Since Minni has checked the flop, turn, and river, it's highly likely she is also weak. However, any card in her hand higher than a 9 (like an Ace, or a 10 or Queen for a better two pair) would beat me. Additionally, any King or 8 makes a straight. Since I am essentially playing the board and have no real showdown value beyond that, and there is little to gain by bluffing into a board that likely results in a split or a loss, I will check behind and take the showdown.

Heads-up on the button with 9♣8♣ — a strong suited connector to play aggressively in position. Pot is only 150 and I only need to call 50, but opening to 300 (≈3× BB) takes the initiative, gains fold equity against Flash’s wide big-blind range, and builds a pot I can play postflop with positional advantage. The stacks are deep enough to maneuver postflop, and 300 is chosen over the minimum to avoid giving cheap flops and to discourage easy squeezes.

I am in the big blind with 7h 5d. Minni, who is on the button (SB) with a relatively short stack of 7800, has raised to 300. My hand is quite weak and does not play well against a button raising range, especially with the current stack dynamics. Calling would put me out of position with a marginal hand, and raising is too risky given the potential for Minni to shove or play back. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my large stack lead.

J2o is an extremely weak hand, even in a heads-up situation. While I have the chip lead and position as the button, Minni has shown enough aggression to make playing garbage hands unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better hand to utilize my positional advantage.

Heads‑up on the button with Th9s — a very playable, aggressive hand. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I want the initiative and fold equity vs Flash’s wide BB defending range. A raise to the minimum (200 total) is standard: it pressures marginal hands, keeps the pot controlled with an 82BB stack, and preserves position for postflop play. If he folds I win the blinds; if he calls I’ll c‑bet frequently on favorable flops and play cautiously vs strong resistance. If he 3‑bets large I’ll re‑evaluate and lean to folding T9o out of position against a sizable 3‑bet. Overall raising to 200 maximizes EV in this heads‑up spot.

I am holding King-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 200 (a 2x raise). KTo is a strong hand in heads-up play, and I am getting 3:1 odds to see a flop. Given the pot odds and the strength of my hand relative to a typical button opening range, calling is a standard and profitable play.

On this flop of Jd-7s-6c, I have King-high with no direct draw. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser. The standard play in this situation is to check to the aggressor and see how they react, keeping the pot small while I have limited hand strength.

I was the preflop raiser and have position, so this is a good spot to apply pressure. Th9s has no pair on Jd‑7s‑6c but does have a 4‑out gutshot to an 8 (~16% to hit by river) and some backdoor possibilities. A continuation bet around half the pot (200 into 400) maximizes fold equity versus Flash’s wide BB calling range, charges his worse hands and draws, and keeps the pot controllable if called. Plan: take it down if he folds; if called, continue cautiously — bet for value when I hit the 8, check‑control on bricks, and fold to sizable turn/river raises since my equity without improvement is limited.

In this heads-up situation, I am holding King-Ten offsuit. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call a half-pot continuation bet from Minni. While King-high is a marginal hand on a Jack-high board, Minni's button opening range is very wide and often includes many hands that I currently beat or have decent equity against. Given the pot odds and the fact that I have two overcards to the middle of the board (Ten and King), calling one street to see if I improve on the turn or if Minni slows down is a standard defensive play in heads-up poker.

On the turn, the Ten of Clubs gives me second pair (Tens) with a King kicker. While this is a significant improvement from King-high, the board is still somewhat coordinated with straight possibilities. Being out of position, checking is the most effective way to control the pot size and observe Minni's betting behavior. If she bets, I have a clear call given the strength of my hand relative to her likely range; if she checks back, I can see a free river and potentially extract value then.

Turn analysis — I paired my Ten (T♥) on the turn and the pot is 800. I was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop (200) and got called, so Flash’s continuing range is wide: Jx, small-to-medium pairs, some overpairs, and draws (most importantly 9‑8 type straight draws). The turn T is a double‑edged card: it gives me a pair but also completes some of his flop draws (89) and improves some of his holdings (Tx combos). Betting now would protect vs pure floats but also bloats the pot when I’m frequently behind to Jx/sets/straights or facing Tx with better kickers — and a bet will often be called by hands that still beat me. In position with a vulnerable second pair, the +EV line is pot control: check back the turn, keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs on the river, and preserve the option to fold to real strength. Plan: check to the river; if I hit a clean improving card (an 8 for the straight, or a safe low card that likely leaves me ahead) I’ll value bet; if the river is a blank and Flash bets small I’ll consider a call, but I will concede to large river pressure.

On the river, the board is Jd-7s-6c-Tc-Ac. I have a pair of Tens with a King kicker. After calling a bet on the flop and seeing a check-check on the turn, my hand has decent showdown value. However, the Ace on the river is a significant scare card that likely improves Minni's range if she was holding overcards or a weak Ace. By checking, I can minimize my losses if she has improved or potentially induce a bluff from a missed draw like 89 or 54, while also seeing a cheap showdown against other marginal hands.

Check. I followed my turn plan to control the pot and the river paired my Ten (I have second pair, T9). Flash’s line (called my flop c‑bet, checked turn and river) is wide and includes many hands that beat me now — Jx, any made straights (8‑9), sets/some two‑pairs, and Ax combinations — plus a few clubs that could make a flush. I only beat underpairs and missed draws, so a river stab would be thin value at best and will often be called by better hands or induce a costly raise. With opponent deep and the pot already medium (800), pot‑control to showdown is the lowest variance, +EV line: check back and show my pair of tens rather than bloating the pot with a marginal value bet.

Holding A9o on the button in a heads-up situation, I have a strong starting hand. My standard open from this position throughout the match has been 300, and given the stack depths and the opponent's tendency to defend their blind, I will maintain this sizing to build a pot while retaining control of the initiative.

Heads-up vs Flash (button) who opened to 300. I have 2♣2♠ on the big blind with ~77.5BB effective. Against a wide, aggressive SB opening range this is a classic set-mining spot — great implied odds if I hit. 3‑betting with a small pair bloats the pot and reduces my ability to realize equity; a small/min raise also invites larger action OOP. I’ll call the extra 200 (300 total) to see a flop and proceed based on texture: fast‑play a set, pot‑control or fold on dry overcard flops, and use position/reads to extract when appropriate.

Check. I called preflop as a set-mining call with ~77.5BB effective; the flop 7♠‑8♣‑3♦ is very dry with no real flush or obvious straight draws and my hand (2♣2♠) is an underpair with modest showdown value heads‑up. Checking controls the pot size, lets me realize equity and allows Flash to bluff or thin-value himself. Plan: call small c‑bets (≈1/3 pot ≈200) because he opens wide and will miss often; call most medium c‑bets (up to ≈1/2 pot) but proceed cautiously on turn if an overcard appears; fold to strong single‑street aggression or a flop check‑raise that polarizes him (sets/strong pairs). If he checks back I’ll check down and show down cheaply. This line preserves my implied odds if I hit a set and avoids bloating the pot out of position with a marginal hand.

I have Ace-high with two overcards on a dry, low board of 7-8-3. As the pre-flop aggressor, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. Minni has checked, and while they might have a piece of this board given it's in a big blind defending range, a bet of 300 (half pot) is a good frequency play to either win the pot immediately or set up a barrel on later streets if I improve. Minni's shorter stack means I should be cautious of a check-raise, but for now, I will maintain the initiative.

I called preflop with 2♣2♠ as a set-mining/protection call and checked the dry 7♠‑8♣‑3♦ flop to control the pot. Flash’s continuation bet of 300 is a small c‑bet (~1/3 pot) into a 900 pot and represents a wide, missed portion of his button range. Raising OOP with just an underpair would bloat the pot with very little equity and often fold out worse hands; folding here is too passive because he will bluff/kat air a lot. Calling preserves my chance to hit a set, keeps his bluffs in, and gives good pot odds (~4:1 to call the bet) for continuing. Plan for the turn: if I hit a deuce I’ll raise/extract; if a clean overcard appears and he fires a sizable bet I’ll strongly consider folding; if he slows I’ll check‑down. Action: call the 300.

Check. I called preflop as a set-miner and checked the dry 7♠‑8♣‑3♦ flop to control the pot against Flash’s wide, small c‑bet. The turn T♦ is an overcard that improves a large portion of his button range (T, broadways) and also puts a second diamond on board, so betting OOP with an underpair would needlessly bloat the pot and fold out many worse hands. I’ll keep the pot small and let him take the lead — if he bets a small/medium size (≈≤½ pot, ≲600) I’ll call to realize equity and hope to hit a deuce on the river; if he barrels big (>≈2/3 pot, >600) I’ll give up. If he checks back I’ll check down and show cheaply (or raise on a deuce river).

On a turn card of a Ten, the board is now 7-8-3-T. My Nine gives me an open-ended straight draw (needing a 6 or a Jack). Since Minni checked a second time, they might have a piece of the board or a draw themselves. While I could continue betting as a semi-bluff, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially improve on the river without risking a check-raise from a short-stacked opponent. Ace-high also retains some showdown value against their air or weaker draws.

Heads‑up on a 7♠‑8♣‑3♦‑T♦‑A♦ runout and I have 2♣2♠. Flash opened wide from the button, c‑bet small on the flop, then checked the turn — a line consistent with a lot of missed broadways, some medium pair/pot‑control hands, and occasionally slow‑played value. The river A♦ is extremely damaging to my equity: it gives villain many A‑x value combos and completes diamond draws (board is 3♦‑T♦‑A♦), so a large chunk of his continuing range now beats me. I also don’t block A♦ or diamond combos, so my fold equity as a bluff is limited. Betting risks getting called by many hands that now beat me (Ax, Tx, diamond flushes) while folding out only a narrow portion of his range; a big bluff would be too high variance for little EV. Best line is to keep the pot small and check — see a showdown cheaply and fold to a bet on the river if he now fires enough to represent real value. Action: check.

On the river, the board is 7-8-3-T-A. I have hit top pair with my Ace. Minni has checked to me for the third time (after check-calling the flop and checking the turn). The Ace is a significant scare card for any middle pairs Minni might be holding, such as a Ten, Eight, or Seven. While I could bet for thin value, Minni has frequently folded to river aggression throughout the session. Checking back guarantees I win the pot against her range of weaker pairs or busted draws while avoiding any potential check-raise from a trapped hand, though that is unlikely. Given the current game state and my lead, checking back to realize my showdown value is a safe and effective play.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with KcQs — a strong, playable hand in position. Calling 50 would be passive and give the BB cheap odds; I want initiative and to isolate. I’ll raise to 300 (about 3x the BB), a standard heads-up sizing that extracts value from worse broadways/suited aces/small pairs, gains fold equity vs weaker hands, and reduces the frequency of multiway or free-runout scenarios. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (KQo can call a reasonable 3-bet to see a flop but should fold to very large 3-bets/shoves); if called I’ll C-bet selectively based on texture. Raise to 300 to take control of the hand.

With Q-8 offsuit in the Big Blind, I am facing a raise from Minni (who is the Button/SB in this heads-up situation). Minni has a relatively short stack of about 70 big blinds (6950 chips). While Q-8 isn't a premium hand, it's strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a button raise, especially with the pot odds offered. I only need to call 200 more into a 400 pot. I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate, keeping the pot manageable against her stack.

I have the Queen-high flush draw on a monotone board [Ac 7c Tc]. Holding the Qc is very significant here as it's the second nut flush card. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, I will check to see how she reacts to this board. Checking allows me to control the pot size and utilize my draw effectively based on her bet sizing.

I was the preflop aggressor with KcQs; flop is Ac 7c Tc and the BB checked. I hold the Kc giving me a four-card club draw (9 outs) plus two overcards to the ten — roughly ~35–40% equity vs a wide calling range — so this is an ideal semi-bluff spot. Betting protects my equity, denies free cards to hands that currently beat me, and extracts fold equity from unpaired hands and non-club Kx/Qx. I prefer a ~50% pot c-bet here: large enough to get folds and small enough to keep worse hands calling. Plan: bet 300; if called I’ll reassess on the turn (barrel on a club or a K/Q, pot-control or check-back otherwise). If Flash raises large I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and range (likely fold to a very large shove/3-bet).

On this monotone Ace-high board, holding the Queen of clubs gives me the second-best flush draw. Minni's bet of 300 into the 600 pot is a standard continuation bet. Given the pot odds and my significant equity with a flush draw, I will call to see the turn. Raising at this point would likely only be called or raised by better hands like an Ace or a completed flush, so calling allows me to keep the pot under control while I wait for my draw to complete.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Ten of Spades, making the board Ac-7c-Tc-Ts. I am first to act. I currently hold the Queen of clubs, which is a strong flush draw, but I have no made hand yet (Eight-high). Given the board has paired and contains an Ace, and that Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, there is a significant chance she has an Ace or a made flush. I will check to see how she responds; if she checks behind, I get a free card to try and hit my flush. If she bets, I will evaluate the price to continue based on my equity.

Turn is A♣ 7♣ T♣ T♠. Holding K♣Q♠ I have a four‑card club draw (9 outs ≈19.6% to hit on the river) and K/Q are overcards to the Ten but I’m still crushed by any A and behind any Ten or set. Flash called my flop c‑bet and checked the turn — he can easily have Ax, Tx, a club/flush draw or a medium pair. My preflop/ flop plan was to barrel the turn only if it improved me (club or K/Q); this T♠ didn’t. With the board now paired my fold equity is reduced, so betting as a pure bluff is poor EV. I prefer pot control: check to see the river, preserving my draw and avoiding getting raised off the hand by an A/T or a set. If Flash bets the river I’ll re‑evaluate (call small bluffs, fold to large polarized raises). Action: check.

On the river, the board is double-paired with Aces and Tens (Ac Ah Tc Ts). My best five-card hand is Aces full of Tens with a Queen kicker (A-A-T-T-Q). This is a very strong hand, but it is vulnerable to any hand containing an Ace (quads), a Ten (Tens full of Aces), or a King (Aces full of Tens with a higher kicker). Minni checked the turn, which may indicate she does not have a Ten, but she could easily be slow-playing an Ace or have a King-high hand. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and would only be called or raised by hands that beat me. Therefore, checking to see if we can get to a cheap showdown or induce a bluff is the most prudent action.

River: A♣ 7♣ T♣ T♠ A♥. I hold K♣Q♠ — no pair and I missed the flush (only four clubs including my K♣). Opponent called my flop c‑bet and then checked turn and river. That line is consistent with a lot of hands that beat me (A‑x, T‑x, or even trap/slowplayed pairs) and also with some medium hands that gave up. On a double‑paired A/T board the frequency of hands that will call a river bluff (any Ace, any Ten, many pairs) is high and my fold equity is low. I have no value to extract. Betting here would be a thin, low‑EV bluff; checking and going to showdown is the least costly line. If Flash had bet the river I’d fold to large, polarized bets and only entertain tiny bluffs, but since he checked I’ll check back and show.

I am on the button with 4d 7d, a speculative suited connector. In a heads-up situation against Minni, I want to maintain my aggressive image and apply pressure, especially given my significantly larger stack. Raising to 300 has been my standard opening size and I will continue with that strategy here to try and take the pot down pre-flop or play in position.

Facing a 300 open from the button with 8♠3♠ in the big blind. This is a marginal but suited hand in heads-up, and the button’s opening range is wide, so folding is too tight. 3‑betting with 8‑3s OOP is thin and will often get me into uncomfortable spots against a deep-stacked, aggressive opponent. The pot is 400 and I need to call 200 (300 total contribution) which gives ~33% pot odds and reasonable implied odds to realize equity. I’ll call to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, and play cautiously postflop — look for spade/straight possibilities or a cheap fold if I miss and face significant aggression.

Heads-up to a wide button open. I flatted 8s3s preflop and the flop A♣ 3♥ 4♠ gives me middle pair (3s) with a weak 8-kicker and only a runner-runner spade backdoor. This is a marginal but showable hand that has decent showdown value versus a wide raising range but is easily dominated by A‑x and many overcards. I'm out of position, so pot control is important — I don't want to inflate the pot with a vulnerable hand. I’ll check to keep the pot small, see what Flash does, call a reasonable continuation bet, and re-evaluate on the turn (folding to large aggression, calling small bets, and betting only when I improve or get clear fold equity).

I have middle pair (fours) on an Ace-high board. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage that includes many Ace-X combinations. Minni's check on the flop is a standard play, and a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) allows me to represent the Ace and potentially take the pot down now. If called, I still have a pair and can re-evaluate on the turn.

Flop: A♣ 3♥ 4♠. I have 3♠8♠ — middle pair with a weak kicker and a backdoor spade. Flash opened wide heads-up preflop and c-bets a lot, so his range contains many bluffs and A‑x, giving my pair decent showdown value. The bet is 300 into a 600 pot (pot becomes 900); calling 300 to win 1200 requires ~25% equity, which my hand likely has against his wide c-bet frequency. Raising OOP with a vulnerable pair/kicker is thin and will build a pot I don't want to play deep out-of-position; folding is too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call to control the pot, re-evaluate on the turn (call small barrels, fold to large turn shove, and extract value if I improve to trips/flush).

I have 8♠3♠ on A♣ 3♥ 4♠ K♦ — a single, vulnerable pair (middle pair with a very weak kicker). After the K♦ turn backdoors that could make big hands are gone (a flush is no longer possible on the river given the current runout), and my only realistic improvements are three 8s or two remaining 3s (≈5 outs, ~11% to improve). I'm out of position vs a very deep, aggressive opponent (Flash, 31k) and my stack (7,250) makes pot control important. Betting/raising here OOP with such a marginal holding would just bloat the pot and invite big pressure from Ax/Kx hands that dominate me. I’ll check to keep the pot small, show willingness to fold to heavy action, and use pot odds to call small turn/river bets for showdown value. Plan: check; call small-to-medium bets (reasonable pot odds for showdown), fold to large bets or shove-sized pressure, and re-evaluate on the river if checked to.

On the turn, the King of Diamonds is a significant scare card. I have a pair of fours, which has some showdown value but is likely behind any Ace or King in Minni's range. Since Minni called my flop bet, she likely has a piece of the board. By checking back here, I control the size of the pot and allow myself to see a free river card. If she was floating the flop with a draw or a weaker hand, I might still be ahead. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands (Aces, Kings, or better pairs), effectively turning my modest showdown value into a bluff.

Board A♣ 3♥ 4♠ K♦ 9♠ — I have 8♠3♠: a one-pair (pair of 3s) with a very weak kicker. Villain opened pre, c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn, which often denotes a range containing many Ax/Kx and some bluffs — plenty of hands that beat my pair. The river 9♠ doesn't complete anything that changes my relative strength (no five-card spade flush or straight), so my hand is still vulnerable and has limited fold equity. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot and get called or raised by hands that dominate me; checking keeps the pot under control and lets me get to showdown against bluffs and missed overcards. Plan: check and see a showdown or call a small/medium river stab, fold to large river aggression.

I have a pair of fours which has some showdown value. Minni's check on the river after calling the flop and checking the turn suggests she might be holding a weak made hand or a missed draw. While my fours are vulnerable to any Ace, King, or higher pair, betting is unlikely to force a fold from better hands (like a weak Ace or King) and I likely beat her air/missed draws. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value without risking more chips in a spot where I'm likely either way ahead or way behind.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 4♣6♦ I'm opening very wide — this hand is weak but playable in position and has some backdoor equity. Raising to the minimum (200 total) is the standard heads-up open: it takes the initiative, applies pressure to the big blind and avoids giving a free flop where my weak holding can get dominated. I have ~72bb, so the risk is small; plan is to fold to a large 3-bet, call small flats and use position to c-bet dry boards and control the pot on coordinated boards. Raising to 200 maximizes my long-run EV here.

I hold King-Jack of Hearts in the Big Blind, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni opened to 200 from the button. I will put in a 3-bet to 600 to build the pot and take the initiative with my premium suited broadway hand, as Minni likely has a wide range in this position.

I opened very wide from the button with 4♣6♦, but facing Flash’s 3-bet to 600 this hand is too weak to continue. The pot is 800 and it costs me 400 to call, so I need ~33% equity to break even. 4♣6♦ is heavily dominated by a standard 3‑bet range (broadways, A/Kx, pairs) and won’t have the necessary equity or playability post‑flop despite having position. 4‑betting as a bluff is also inappropriate with this holding — the min‑raise to 1000 would bloat the pot with garbage. Fold and conserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 7h5d, which is a marginal hand in a heads-up scenario. Minni, as the big blind, is sitting on a relatively short stack of 6,950 compared to my 32,900. Throughout the match, I have been fairly aggressive from the button. Given the current stack sizes and my position, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to pressure the big blind and attempt to take the pot down pre-flop. Even if called, I have position for the rest of the hand.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with QhJh — a strong, well-blocking, suited broadway hand in a heads-up spot (≈69BB effective). Against a wide SB open I want both fold equity and to take the initiative; 3-betting is the +EV line. I’ll raise to 1000 total (10BB) — big enough to fold out weaker holdings and isolate, but small enough to leave healthy postflop stacks (I’ll still have ~5950 behind). Plan: if he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll continue based on flop texture (c-bet on hearts/broadways or high-equity boards, play cautiously on coordinated runouts); if he 4-bets large I’ll typically fold QJs to avoid getting stacked by a polarized 4-bet.

I am on the button with 7h5d, which is a marginal hand. I initially raised to 300, and Minni responded with a 3-bet to 1000. Minni is sitting on a relatively short stack of 6,050. While 7h5d has some playability in heads-up, calling a significant 3-bet out of a 60BB effective stack with a weak semi-connector is generally a losing play, especially when the opponent's range for 3-betting is likely much stronger. Folding preserves my chip lead and allows me to find better spots to exploit my positional advantage.

Heads-up on the button with K♥T♥ — a strong, suited broadway with position postflop. I’ve already posted the 50 SB; calling 50 would be passive. I’ll raise to the minimum allowed (200 total) to seize the initiative, build a pot I can play in position, and deny cheap odds to draws. If Flash calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and pressure; if he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies (fold to very large 3-bets, consider calling or 4-betting vs smaller/looser 3-bets). This size preserves stack depth for postflop play while keeping the pot controllable.

I am in the Big Blind holding 10c 7h. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 200. This is a standard min-raise from a relatively short stack (71.5 BB). I need to call 100 more into a total pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While T7o is not a premium hand, it is strong enough to defend against a min-raise in a heads-up situation, especially with the favorable odds. I will call and see a flop.

I have an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with 10c 7h on a 5c 6s 8c board. Any 4 or 9 will give me a straight. I also have a backdoor club flush draw. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, checking is the standard play to see how Minni proceeds on this coordinated board. I have sufficient equity to call a bet or potentially check-raise depending on the sizing.

I raised preflop with K♥T♥ and was called; I have position and a wide raising range heads‑up. Flop 5♣-6♠-8♣ is relatively dry but with two clubs — I hold no pair or clean draw. As the preflop aggressor I should c‑bet to leverage range advantage and fold out unpaired overcards and weak holdings, but size it small to keep pots manageable against club draws. Pot is 400, so I make a ~1/3–40% pot c‑bet to apply pressure while minimizing loss if called. If I get raised big I’ll fold; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and often check/fold unless I improve.

I have an open-ended straight draw on a 5-6-8 board with my 7 and 10. Any 4 or 9 will complete my straight. Minni has made a small continuation bet of 150 into a 400 pot. I am getting approximately 3.7:1 odds to call (150 to win 550 total). Given that I have 8 outs to a straight (and some backdoor potential), calling is the most profitable play to realize my equity without over-committing to the pot out of position.

On the turn, the 10d provides me with top pair (Tens) to go along with my open-ended straight draw (any 4 or 9 completes the straight). As the player out of position who check-called the flop, the standard play is to check again to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows Minni to continue betting her range, including potential bluffs or semi-bluffs on a coordinated board. Given my improved hand strength and significant draw equity, I am very comfortable seeing a bet from her, but checking first is the best way to manage the pot and potentially induce action.

Turn gives me top pair (T) with a K kicker. Heads‑up I was the preflop aggressor and have a wide value range; Flash called my small flop c‑bet and then checked the turn, which is consistent with weakness, single‑club draws, or medium pairs. I should bet for value and protection — deny free cards to club/straight draws and extract from worse Kx/Ax/Qx and thin Tx hands. Pot is 700, so I size to about 1/2 pot (350): big enough to charge draws and get value, small enough to keep worse hands and some floats calling. If he raises large I’ll assume a made hand (sets/two‑pair) and fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river and proceed for thin value or pot control depending on the river card and his action.

On the turn, the Ten of Diamonds improves my hand to top pair (Tens) while maintaining an open-ended straight draw (any 4 or 9). Minni has bet 350 into a pot of 700. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call. Given my significant draw equity (8 outs for a straight) and current hand strength, calling is the most appropriate action. Raising here could over-inflate the pot against a potentially better hand (like a set or a straight already made) and might fold out her bluffs. Calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the river action.

On the river, the Nine of Clubs completes several possible hands. I have a Ten-high straight (6-7-8-9-10) using the Seven in my hand. However, the board is extremely coordinated and dangerous. With three clubs on the board (5c, 8c, 9c) and me holding the Tc, any two clubs in Minni's hand would make a flush. Furthermore, a higher straight is possible if Minni holds QJ (for a Queen-high straight) or J7 (for a Jack-high straight). Given that Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand (raising pre-flop and betting flop and turn), checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to evaluate her reaction to this highly wet board. If she bets, I will have to consider whether she is value betting a flush/higher straight or if she is bluffing/betting a worse hand like two-pair or a set. Checking minimizes my losses against the top of her range while keeping the pot manageable.

River 9♣ completes a very dangerous board (5♣-6♠-8♣-T♦-9♣). I hold K♥T♥ — top pair with a good kicker, but the river completes any 7-straight (7x) and makes a club-heavy board where two clubs in Flash’s hand are a made flush. Flash’s line (call flop 150, call turn 350) is exactly the line for made flushes, sets/pairs that improve, 7x semi-bluffs that became straights, and strong T/9 combinations. Betting into a pot of 1,400 now will usually (a) fold out the very hands I beat, (b) get called by many hands that beat me, and (c) expose me to a check-raise I should fold — so a thin-value bet has poor risk/reward. In heads‑up spots with a marginal but vulnerable top pair I prefer pot control and showdown. I check to realize my equity and avoid bloating the pot against hands that dominate me.

I have Ace-Deuce of hearts in the Small Blind. In heads-up play, an Ace is a very strong starting hand. Minni has a relatively short stack compared to mine (65.5 BB vs 333 BB). I will put pressure on her by raising to 300 total to take control of the pot early.

Heads-up facing a 300 open from the button with 6♥7♦ offsuit. The button's opening range is wide, and 6-7o has decent playability and equity heads-up (can make pairs, straights, and occasional backdoor draws). The pot is 400; calling 200 (total commit 300) requires about 33% equity to break even, which this hand will often have versus a wide SB range. Stacks are deep (~65bb) so implied odds support defending. A min‑3bet to 500 is possible but I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a weak, non-blocking hand—that size gives limited fold equity and creates awkward postflop spots. Plan: call, see the flop, and proceed straightforwardly (continue with pairs/draws, fold to heavy aggression on unfavorable boards, and extract value when I improve).

Flop 3c 4c 9h, I hold 6h7d — no pair but a clear straight draw: 4 fives complete 3-4-5-6-7, plus three remaining 6s and three remaining 7s gives 10 clean outs. That’s ~21% to hit on the turn and ~38% to hit by the river. Pot is 600 and stacks are deep (~63bb), and Flash opened from the button so his continuation-bet frequency on this relatively dry board is high. Leading out OOP with a weak, non‑blocking hand would bloat the pot when I’m often behind and gives poor fold equity. Checking preserves pot control and lets me realize my equity. Plan: check; if Flash fires a small-to-medium c‑bet (~≤1/2 pot) I’ll call and pursue my draws; if he overbets very large I’ll fold unless I have additional info he’s frequently over‑bluffing. For now I check to see his action and keep the pot manageable.

On a flop of 3c-4c-9h, I have Ace-high with a backdoor straight draw. Minni checked to me. While I haven't hit anything, I have a range advantage and position. A continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is standard here to apply pressure and potentially take the pot down immediately, while still leaving room to navigate if called.

Heads‑up vs the button who opened wide preflop and now c‑bet half‑pot (300). I hold 6♥7♦ on 3♣4♣9♥: I have 10 clean outs (4 fives + 3 sixes + 3 sevens) — ~21% to hit on the turn, ~38% to hit by the river. Pot odds to call 300 into a 900 pot = 300/(900+300) = 25%. Multi‑street equity + deep stacks/implied odds make a call correct here. Raising OOP with a weak, non‑blocking hand is unattractive (poor blockers, awkward postflop spots, and limited fold equity). Plan: call, realize equity; if the turn misses and Flash barrels very large I will fold; if I pair or complete the straight I will look to extract value. Therefore I will call the 300.

I missed my draw. On 3♣-4♣-9♥-T♣ my 6♥7♦ has no pair and the only realistic made-hand I can credibly represent is a 5 (straight) — a very narrow story. I called down the flop and turn to realize equity against Flash’s wide, aggressive opening range; now the river is here and I have essentially zero showdown value. Pot is 1,200 and I’m first to act. Betting as a bluff is thinly profitable against a deep-stacked, heavily-aggressive opponent who will call many thin value bets; I don’t want to risk a meaningful portion of my ~60bb stack with a marginal bluff. Best play is to check and preserve chips, folding to any meaningful river bet and only calling a very tiny probe if offered (rare). Action: check.

The turn is the Ten of Clubs, which completes some potential club flushes and straight draws (like 78 or QJ). I have Ace-high with the Ace of Spades. Minni has checked to me again. While the board has become more coordinated and favors some of her potential calling range from the flop, I still have the initiative. However, since the flush draw got home and I don't have a club, checking back is a prudent move to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable. I can look to potentially bluff or value bet the river depending on the card and her action.

River: 3♣-4♣-9h-T♣-9♣. I hold 6♥7♦ — no pair, no club, zero showdown value. I called flop/turn to realize draw equity; the river paired the board and gave many flush possibilities (4 clubs on board), so Flash can have a lot of hands that beat me and will call a bluff. I don’t block clubs or meaningful river holdings, and Flash is deep-stacked and likely to call thinly, so bluffing is thinly profitable. Pot control is correct: check and preserve my ~60bb stack, folding to any meaningful river bet.

The river brings a fourth club and pairs the board with nines. I have Ace-high with no club. Minni has checked for the third time (flop, turn, and river). While I could attempt to bluff, the presence of four clubs makes it very likely that any club in her hand will call a bet. Ace-high has a small amount of showdown value against missed straight draws like 56 or weaker Ace-high/King-high hands. Checking back is the safest way to realize my equity without risking more chips in a pot where I have very little strength.

Heads-up on the button with Kc Kh — essentially the best hand preflop except AA. I want to take the initiative, build a pot and charge any Ace-highs, broadways and suited connectors that will call a small raise. I’ll make a meaningful raise to 1,200 total (I’ve already posted 50 SB so I’m adding 1,150) — big enough to extract value and deny cheap equity to draws, but small enough to leave maneuvering room with a 6,000 stack (leaving ~4,800 behind). If Flash 3-bets I’ll reassess, but K-K is strong enough to continue vs most ranges and to call or shove depending on sizing. Total commitment shown is the amount I will have in the pot after my action.

I am in the big blind holding 8h 4c. Minni, on the button, has made a very large raise to 1200, which is approximately 1/4 of her remaining stack. Given the strength of my hand (trash), the pot odds do not justify a call against such a polarized or committed sizing from a stack of less than 50 BBs. Folding is the only sensible play.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit on the button against Minni, who is playing with a very short stack of approximately 60 big blinds (6050 chips). AQo is a premium hand in heads-up play. Given Minni's short stack and the tendency to defend or shove, I want to build the pot and apply pressure. A standard raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and sets up for a potential commitment if she shoves.

Heads-up vs Flash (button) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with 3♦5♥ offsuit. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call (pot after call ~600 → ~3:1 pot odds needing ~25% equity). Against a BTN open range, 3-5o is heavily dominated by broadways, Ax and pairs and has poor playability out of position. A light 3-bet is technically available (min to 500) but I have no strong blockers and Flash is deep and calls/plays back a lot in this game, so my fold equity would be thin — a bluff 3-bet is low EV. With 60BB effective, I want to avoid marginal OOP multi-street spots with a hand that rarely improves. Fold and wait for a better spot to exploit him.

Heads-up on the button with 7♠4♣ and ~60bb effective. This is a weak hand, but being on the button gives positional advantage and a lot of fold equity preflop. I’ll open to the recommended minimum (raise to 200) to take the initiative, pressure the BB, and avoid giving a free flop. If Flash folds I pick up the blinds; if called I’ll play the flop in position, c-bet favorable textures and keep the pot under control — fold to heavy 3-bet aggression or large turn/river pressure when I have no equity.

I am in the Big Blind with Th8h, a solid suited connector. Minni is on the Button with a relatively short stack of 5,850. She raised to 200, making it 100 more for me to call. Given my position and the strength of my hand, calling to see a flop is a standard play, especially since the pot odds are 2:1 and I have post-flop playability with suited connectors. Raising is also an option but calling preserves the ability to see how she plays on various flop textures while keeping the pot manageable against her stack.

The flop of Ac Qc Qh is very heavy and likely hits Minni's button opening range. I have total air with Th 8h. As the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I will evaluate based on her action, but I have no reason to lead out on this board.

Flop is A♣ Q♣ Q♥; I have 7♠4♣ — no pair, only a tiny backdoor club draw. However I'm the preflop raiser and my range credibly contains A‑x and Q‑x, so a single small c‑bet can often take this pot down vs a checking BB. I want to apply pressure without bloating the pot or committing many chips with near‑zero equity, so I’ll bet small (≈1/3 pot). Plan: take the pot now if he folds; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and fold to sizable raise or continued aggression unless I pick up a meaningful equity change (club or pair).

The flop comes Ac-Qc-Qh, which is a very coordinated board that heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I have Ten-high with no real equity or draws. Minni's small continuation bet of 150 is standard, but since I have completely missed this board and have zero showdown value or reasonable drawing potential, I will fold and move on to the next hand.

I have Ace-King offsuit on the button. This is a premium hand, especially in a heads-up situation against an opponent with a relatively short stack (about 60 big blinds). My goal is to maximize value and potentially get Minni to commit her remaining chips. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Heads-up vs a 300 open from the button, I have K♠7♠ in the big blind. K7s is a clear defend here: it has good heads-up equity and spade backdoors, and I only need ~33% equity to call 200 into a 400 pot, which K7s comfortably meets against a standard button opening range. I’ll call (total commit 300) rather than 3-bet—3-betting OOP with this hand is marginal (small 3-bet gives little extra fold equity and a larger 3-bet should be reserved for stronger holdings). Plan: see the flop, play aggressively when I hit (top pair, strong spade draws) and pot-control or fold to heavy aggression when I miss.

I defended preflop with K♠7♠ for heads-up equity, but on this flop (3♠ Q♦ 9♣) I have no pair and only a very remote backdoor flush (need both turn+river spades). Out of position I want to control the pot and avoid barreling with a hand that will be called too often by Qx/Kx/9x and various floats. A small c-bet is unlikely to fold enough of his continuing range and would put me in tough spots on later streets. Checking preserves fold equity on later streets if the turn is favourable (K or spade) and lets me see a free card while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll check and re-evaluate based on his action and the turn card.

I have Ace-King offsuit on a relatively dry flop of Qd-9c-3s. While I haven't connected with the board, my hand still has significant showdown value and could easily be the best hand against Minni's range. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to maintain initiative, fold out her air, and potentially get value from weaker holdings or draws. This also allows me to represent the Queen or a strong pocket pair.

I checked the flop (K♠7♠ on 3♠ Q♦ 9♣) to keep the pot manageable OOP. Flash bets 300 into 900 (1/3 pot) — my break-even equity to call is 25%. K7s has reasonable equity versus a wide c-bet range (backdoor spade potential if the turn is a spade, and decent chance to hit a King), so calling is +EV given the pot odds. A check-raise/bluff here is too risky OOP with limited fold equity and tricky turn decisions. I’ll call the 300 (add 300 now, total invested 600) to see the turn and then apply pressure only if I pair a King, pick up spade equity, or get a favorable, low-card blank; otherwise I’ll control the pot or fold to heavy aggression.

Turn (3♠ Q♦ 9♣ 2♥) with K♠7♠: I missed — no pair and the backdoor spade flush is effectively dead on the turn. I’m out of position against a player who opened and c-bet the flop, so leading out or check-raising here with K-high is a poor use of chips: I have little fold equity and I’ll be in very tough spots facing a shove or large bet. Pot control is the priority. I’ll check and keep the pot manageable. If Flash fires a small turn bet (e.g. ~300–400) I can call as a bluff-catcher because the pot odds are attractive; I’ll fold to larger sizings that put me to a difficult decision with no pair. If he checks back I go to the river and evaluate with the spare K blocker and any river card that might improve me (K or a spade) to decide whether to bet/raise or check down.

I have Ace-King high on a Queen-high board. After my continuation bet was called on the flop, the turn deuce is a blank. Minni has checked again. While I still have the best hand against some of her range, I'm likely behind her calling range from the flop (which includes Qx, 9x, or small pocket pairs). Checking behind allows me to realize my equity and see the river for free, preserving my stack and potentially winning at showdown if the board doesn't improve for her or if I hit an Ace or King on the river.

Heads-up on a dry board (3♠ Q♦ 9♣ 2♥ 6♥), pot is 1,200 and I hold K♠7♠. I have no pair and the spade backdoor is gone — this is essentially a pure bluff candidate. Flash’s line (open > c-bet flop, then check turn) contains a lot of Qx/9x, some medium pairs and missed hands that will often call a small river probe, especially given his deep stack. A small river bluff would need a fairly high fold frequency (≈25%+ for a ~300–400 bet) to be +EV; I don’t trust this spot to generate that many folds. Pot control and stack preservation are correct: betting risks chips for little fold equity and creates tough OOP decisions if he calls or raises. I’ll check and either fold to real strength or call only very small, highly suspicious bets; checking keeps the pot manageable and avoids losing value to hands that already beat me.

I have Ace-King high on a dry board of 3s-Qd-9c-2h-6h. After raising pre-flop and betting the flop, Minni called. We both checked the turn. On the river, Minni has checked again. My hand has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot, as it beats all missed straight draws (like J-10, K-10, J-8). If I bet, I am unlikely to be called by a worse hand, and I am unlikely to fold out a Queen or a Nine. By checking, I can realize my equity and potentially win the pot without risking more chips against a range that might contain a weak pair she is unwilling to fold.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 10♦-6♠: this is a marginal hand but in heads-up play we should be aggressive and open a very wide range to seize initiative and deny profitable odds to the big blind. The table standard is a 2x BB open and the minimum raise to 200 is reasonable here — it keeps the pot manageable with my ~56bb stack while still applying pressure. If Flash three-bets big I can fold or re-evaluate; if he calls I’ll see a flop in position and can use position to realize equity or apply further pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine suited, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. Minni, on the button, has raised to 200. With Minni's stack sitting at roughly 54 big blinds, I want to exert pressure and potentially take the pot down immediately or build a pot where I have significant equity. Raising to 600 (a 3-bet) serves to define the strength of my hand and forces Minni to make a decision with her remaining stack.

Facing a small 3-bet to 600 after my button open to 200 with T♠6♦. I have ~54.5BB effective. Folding is too nitty in heads-up — my range should be wide from the button — and 4-betting is the wrong play with a weak, non-blocking hand (too weak to get value and a poor bluff candidate against a deep stack). Calling 400 more (600 total) keeps the pot manageable, preserves position, and lets me exploit postflop play. Pot odds on the call are about 33% (400 to win ~1200) and SPR after a call will be ~4, so I have room to maneuver postflop. Plan: see the flop in position, c-bet/raise when I connect (top pair/strong draws) or float and apply pressure on favorable textures; fold to heavy continued aggression on A/K/Q-heavy boards where I have little equity. Call to realize equity and use positional advantage.

I am the pre-flop aggressor after 3-betting to 600. The flop of Queen-Seven-Deuce is relatively dry. While I haven't connected with the board (holding King-high), I maintain a range advantage as the 3-better, potentially representing high pocket pairs or a strong Queen. A continuation bet of 400 (one-third of the pot) is a standard play here to put pressure on Minni's calling range and attempt to take down the pot immediately.

Heads-up I opened a very wide button range preflop and Flash 3-bet to 600. On this flop (7♦ Q♥ 2♣) his small bet (400 into a 1600 pot) is tiny — it gives me ~20% pot odds to call (400 to win ≈2000). My T6o has no pair and only a weak backdoor diamond/runner potential, but I have position and can float turn cards to take the initiative or fold to real strength. A raise here would be a thin bluff (I have poor blockers to Q/A and he’s deep and aggressive), so the best line is to call, keep the pot manageable, and evaluate the turn: fold to a big turn barrel if I don’t improve or find a good bluffing card; raise/c-bet for value or as a semi-bluff if I pick up equity.

On the turn, the Jack of Spades is an overcard to most of the board besides the Queen. Having been the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop with King-high, I have met resistance from Minni's call. Since her stack is relatively short (SPR around 2.3), she is unlikely to fold a Queen or even a middle pair or a Jack to another bet. I will check here to see if she checks behind, which would give me a free river to potentially improve my King-high or re-evaluate the situation, while also controlling the size of the pot against a potentially committed opponent.

I called the flop as a positional float with T6o, but the turn (J♠) gives me nothing — no pair, no realistic straight or flush (I only have T♠ plus J♠ on board so a flush is impossible by the river). Flash checked the turn after a small flop stab; that line can show weakness/pot control or a trap, but I have very little equity and poor blockers. Betting here as a thin bluff is unattractive: the min-bet is tiny and won’t fold enough better hands, while a larger bluff needlessly risks a big chunk of my ~46.5bb stack. I’ll check to control the pot and see the river in position. Plan: if the river pairs my Ten (or brings a clear improvement) I can value-bet; if I miss and Flash bets a meaningful amount I’ll fold.

On the river, the King of Spades has improved my hand to top pair. After the turn went check-check, it is likely that Minni is holding a marginal hand like a Queen, a Jack, or a middle pocket pair. Given that I was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, this King is a very good card for my perceived range. I will place a value bet of 1,000, which is half the pot, to extract value from her weaker made hands that might call thinking I am bluffing or trying to take the pot after the turn checked through.

Board K♠ J♠ Q♥ 7♦ 2♣ on the river and I hold T♠6♦ — I have no pair, no realistic straight/flush, just a T-high that only beats pure bluffs. Flash 3-bet preflop (BB) and then c-bet the flop, checked the turn and now fires a small 1,000 into a ~3,000 pot. Calling 1,000 to win ~4,000 gives me ~20% break-even equity; given his line and range (broadways, Qx, KQ/KJ two-pair combos, possible A10 straights, and some value Jx/sets), my actual equity is well below that. A raise is nonsense with no showdown value and would commit too much of my stack. I fold to avoid a negative-EV call and preserve my stack for better spots.

I hold K9o on the button. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, especially against a short stack of 45 big blinds. I will follow my standard aggressive strategy and raise to 300 to exert pressure and take control of the pot early.

I have 3♣9♦ — a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand that will be dominated by a large portion of the button’s opening range. Flash opened to 300 and I must call 200 more (pot is 400) but I’ll be out of position post‑flop facing a very deep stack (35k) able to apply heavy pressure. Although the immediate pot odds (call 200 to win 400) look tolerable, this hand has poor postflop playability and little to no fold equity if I try to bluff later. Preserving my 4.55k stack and avoiding marginal, multi‑street decisions vs a large, aggressive stack is the higher EV line here — fold.

Heads-up on the button with T♦7♥ — this is a standard open in heads-up. I want to seize the initiative and pressure the big blind rather than limp. A 2xBB open (raise to 200 total) is the right size: it keeps the pot controllable with my ~45BB stack, steals the blinds often, and lets me play postflop in position. If Flash folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I can c-bet or float on favorable textures; if he 3-bets large I’ll fold this marginal hand. Raising maximizes EV here versus a passive call.

I am in the big blind with J9o. Minni has raised to 200 (a 2x open) from the button. With the pot at 300 and only 100 more to call, I am getting 3:1 odds. J9o is a standard defending hand in this position against a button open. I will call to see the flop and evaluate based on how it connects with my range and Minni's likely holdings.

I have flopped top pair with a nine kicker on a board that is somewhat coordinated (straight and flush draws are possible). As the big blind who called a pre-flop raise, checking to the aggressor is standard here. I want to see how Minni acts on this board before committing more chips, but I am certainly prepared to continue with top pair against a small stack.

I have top pair (T♦) with a weak 7 kicker on Ts Jh 6h and I’m in position. Flash checked, which in heads‑up often represents a wide calling/checking range (Jx, weaker tens, medium pairs, hearts/overcards and some draws). Betting for value and protection is best — checking lets many worse hands (and some bluffs) realize equity for free. I’ll make a standard half‑pot c‑bet to extract from worse tens/Jx and to deny equity to turn cards. Bet size is modest so I can call small raises and still control pot size with my ~43BB stack. Plan: bet 200 into 400; call small turn actions and re‑evaluate, fold only to a polarized large check‑raise that strongly indicates sets/straights/better two‑pair.

I have flopped top pair with a nine kicker on a board of Ts-Jh-6h. Minni, who has a relatively short stack of 4150, has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 400. This small sizing is often used as a probe or a thin value bet. Given my top pair, I am ahead of many of the hands in Minni's button range, including straight draws, flush draws, and lower pairs. Calling is the standard play here to keep the pot manageable and allow Minni to continue bluffing with weaker hands on future streets, while also protecting myself against being blown off my hand by a raise on this wet board.

On the turn, the 5c is a complete blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. I still have top pair with a nine kicker. As the player out of position who called on the flop, the standard play is to check to the aggressor again. This allows Minni to continue her bluffs or thin value bets with draws or weaker hands. Leading here would likely fold out her air and only get called by hands that beat me or strong draws. I intend to check and see how she responds.

I have T♦7♥ on T♠ J♥ 6♥ 5♣ — top pair with a weak kicker. I bet the flop (200) and was called; the 5♣ on the turn is a relative blank and Flash checked. His call on the flop indicates a wide range (Jx, weaker tens, medium pairs, some two‑heart combos/draws and floats). Checking back lets lots of worse hands and floats realize equity for free. I want to both extract value from worse pairs/Tx and charge the draws, while keeping the pot manageable with my ~41BB stack. A ~50% pot turn bet (~400 into 800) is the right balance: it prices out many equity hands, gets value from worse, and is small enough to preserve fold/call options if he responds. Plan: bet 400; if he calls re‑evaluate on the river; only fold to a large polarizing check‑raise that represents sets/straights/two‑pair.

I have top pair with a nine kicker on a board of Ts-Jh-6h-5c. Minni has bet 400 into a pot of 800 on the turn, which is a half-pot bet. This sizing can represent a range of hands from value (better Jacks like AJ, KJ, QJ) to semi-bluffs (straight draws like KQ or flush draws). Given my hand strength and the board texture, calling is the most appropriate action. It keeps the pot manageable, prevents me from over-committing against a potentially stronger hand, and allows Minni to continue bluffing on the river if she misses her draws.

The river card is the King of Clubs (Kc), which is an overcard to my pair of Jacks. The board is now Ts-Jh-6h-5c-Kc. My hand, J9, has decent showdown value, but the arrival of the King makes it less likely that I'm leading if Minni continues to bet, especially since hands like KQ or KT are in her range. By checking, I can see if she checks back with a weaker hand or a missed draw, allowing me to win at showdown without further investment. If she bets, I will have to decide if it's a bluff (missed heart draw or straight draw like Q9/89) or value based on her sizing.

Heads‑up, I have top pair (T♦) with a weak 7 kicker and I’m in position. I c‑bet the flop (200) and barreled the turn (400); both were called and the pot is 1,600. River K♣ checked to me — that card improves some Kx combos but Flash’s passive check/calls on previous streets indicate a wide, often medium‑strength calling range (Jx, Tx, underpairs, floats). Checking back guarantees showdown and gives up value from worse pairs/floats; a small value bet pressures missed hands and extracts from weaker pairs while keeping the pot manageable with my ~37.5BB stack. I’ll size small — about one‑third of the pot — to get called by worse hands but fold to a large polarized check‑raise. Bet 500 to target worse tens, 9x/8x pairs and floats; if he jams or puts strong pressure I’ll fold.

I have second pair (Jacks) on a board that just saw an overcard (King) land on the river. The final board is Ts-Jh-6h-5c-Kc. Minni has bet 500 into a pot of 1600, offering me odds of 4.2 to 1. While the King is a bad card for my hand as it connects with hands like KQ, KJ, and KT, Minni's small sizing could also be interpreted as a thin value bet with a weaker Jack, or a missed draw (like hearts or a straight draw) attempting to buy the pot cheaply. Given the excellent price I am getting, I only need to be right about 19% of the time to make this call profitable. My hand blocks some straight combinations like Q9 and 98, and has enough showdown value to warrant a call against this specific bet size.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. My opponent, Minni, is currently short-stacked with only about 31 big blinds left. In heads-up play, Jack-Seven is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the small blind to put pressure on the big blind. I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive table image and attempt to take the blinds or play the hand in position.

Facing a standard heads-up SB open to 300. I’ve already got 100 posted in the BB, so it’s 200 more to call (300 total). My hand Kc3s is marginal and vulnerable OOP, but heads-up the button’s opening range is wide so Kx has decent defensive value. I don’t want to 3-bet a weak kicker out of position (would face tough decisions and likely be dominated), and folding is too tight given pot odds and frequency I should defend the BB. Plan: call 200 more (300 total), see a flop, play cautiously OOP — check and fold to large turn/river aggression unless I improve to top pair/strong draw.

Check. I defended the BB preflop with K3s against a wide heads‑up open, but on this flop (4♦ 8♦ 6♣) I have no pair and no meaningful draw — only 3 outs to make top pair (the remaining kings) and pairing the 3 is of little value. The board has two diamonds, so Flash's continuation-bet range will include lots of diamonds and medium pairs; I’m OOP and don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal high card that’s often dominated. Checking controls the pot size, keeps weaker hands/air in the fold, and lets me fold to a sizable bet. If Flash checks back I can see a cheap turn and re-evaluate (calling a very small probe bet or folding to larger c-bets); I’ll only continue aggressively if I improve to a real made hand or pick up clear equity/plan to bluff efficiently.

As the pre-flop aggressor with overcards and backdoor straight/flush potential, I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) on this board. Minni's check indicates a lack of immediate strength, and a bet here allows me to take down the pot or define her range while maintaining my aggressive strategy.

Flash's flop stab is small (300 into a 900 pot = 1/3 pot). I have K♣3♠ — no pair, no diamond backdoor, and only 6 clean outs to pair by the river (3 kings + 3 threes ≈ 24% to improve). However the bet gives me 300/(900+300)=25% pot odds, and a small c-bet from the SB often contains a lot of air and diamond draws. Folding here would be too tight given the odds and my decent blocker to some bluffs; raising or check‑raising out of position with K3 is spewy. Plan: call the 300 to see a turn, but fold to substantial turn aggression unless I hit a king/three or pick up a clear draw or board texture that lets me continue. Call 300.

Check. I have Kc3s on 4♦ 8♦ 6♣ T♥ — no pair, no meaningful draw. Pot is 1,200 and opponent (SB) has a very wide opening/c-bet range; he made a small flop stab (1/3 pot) that I called to see a turn, but betting out of position with a weak high-card (no backdoor diamond or real equity) would just bloat the pot and put me in awkward spots. I only have 6 clean outs to pair on the river (3 kings + 3 threes) — ~6/46 ≈ 13% to improve — so I should control the pot. Plan: check and see what Flash does. If he checks back I’ll see the river cheaply and re-evaluate; if he bets a very small amount (~200 or less) I can consider a call to exploit bluffs (pot odds are close), but I will fold to medium/large turn bets (anything north of ~200–300) because my equity doesn’t justify calling bigger aggression with K3. No bluff-raise — keep the pot under control and preserve stack for better spots.

On the turn, the board is 4d-8d-6c-Th. I have Jack-high with a double gutshot straight draw (needs a 5 or a 9). Minni has checked to me again. With her stack being so short (2650 chips remaining), she is very likely to commit the rest of her chips if she has any piece of the board or a draw. While my double gutshot gives me 8 outs, betting here might trigger an all-in from her, and I don't have the pot odds to call a shove with only one card to come. By checking back, I take a free card to try and realize my equity or potentially win with a Jack-high if the river goes check-check and she was on a missed draw.

I missed — K♣3♠ on 4♦-8♦-6♣-T♥-2♥ has no pair and no meaningful blocker/draw. Pot is 1,200, opponent checked the turn after a small flop stab, so his range includes many made pairs and missed diamond draws; I’m behind to most of that range. Bluffing the river can work sometimes, but Flash has shown willingness to call down and has a very deep stack, so risking chips on a marginal bluff is unnecessary. My stack (2,650) isn’t committed and I can preserve fold equity for better spots. I’ll check and take the showdown line — fold to any river bet if he fires — rather than bluff into a wide, often-calling range.

I have Jack-high on a board of 4d-8d-6c-Th-2h. I checked the turn to realize equity for my double gutshot, which did not arrive on the river. Minni has checked to me for the third time (flop, turn, river). While my Jack-high has very little showdown value, Minni's stack is quite short (2650 chips), making a bluff risky as she might feel committed to call with any middle or bottom pair. Given the pot size and her remaining chips, I will check behind and hope to win against a missed draw like 5-3 or 7-5, or simply concede the pot to a better hand without losing more chips.

Raise to 300 (3x BB). Heads-up on the button I open very wide — 3c5s is a standard steal candidate. Raising gains significant fold equity and lets me play postflop in position. I size to 300 (above the 200 min) to deny the big blind cheap odds and discourage light calls/3-bets. With 38BB I can fold to a large 3-bet and preserve my stack; if called I'll use position to evaluate the flop and proceed accordingly.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300 chips. While the price to call is relatively small (200 more into a pot of 400), Jack-Four is a very weak starting hand that plays poorly out of position. Minni has a relatively short stack of 3,550 chips, and I prefer to wait for a stronger hand to potentially eliminate her rather than defending with such a marginal holding.

I have been dealt pocket aces, the strongest possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With only 10 hands remaining in the game and a significant stack advantage over Minni, my primary objective is to extract as much value as possible from her remaining 3,850 chips. A standard raise to 300 from the button is the most effective way to build the pot while keeping her range wide enough to call or even shove over the top if she has a medium-strength hand.

I'm in the big blind with pocket fives (5h5d). Flash opened to 300 from the SB; pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call (300 total). Pocket 5s are a medium pair that do well heads-up against a wide SB opening — I have decent equity vs a wide range and very good set-mining/implied-odds potential. Folding is too tight; a min-3bet to 500 is an option but it bloats the pot out of position and reduces my implied odds if I hit a set, and Flash has shown tendency to call or continue with a wide range. I will call to keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and see a flop where I can either extract value if I hit or proceed cautiously on scary boards. Call 200 more (300 total).

Board: 3c Td 2d. I have pocket 5s — a medium/second pair with no diamond. I'm out of position and Flash is a wide, frequently-aggressive opener. This is a relatively dry board except for two diamonds and a single overcard (T), so I want to control the pot size and realize equity rather than bloating the pot OOP with a vulnerable pair. Checking keeps worse hands and bluffs in his range so I can call a reasonable c-bet and extract value or fold to heavy aggression. Plan: check here. If Flash C-bets a small-to-moderate size I'll call to see a turn (we beat many bluffs and weaker broadways); if he barrels big (pot-sized or larger/raises), I will fold unless I pick up a clear read or improvement. If he checks back I'll reevaluate the turn for a delayed value bet or another check for pot control.

I have pocket aces on a relatively dry flop of 3c-Td-2d. Minni has checked to me, and with her stack at only 3,650 chips, my goal is to maximize value and eventually get her all-in. A standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) is appropriate here to extract value from her 'Ten' pairs, small pocket pairs, or diamond draws while maintaining a balanced betting range.

Call 300. I'm OOP with pocket 5s on 3c Td 2d — a relatively dry flop with one overcard and two diamonds. Flash is a wide, aggressive SB opener and the 300 c-bet into the 600 pot gives me ~25% break-even pot odds (300 to win 1200).
