My pocket fives have good equity vs a wide c-bet range (we beat many unpaired broadways and bluffs and have ~8.4% to hit a set by river), so folding here would be too tight. Raising OOP would bloat the pot, reduce my implied odds for set-mining, and allow him to fold many worse hands. I'll call to keep the pot manageable and realize equity. Plan: reevaluate on the turn — call small-to-medium turn barrels, fold to very large continued aggression unless I improve to a set; if he checks I may use a small delayed value bet or check back depending on the card.

Turn: 3c Td 2d 7d. I hold 5h5d. I called the flop to control the pot; on the turn a third diamond fell. With 5d in hand I now have 4 diamonds total (3 on board + my 5d) and need one diamond on the river to make a flush (9 diamond outs), plus 2 outs to hit a set — ~11 total outs (~24% to improve). Pot is 1,200 and my stack 3,350; Flash is a huge, wide/aggressive stack who c-bet the flop.

Given I'm out of position with only a small pair and the board now heavily favors hands with diamonds, I want to control the pot and keep his weaker/bluff hands in. Leading out OOP would bloat the pot and is unlikely to fold many better hands. I will check to induce actions from Flash: if he checks back I keep the pot manageable and reevaluate the river; if he bets a small-to-medium sized barrel (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot, ~≤400), I will call because my ~24% to improve + showdown value justifies calling small bets. If he fires a large pot-sized bet or overbet, I will fold — we are often behind to made flushes/ten-pairs and calling big would commit too much of my stack. 

Action: check now, plan to call small turn bets (≈≤400) and fold to large aggression; re-evaluate on river if I hit or the board runs out safely.

I hold pocket Aces with the Ace of diamonds on a board that now has three diamonds. Minni has checked to me again on the turn. Since I have the Nut Flush draw and an overpair, I have significant equity and want to continue building the pot against her range, which likely includes Tx, smaller pairs, or small diamond draws. A bet of 800 into the 1,200 pot represents roughly 66% of the pot, allowing me to extract value and set up a potential river shove for her remaining chips if the board remains favorable.

Board: 3c Td 2d 7d, my hand 5h5d. Flash (SB) bet 800 on the turn; pot shown 2000 and I must call 800. Calling 800 to win a pot that will be ~2800 requires ≈28.6% equity. I have 11 outs (9 diamonds + 2 remaining fives) ≈24% to improve on the river, which is below the break-even equity — a call is -EV. Flash is a wide/aggressive opponent but this size of turn barrel is large and consistent with made diamonds, tens or other value hands; raising OOP would bloat the pot with poor fold equity and is not profitable. I previously planned to call small-to-medium turns (≈≤400) but fold to large aggression; this bet exceeds that threshold. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with pocket 2s and 33bb effective. This is a small pocket pair that plays best by taking the initiative and getting a reasonable price to set-mine while denying free cards. A standard 3xBB open (to 300 total) gives decent fold equity, matches table sizing, and keeps the pot size manageable if called. If Flash folds I pick up the 150 pot; if he calls the pot will be ~600 and I still have ~3k behind — good implied odds for a set. Postflop plan: if I miss, play for pot control (check/call or small c-bets on favorable textures); if I hit a set I extract value; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (call modest 3-bets for set-mining if the price is right, but avoid getting stacked off without a hand).

I am in the big blind with 5s 3s. Minni, who has a relatively short stack of 30.5 BB, has raised to 3x from the button. I am getting 2:1 odds to call and see a flop with a suited gapper. Given our stack depths and the pot odds, calling is a standard play to see if I can connect with the board, while keeping the pot manageable against a short-stacked opponent who might be looking to commit her remaining chips.

On a board of Tc Ks 5h, I have hit bottom pair with my 5s. This is a marginal hand that is vulnerable to many overcards and currently trails any King or Ten in Minni's pre-flop raising range. Since I am out of position against a short-stacked opponent, the best strategy is to check to her. This allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate her aggression. If she checks behind, I can potentially see a cheap turn; if she bets, I will decide whether to call based on the bet sizing and my pot odds.

I have pocket 22 and missed the Tc Ks 5h flop. Flash checked and I have position — this is the textbook spot to control the pot and preserve my set-mining implied odds. Betting here would bloat a pot I don’t want to build with just a small pair and would make it harder to realize equity on the turn. I’ll check: if Flash leads a small/medium bet I can call to see a turn (good price to try to hit a set); if he checks back I get a free card and can continue with pot control or extract if I improve. If he puts in a large turn/river bet and I haven’t improved I’ll fold rather than getting stacked off.

On the turn, the Queen of Spades (Qs) is a very coordinated card that likely hits many parts of Minni's range that she might have checked back on the flop, such as QJ, QT, or AJ. My bottom pair (5s) has very little value now and is mostly a showdown value hand if the action stays cheap. Given Minni is short-stacked, I want to avoid building a large pot. Checking again is the best way to control the pot and potentially get to a cheap showdown or see how she reacts to the board becoming increasingly dangerous.

I have pocket deuces on Tc Ks 5h Qs and have missed — only two outs (~4%) to make a set on the river. Both players checked flop and Flash checked the turn; with position and a small pair my priority is pot control and preserving my stack for a legitimate hit. Betting the turn would bloat the pot with a very weak holding, get called by better hands, and reduce the implied odds of set-mining. I’ll check to see a free/cheap river; if I hit a deuce I can extract value, and if Flash barrels the river I’ll fold to large bets but may call small ones with the right pot odds.

On the river, the board is 10c Ks 5h Qs Kd. I hold 3s 5s, which gives me two pair: Kings and Fives (Ks Kd 5s 5h with a Queen kicker from the board). This is a marginal showdown hand. Since Minni checked back both the flop and the turn, it is unlikely she holds a King or a Queen, although she could easily have a Ten for a better two pair (Kings and Tens). My hand beats all 'air' and Ace-high hands. Betting doesn't achieve much because a better hand (like a Ten or Queen) will call, and a worse hand will fold. By checking, I can reach showdown cheaply and potentially win against her missed draws or low-card hands that didn't connect with this board. If she bets after I check, I will have to evaluate the size of the bet, but checking is the best move to control the pot size and maximize my expected value.

Board: Tc Ks 5h Qs Kd. I hold 2h2s — missed. Both streets were checked so opponent’s checked range is wide: missed broadways, Qx/Tx, small pairs, and also Kx slowplays that now convert to trips on the river. With the river pairing a King, any Kx in his range has turned into a very strong hand and will crush me. My hand has virtually no value at showdown except vs pure bluffs. To bluff here I need a very high fold-frequency (e.g. >33% if I bet ~300) and risk a costly check-raise or a call from many hands that beat me. Given the large stack disparity and opponent’s mixed/passive lines this spot favors pot control and preserving my 3k stack for real equity (set-mining or better holdings). I’ll check and concede the pot rather than bluff into a range that includes trips and many pairs.

I am currently the chip leader with a substantial advantage over Minni, who is playing with a relatively short stack of less than 30 big blinds. Queen-Four offsuit is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up situation from the small blind. Given the current stage of the game (Hand 142 of 150), my primary goal is to minimize unnecessary risk and protect my stack. Raising with such a weak hand invites a potential shove from a short-stacked opponent, and calling just to see a flop out of position is generally sub-optimal with this holding. Therefore, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

5♦2♣ is one of the worst holdings. Heads‑up from the button/SB you can open wide, but this hand is so weak that I don't want to inflate the pot facing a deep, aggressive big blind — a min‑raise to 200 will often be called and can get me in trouble postflop or face 3‑bets. Calling 50 more (100 total committed including my posted SB) is cheap: the pot will be ~200 after my call, so I only need ~25% equity to break even. Against Flash's likely calling/defending range I have roughly that equity and I keep positional advantage. Plan: call to see a flop, fold to substantial aggression unless I pair a 5 or 2 or pick up strong backdoor equity, otherwise use position to control pot size or take the pot on favorable runouts.

Minni has limped in from the small blind with a relatively short stack of 30 BBs. With 7d 4d in the big blind, I have a marginal hand that plays well enough in a limped pot but is not strong enough to warrant a raise for value or a bluff against a short-stacked opponent who might be trapping or willing to commit. I will check and take the free flop to see if I can improve my hand without increasing the risk at this stage of the game.

I have middle pair (Sevens) on an Ace-high board. In a limped pot, there's no need to inflate the pot immediately. Checking is the most standard play here to see how Minni reacts and to control the size of the pot, especially since I have some showdown value but am vulnerable to any Ace or better pairs.

Board A♠-7♥-3♥; I hold 5♦2♣ — no pair, no heart, almost no equity (only six outs to a single pair and no reliable backdoor). Flash checked, which can be a wide range in the big blind. Heads‑up and on the button I should c‑bet fairly often as a bluff to pick up pots, but keep it small because I’m behind to any Ace or pair. Min‑bet 100 (table min) applies pressure, buys the pot often, and keeps my risk limited. If Flash raises substantially I’ll fold (I have no hand to continue with); if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and only continue with real improvement or a clear plan to barrel with fold equity.

I have middle pair (sevens) on an Ace-high board in a limped pot. Minni's bet of 100 into a 200 pot is a standard continuation bet that could represent an Ace, a heart draw, a lower pair, or a pure bluff. Folding middle pair to a small bet in a limped pot would be too exploitable, and there's no reason to raise and potentially isolate myself against a better hand like an Ace. Calling allows me to keep the pot controlled and see a turn card.

On the turn, the board is 7h As 3h 5c. I have middle pair (Sevens) with a weak kicker. Minni has checked. Given the board texture and the fact that it was a limped pot, my Sevens still have significant showdown value. However, checking behind is the best play here to control the pot size and avoid a check-raise from a short stack. If I bet, I mostly get called by better hands (Aces) or draws, and I don't want to build a pot with a hand that is likely ahead but vulnerable or behind and drawing thin. Checking keeps the pot small and allows me to reach the river cheaply.

I c‑bet the flop as a bluff and was called. The turn (5♣) paired my 5♦ — a weak but real made hand. This is a spot to extract thin value and charge any backdoor draws while keeping the size small because I’m still behind any Ace/7/3 or bigger pair. A half‑pot bet (200 into a 400 pot) accomplishes that: it gets called by worse Kx/Qx/other floats and charges hearts/draws, but doesn’t commit me if Flash shows aggression. Plan: bet 200; fold to a sizable check‑raise, and if called re‑evaluate on the river.

On the turn, the board is 7h As 3h 5c. I have middle pair (Sevens) with a weak kicker. Minni has bet 200 into a 400 pot after my check. In a limped pot, Minni's small sizing across two streets could represent a weak Ace, a heart draw, or a mid-pair like the one I hold. Folding middle pair for this price would be overly conservative, as I am getting 3:1 odds on a call. Raising is not advisable because it would likely only be called by better hands and would bloat the pot against a short stack. I will call to see the river and evaluate.

On the river, the board pairs the Ace, giving me two pair: Aces and Sevens. In this heads-up pot, I am first to act. Minni has shown aggression on the flop and turn with half-pot bets after limping pre-flop. Checking is the most prudent move here. It allows me to see if Minni will continue her aggression with a missed draw (like hearts) or check behind with a hand that has showdown value but is weaker than my Sevens. If she bets, I'll have to evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking to the aggressor is the standard play.

Board: 7♥ A♠ 3♥ 5♣ A♦. My hand: 5♦2♣ → two pair, Aces and Fives. Opp called my flop and turn bets but checked the river. The river Ace is dangerous — any A‑x in Flash’s range now makes trips (or better), and pocket 5s (or 55) would make a full house. At the same time a lot of hands I beat (7x, 3x, Kx/Qx floats, missed hearts, single‑5 combos that tie) will either check behind or call a small value bet.

I don’t want to give up thin value, but I also don’t want to inflate the pot and get check‑raised by a trip Ace. Plan: make a modest value bet to charge worse pairs and floats, but keep it small enough that I can fold to a sizable check‑raise. Pot is 800 — I’ll bet ~300 to extract calls from weaker hands while limiting downside. If Flash check‑raises big or jams, I’ll fold (he’s representing trips/full house); if he just calls, I get paid by worse.

On the river, the board pairs the Ace, giving me two pair: Aces and Sevens with a 5 kicker. Minni has bet 300 into a 1100 pot, which is a small sizing consistent with her previous bets on the flop and turn. While it is possible Minni has an Ace for trips, her line of limping pre-flop and then firing three small barrels could also represent a weaker pair (like a 3 or 5), a better 7, or a missed heart draw. I am getting roughly 3.7-to-1 odds on a call (300 to win 1100 + 300). Given the pot odds and the fact that I have a reasonably strong two-pair hand in a limped pot, folding is too tight. I will call to see the showdown.

I am on the button with T9o. Minni has a relatively short stack of 2300. Given the late stage of the game (Hand 144/150) and the pot odds, I will raise to put pressure on the short stack or take the pot down immediately. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate.

Heads-up vs BTN open to 300. I have K♠3♣ and 2,300 (≈23BB). Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (300 total committed). The button's 3x open in heads-up is a wide range, so K3o has respectable equity and is worth seeing a flop. A min 3-bet to 500 would have very little fold equity; a larger 3-bet would commit too much with a weak kicker. I prefer to keep the pot controllable and realize equity postflop: call to 300 total, then proceed cautiously — continue when I hit a King or good draw, fold or pot-control against strong aggression on unfavorable boards.

I have K♠3♣ and the flop is 3♦ 8♦ 9♣ — I’ve paired my 3 for a small but real hand heads-up. Button’s preflop open is wide, so a large portion of his range are unpaired overcards and draws; my pair is often ahead but vulnerable to diamond draws and any 8s/9s. I’m out of position so I want to charge draws and get value from worse hands while keeping the pot controllable. A half‑pot c-bet (~300 into 600) achieves that: denies correct odds to many diamond draws, gets called by Kx/Qx and other overcards, and lets me fold to big aggression without committing a large portion of my short stack. If he calls I’ll evaluate the turn; if he raises big I can release and preserve chips.

On the flop of 3d-8d-9c, I have top pair (nines) with a ten kicker. Minni, who started the hand with only 2100 chips, has led out for 300 into a 600 pot. This 'donk lead' from a short stack often represents a draw or a marginal pair. Given my top pair and the fact that Minni has only 1800 chips remaining, I have a very strong hand relative to her stack size. I will raise to 600 to put more pressure on her draws and potentially commit her to the pot, while also protecting my hand against various diamond draws and straight draws.

Heads‑up, I have K♠3♣ and flopped a small pair on 3♦‑8♦‑9♣. I c‑bet 300 to deny diamond draws and get value from worse Kx/Qx type hands; Flash’s reraise to 600 can be diamonds, 8x/9x/top pairs, sets or a semi‑bluff. The pot is 1500 and it costs me 300 to continue (~5:1 pot odds → ~16.7% equity needed), so my pair has enough equity versus his continuing range to call. I’m out of position and only 18BB effective, so I don’t want to inflate the pot or commit with a marginal hand by raising. I’ll call the 300 more (600 total committed) to keep the pot controllable, see the turn, and fold to clear strength (diamond turn or big shove) while extracting value from worse hands or calling small turn bets. If I hit trips or villain gives a weak turn check‑back, I’ll extract; otherwise I’ll preserve my stack.

Heads‑up out of position with K♠3♣ and a small pair (trip blocker but only a pair of 3s) on 3♦‑8♦‑9♣‑4♠. Flash raised the flop and then checked the turn — that line is consistent with missed diamond semi‑bluffs, medium strength hands checking for pot control, or an occasional slow‑played strong hand. The pot (1,800) is already large relative to my 1,500 stack, so betting risks turning a marginal hand into a bloated, commit‑or‑fold situation out of position where I’m likely behind to 8x/9x/top pairs and could face a check‑raise shove. I’ll check and see the river for free; plan is to call small river bets for thin value or fold to large aggression. Checking preserves stack flexibility and keeps marginal hands in my calling range without overcommitting.

On the turn 4s, the board remains relatively safe for my top pair of nines. Minni has checked, and with her stack now reduced to 1,500 chips while the pot is 1,800, the stack-to-pot ratio is below 1. This is a clear situation to commit the rest of the chips. I want to maximize value from weaker nines, eights, or pocket pairs, and also protect my hand against the diamond flush draw and various straight draws (76, T7). By betting her remaining 1,500, I effectively put her all-in, forcing her to decide whether to realize her equity or fold, while I likely hold the superior hand.

I’m facing a 1,500 bet into a 1,800 pot (pot 3,300) with K♠3♣ and a 1,500 stack — calling is effectively an all‑in and requires ≈31.3% equity to break even. Flash’s line (raise the flop after my c‑bet, then a large turn bet) is weighted toward strong value (8x/9x, sets) and two‑card diamond semi‑bluffs; my small pair of threes is behind most of that range and vulnerable to the flush/overcard combos that continue. Shoving as a bluff has poor fold equity versus a deep, aggressive opponent and would be spewy if called. With only ~15BB I’ll preserve my stack for a better spot rather than risk it here — fold.

Heads-up on the button with Ac As. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50 to see a flop, but I have a 14.5BB effective stack (1450) against a very deep BB. With premium pair heads-up and so few hands left in the session, the highest EV line is to commit now: a small raise leaves too much SPR and invites tricky postflop decisions or a 3-bet; just calling is overly passive and loses value. Shoving (all-in for my 1450) maximizes value and fold equity — Flash will call light enough that I get paid by worse pairs and broadways, and if he folds I pick up the 150 pot. Total all-in also simplifies decisions and preserves the value of AA vs his calling range. Action: shove (raise to 1450 total).

In Hand #145, Minni is on the Button/Small Blind and has raised to 1450, leaving her with only 50 chips. This is effectively an all-in move given her tiny remaining stack. I am in the Big Blind with 8d 6d. To call, I need to put in 1350 more into a pot that will be 2900 after my call. This gives me excellent pot odds, needing roughly 46% equity to break even. 8d 6d is a decent suited connector and performs well enough against a wide range of hands that a short-stacked opponent would shove. I will call to see the flop and likely commit the final 50 chips on any board.

Minni has only 50 chips left in her stack after the pre-flop action. I have flopped middle pair with my six. Since the 'To Call' amount is 0, it indicates that Minni has checked. I will bet her remaining 50 chips to put her all-in, as I have a made hand and the pot odds are overwhelming to commit the final chips regardless of her holding.

I shoved most of my stack preflop (1450) and have Ac As — I’m still far ahead on the Kd 6h 7s flop with a huge overpair. Flash’s 50 into a ~2950 pot is a tiny probe/blocking bet; folding would be surrendering enormous equity for virtually no reason. I only need to put in 50 (effectively my remaining stack) for extremely good pot odds and to get value from Kx, smaller pairs and bluffs. Raising is irrelevant since I only have 50 behind — calling is effectively committing the rest and is the clear +EV play to realize equity and see showdown.
